abhijit-banerjee-economics.l: Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL)
abhijit-banerjee-economics.s: J-PAL is a global development-economics research center that uses randomized controlled trials to evaluate which anti-poverty programs actually work. Banerjee's group designs and runs field experiments across health, education, credit, and governance in dozens of countries. Methodologically the lab is built around RCTs, regression-based program evaluation, and causal inference from large field datasets.
abhijit-banerjee-economics.notes: Economics/development — only loose fit to SciBowl taxonomy; tagged under stats/causal inference. Verified scholar id HLpqZooAAAAJ via search.

adam-c-martin-biology.s: The Martin Lab studies tissue morphogenesis — how groups of cells generate the mechanical forces that fold and shape embryonic tissue. Their main system is gastrulation in the fruit fly Drosophila, where they track how actomyosin contraction, cell adhesion, and cell signaling drive epithelial folding. The group relies heavily on live confocal microscopy, quantitative image analysis, and genetic perturbations to connect molecular activity to tissue-scale shape change.
adam-c-martin-biology.notes: Upgraded scholar from search-query URL to canonical user=sILsSIsAAAAJ profile (verified via search).

adam-chlipala-eecs.s: Chlipala's group at CSAIL builds machine-checked correctness proofs for software and hardware systems, specializing in formal verification with the Coq proof assistant. Notable projects include Fiat Cryptography (verified cryptographic primitives shipped in Chrome and BoringSSL) and FSCQ (a formally verified file system). Methodologically the group develops new programming-language abstractions, type systems, and proof-engineering techniques to make full-system verification tractable.
adam-chlipala-eecs.notes: Scholar Imh0w3AAAAAJ verified via search results.

adam-p-willard-chemistry.s: The Willard Group is a theoretical/computational physical chemistry lab studying how nanoscale disorder controls emergent behavior in liquids, biological interfaces, and energy materials. Active topics include electron dynamics in conjugated/organic semiconductors, water structure around proteins and electrodes, and electrochemistry at disordered interfaces. The group's methods are molecular dynamics simulation, statistical mechanics, coarse-grained modeling, and machine-learning analysis of simulation trajectories.

adam-schlosser-eaps.l: MIT Center for Sustainability Science and Strategy (CS3)
adam-schlosser-eaps.s: Schlosser is Deputy Director of CS3 (formerly the MIT Joint Program on Global Change), where he leads research on hydro-climate, water resources, and the risk of weather extremes under climate change. The group couples global Earth-system models with socioeconomic and energy-system models to produce probabilistic projections of droughts, floods, and renewable-energy variability. Methodologically the work is built around large Monte Carlo ensembles of coupled climate–economy simulations and statistical downscaling against observational data.
adam-schlosser-eaps.notes: Scholar e2eMfdoAAAAJ verified via search results. Lab name updated from 'Joint Program' to current CS3 name.

ahmed-f-ghoniem-meche.s: Ghoniem's RGD lab develops the science and engineering of low-carbon energy conversion: clean combustion, CO2 capture, oxy-fuel and chemical-looping systems, hydrogen production, and hybridization of fossil fuels with concentrated solar thermal energy. The group works on multiscale and multiphase reacting flows, building both reduced thermochemical models and high-resolution turbulence simulations. Methods span computational fluid dynamics, large-eddy simulation, detailed kinetic modeling, and bench-scale combustion experiments.

akintunde-i-akinwande-eecs.s: Akinwande's group at MIT's Microsystems Technology Laboratories designs and fabricates micro- and nano-scale electronic devices, with emphasis on vacuum nanoelectronics (cold-cathode field emitters), MEMS sensors and actuators, and electronics on flexible substrates. Recent work covers field-emission electron sources, ion sources for portable mass spectrometry, and thin-film transistor backplanes for displays. The group's methods are semiconductor cleanroom processing, device physics modeling, and electrical characterization of nanoscale structures.

alan-d-grossman-biology.s: The Grossman lab studies the molecular biology of bacteria, using Bacillus subtilis as the main model. Two major lines of work are the lifecycle and regulation of integrative and conjugative elements (ICEs) — mobile DNA elements that are a major route for horizontal spread of antibiotic-resistance genes — and the control of chromosomal DNA replication initiation by the conserved replication initiator/transcription factor DnaA. Methods include bacterial genetics, biochemistry of DNA-binding proteins, fluorescence microscopy of single cells, and high-throughput sequencing.

alan-edelman-math.s: Edelman is co-creator of the Julia programming language and runs the JuliaLab in CSAIL, which builds the language's compiler, scientific-computing libraries, and tools for differentiable and scientific machine learning. Research interests include numerical linear algebra, random matrix theory (eigenvalue distributions, condition numbers), and high-performance parallel computing. Methodologically the group combines pure-math analysis (Lie theory, geometry of matrix manifolds) with compiler engineering and large-scale numerical experimentation.

alan-jasanoff-bioe.s: The Jasanoff lab develops 'molecular fMRI' — a new generation of magnetic-resonance imaging tools that read out brain activity at the level of specific neurochemicals rather than just blood flow. The group engineers MRI contrast agents and protein/nanoparticle sensors that respond to neurotransmitters (dopamine, calcium, serotonin) and uses them to image neural circuits in living rodent brains. Methods span protein engineering, nanoparticle chemistry, in vivo MRI, optogenetics, and quantitative behavioral neuroscience.

alex-k-shalek-chemistry.s: The Shalek lab builds and applies single-cell genomic technologies to dissect how individual immune, epithelial, and tumor cells behave inside complex tissues. They are best known for inventing Seq-Well, a portable low-cost platform for single-cell RNA sequencing that can be deployed in low-resource clinical settings around the world. Methods include droplet- and well-based scRNA-seq, multimodal omics (CITE-seq, spatial transcriptomics), microfluidics, and large-scale computational analysis of cell-state landscapes.

alexander-h-slocum-meche.s: PERG develops mechanical systems whose performance is set by deterministic design — exact-constraint kinematics, precision bearings, flexures, and metrology — rather than by trial-and-error tuning. The group's projects span machine tools, semiconductor lithography stages, medical devices, and renewable-energy hardware (wave, wind, and ocean-thermal systems). Methods are first-principles mechanics, error-budget analysis, finite-element modeling, and extensive prototyping in MIT's machine shops.
alexander-h-slocum-meche.notes: Duplicate of alex-slocum-meche; same enrichment applied to both. See data/duplicates.txt.

alex-slocum-meche.s: PERG develops mechanical systems whose performance is set by deterministic design — exact-constraint kinematics, precision bearings, flexures, and metrology — rather than by trial-and-error tuning. The group's projects span machine tools, semiconductor lithography stages, medical devices, and renewable-energy hardware (wave, wind, and ocean-thermal systems). Methods are first-principles mechanics, error-budget analysis, finite-element modeling, and extensive prototyping in MIT's machine shops.
alex-slocum-meche.notes: Duplicate of alexander-h-slocum-meche; same enrichment applied to both. See data/duplicates.txt.

alexander-logunov-math.s: Logunov is a pure mathematician working in harmonic analysis, potential theory, and the geometry of nodal sets — the zero sets of solutions of elliptic partial differential equations such as Laplace eigenfunctions on manifolds. His Clay-Award-winning work (with Eugenia Malinnikova) introduced new geometric-combinatorial methods that resolved long-standing conjectures of Yau and Nadirashvili on the size of nodal sets. The work is theoretical: PDE estimates, complex analysis, and combinatorial/geometric arguments rather than computation or experiment.
alexander-logunov-math.notes: Pure math (harmonic analysis / nodal geometry); no group website. Tags are loose connections (eigenfunctions of Schrödinger-like operators).

alexander-m-klibanov-chemistry.s: Klibanov's lab pioneered non-aqueous enzymology — the discovery that enzymes can be made to function as efficient catalysts in nearly anhydrous organic solvents, with dramatically altered thermostability and stereoselectivity compared to the same enzymes in water. Current work uses these solvent-engineered biocatalysts for asymmetric organic synthesis, develops antimicrobial polymeric coatings, and studies the stabilization and delivery of protein pharmaceuticals. The group's methods combine enzymology, organic chemistry, polymer chemistry, and physical biochemistry.

alexei-borodin-math.s: Borodin works at the interface of probability, representation theory, combinatorics, and statistical physics — an area he helped found and is now called 'integrable probability'. He studies exactly-solvable models of random growth and interacting particle systems (KPZ universality, six-vertex models, Macdonald processes, last-passage percolation) where symmetry and algebra produce closed-form limiting distributions. The work is theoretical: representation-theoretic identities, asymptotic analysis, and random matrix theory rather than simulation.

ali-jadbabaie-cee.s: Jadbabaie's group studies collective behavior and decision-making in large interconnected systems — opinion dynamics in social networks, distributed control of multi-agent systems, learning over networks, and the structure of complex urban and infrastructure systems. The toolbox is mathematical: control theory, network science, dynamical systems, convex optimization, graph theory, and statistical learning. Recent work combines these with machine learning for forecasting epidemics, traffic, and social-media dynamics.

alison-e-ringel-biology.s: The Ringel lab is a T-cell immunology group studying how immune cells survive and adapt to hostile environments such as the inside of a tumor. Work focuses on the metabolic programs of CD8+ T cells under nutrient deprivation, oxidative stress, and immunosuppressive cytokines, looking for vulnerabilities that could be exploited therapeutically. Methods include in vivo mouse tumor models, primary T-cell culture, single-cell RNA-seq, metabolomics, and CRISPR screens.

alison-e-wendlandt-chemistry.s: The Wendlandt group develops new selective catalytic reactions for organic synthesis, especially methods that can isomerize alkenes or epimerize sugars site-selectively to access otherwise hard-to-make stereoisomers. The hallmark approach uses dual catalysis — pairing a photoredox catalyst with a hydrogen-atom-transfer or transition-metal catalyst — to break and re-form bonds at remote positions on a molecule. Work spans organometallic synthesis, physical organic chemistry, mechanism studies, and total synthesis of carbohydrates and natural products.

amy-e-keating-biology.s: The Keating lab studies how proteins recognize and bind one another with sequence-encoded specificity, focusing on α-helical coiled coils and the Bcl-2 family of apoptosis regulators. They combine bioinformatics, structural modeling, and computational design with high-throughput experimental measurement of binding affinities, then engineer synthetic peptides that selectively disrupt disease-relevant protein–protein interactions. Methods include deep mutational scanning, yeast surface display, X-ray crystallography, molecular dynamics, and machine-learning-based protein design.

anders-s-hansen-bioe.s: The Hansen lab studies how the three-dimensional folding of the genome controls gene expression, focusing on chromatin loops and enhancer–promoter contacts. They develop new super-resolution and single-molecule imaging tools that track individual proteins, DNA loops, and nascent transcripts inside living human cells with millisecond-and-nanometer resolution. Methods include live-cell single-particle tracking, lattice-light-sheet microscopy, Micro-C and Hi-C, and biophysical modeling of polymer dynamics.

andrew-j-whittle-cee.l: Whittle Research Group (Geotechnical Engineering & Underground Infrastructure)
andrew-j-whittle-cee.s: Whittle's group works on geotechnical engineering — predicting how soils and underground structures deform and fail. The lab develops constitutive models of clays and sands within elasto-plasticity, simulates coupled thermo-hydro-mechanical soil behavior, and applies these models to deepwater oil-platform foundations and major urban tunneling projects (Boston's Big Dig, Tren Urbano in San Juan). Methods are continuum mechanics, finite-element modeling, large-scale lab and field experiments on soils, and instrumentation of real construction sites.

andrew-r-babbin-eaps.s: The Babbin lab is a marine biogeochemistry group studying how microbes in low-oxygen ocean zones control the global nitrogen cycle and the production of greenhouse gases like nitrous oxide. They combine seagoing oceanographic cruises with high-precision isotope-tracer incubation experiments to measure microbial denitrification, anammox, and nitrification rates in situ. Lab work integrates stable-isotope mass spectrometry, gene-based microbial community analysis, and numerical biogeochemical models that connect microscale microbial activity to global climate.

andrew-v-sutherland-math.s: Sutherland is a senior research scientist in the MIT math department who works on the computational side of number theory and arithmetic geometry. He is one of the principal builders of the L-functions and Modular Forms Database (LMFDB) and has led large computations including the Polymath bounded-gaps-between-primes project and the resolution of Mordell's question on writing 3 as a sum of three cubes. Methods are explicit algorithmic number theory, large-scale distributed computing on elliptic curves and modular forms, and classification of Sato–Tate distributions.
andrew-v-sutherland-math.notes: Pure / computational number theory; cryptography tag is the loose touchpoint (elliptic curves used in crypto).

andrew-w-lo-eecs.s: Lo runs the MIT Laboratory for Financial Engineering (out of Sloan, but cross-appointed in EECS/CSAIL), which studies financial markets through the lens of statistics, evolutionary biology, and engineering. Recent work covers the Adaptive Markets Hypothesis, financial engineering of biomedical-research funding (mega-funds for cancer drug development), and machine-learning models of investor behavior and systemic risk. Methods are time-series econometrics, computational finance, behavioral experiments, and ML on large financial datasets.
andrew-w-lo-eecs.notes: Listed under EECS in labs.json but he's primarily a Sloan finance professor; cross-appointed in CSAIL/EECS.

anette-hosoi-math.s: Peko Hosoi directs the Hatsopoulos Microfluids Laboratory, which works at the intersection of fluid mechanics, biomechanics, and bio-inspired design. Past projects include 'RoboSnails' that mimic gastropod locomotion on shear-thinning mucus, the mechanics of swimming microorganisms, sports analytics, and the design of soft robots. Methods are low-Reynolds-number fluid dynamics, asymptotic analysis, numerical PDE simulation, and tabletop experiments with non-Newtonian fluids.

angela-m-belcher-bioe.s: The Belcher lab uses directed evolution of viruses (especially the M13 bacteriophage) to template inorganic nanomaterials for energy and medical applications. They have engineered viruses that bind and pattern metal and semiconductor surfaces, and used them to assemble lithium-ion battery electrodes, photocatalysts for water splitting, and nanoprobes that detect ovarian tumors smaller than a millimeter. Methods combine phage display, materials chemistry, electrochemistry, fluorescence imaging, and in vivo cancer models.

angela-n-koehler-bioe.s: The Koehler lab is a chemical-biology group that builds small-molecule probes against historically 'undruggable' targets — transcription factors, RNA-binding proteins, and chromatin-modifying enzymes — mostly in a cancer context. Their signature platform is small-molecule microarray screening, where thousands of compounds are immobilized and scanned for binders against a labeled protein of interest. Methods span synthetic and combinatorial chemistry, biochemical and cellular assays, proteomics, and translation of hits into imaging agents and therapeutic leads.

ankur-jain-biology.s: The Jain lab studies how biomolecules self-organize into membraneless compartments inside cells, especially RNA-protein granules formed by liquid–liquid phase separation. They discovered that certain repeat-expansion RNAs (the same ones that cause ALS, Huntington's, and other neurodegenerative diseases) can clump into gel-like aggregates, and the lab now searches for cellular safeguards that prevent this and for drugs that can dissolve the aggregates. Methods include single-molecule fluorescence imaging, in vitro reconstitution of phase-separated droplets, RNA biochemistry, and small-molecule screens.

ankur-moitra-eecs.s: Moitra is a theoretical computer scientist whose group designs machine-learning algorithms with provable guarantees, even on hard problems like learning Gaussian mixture models, robust statistics under adversarial corruptions, sparse coding, tensor decomposition, topic models, and quantum learning. The work bridges classical theory of computation (LP/SDP relaxations, the method of moments, information theory) with modern statistical learning. Output is mostly theorems and algorithms rather than experiments.

ann-m-graybiel-bcs.s: The Graybiel lab studies the basal ganglia — the deep forebrain circuits that go wrong in Parkinson's, Huntington's, OCD, addiction, and depression, but also drive normal habit learning and motivation. They map striatal striosome and matrix circuits, follow how dopamine and serotonin signals shape decision-making, and test therapeutic strategies including chronic drug-delivery implants. Methods include in vivo electrophysiology, two-photon and fiber-photometry imaging, optogenetics, mouse genetics, and behavioral neuroscience.

anna-frebel-physics.s: Frebel's group does 'stellar archaeology' — finding and chemically dissecting the oldest, most metal-poor stars in the Milky Way and its dwarf-galaxy satellites to reconstruct what the universe looked like in its first few hundred million years. By measuring the elemental abundances printed in these fossil stars, the group constrains the masses of the very first generation of stars, the yields of early supernovae, and the r-process events (like neutron-star mergers) that created the heaviest elements. Methods are high-resolution optical spectroscopy on large telescopes (Magellan, VLT) and detailed stellar-atmosphere modeling.

annachristina-eilers-physics.s: Eilers leads the Cosmic Dawn Group, an observational astrophysics lab studying the first galaxies, quasars, and supermassive black holes that formed within the first billion years after the Big Bang. The group develops new techniques to measure how long quasars have been shining (using the size of their ionized 'proximity zones') and recently used JWST to find ancient quasars sitting in surprisingly empty regions of space — challenging models of how supermassive black holes grow. Methods include rest-frame UV/optical spectroscopy with Magellan, VLT, and JWST, and statistical analysis of large quasar surveys.

antoine-allanore-dmse.s: The Allanore group works on sustainable extractive metallurgy — replacing the carbon-intensive smelting of metals with cleaner electrochemical processes. Their flagship technique is molten oxide electrolysis (MOE), which directly reduces iron, ferroalloys, and other metals from their oxides in a high-temperature electrolytic cell, eliminating the CO2 emissions of traditional steelmaking; the work spun out as Boston Metal. Methods are high-temperature electrochemistry, molten-salt thermodynamics, materials characterization of refractory anodes, and process engineering.

aram-w-harrow-physics.s: Harrow is a theorist at MIT's Center for Theoretical Physics working on quantum information and computation — particularly the question of which problems quantum computers can solve exponentially faster than classical ones. He is best known as a co-author of the HHL algorithm for solving linear systems on a quantum computer, and his group works on quantum algorithms, quantum complexity theory, entanglement theory, and the connections between quantum information and quantum gravity (holography, AdS/CFT). The work is purely theoretical: complexity-theoretic proofs and quantum-algorithmic constructions.

areg-danagoulian-nse.l: Laboratory for Nuclear Security and Policy
areg-danagoulian-nse.s: Danagoulian's lab applies experimental nuclear physics to nuclear-security problems: verifying nuclear-warhead dismantlement, screening shipping containers for smuggled fissile material, and developing physical cryptographic protocols that confirm a treaty-relevant warhead is genuine without revealing classified design information. The signature technique is neutron resonance transmission analysis (NRTA), which uses a neutron beam to fingerprint isotopes and geometry inside a sealed object. Methods include accelerator-based neutron and gamma sources, scintillator and semiconductor detectors, Monte Carlo radiation-transport simulation, and treaty/policy analysis.

arlene-m-fiore-eaps.l: TEAMPACCC (Trends in Earth's Atmospheric Makeup: Pollution-Air-Chemistry-Climate Connections)
arlene-m-fiore-eaps.s: Fiore's TEAMPACCC group studies how natural and human emissions shape atmospheric composition, air quality, and climate, and how those connections feed back on each other. The group runs global chemistry-transport and chemistry-climate models that produce hundreds of terabytes of output, then compares them with surface, aircraft, and satellite observations to track ozone, methane, aerosols, and short-lived climate forcers. Their four pillars are air pollution, chemistry-climate connections, trends and variability of atmospheric constituents, and biosphere-atmosphere exchange.

arnaud-costinot-economics.s: Costinot is an international-trade economist who works on the theory and quantitative empirics of comparative advantage, the gains from trade, and trade policy. His work has helped revive interest in classical Ricardian and Heckscher-Ohlin models by showing how they can be made tractable for modern data and used to estimate the welfare effects of tariffs, technology shocks, and globalization. Methods are mathematical economic theory and structural quantitative trade modeling on bilateral-trade datasets.
arnaud-costinot-economics.notes: Pure economist (international trade); no group lab. Tags are loose connections via game theory / optimization.

arup-k-chakraborty-chemistry.s: Chakraborty applies the tools of statistical physics and theoretical chemistry to immunology and virology — building models of how T cells, B cells, and antibody repertoires recognize pathogens, why HIV is so hard to vaccinate against, and how to design better immunogens. The group's HIV-vaccine immunogen designs are now in preclinical trials. Methods include statistical mechanics of disordered systems, molecular dynamics simulations, large-scale sequence analysis of viral and antibody repertoires, and population-level evolutionary modeling.

asegun-henry-meche.s: The ASE group studies how heat moves at the atomic scale (phonon transport, interface conductance, radiative transfer) and uses that physics to engineer high-temperature energy systems for deep decarbonization. Flagship projects include thermal-energy grid storage in molten metals above 1,200 °C (TEGS-MPV), liquid-metal pumps and heat exchangers, methane pyrolysis for CO2-free hydrogen, and high-energy-density flow batteries. Methods combine molecular-dynamics phonon simulation, custom heat-transfer experiments, and pilot-scale energy hardware.

ashesh-rambachan-economics.s: Rambachan is an econometrician working at the intersection of machine learning, causal inference, and public policy. His research builds methods for robust inference in difference-in-differences, event studies, and other quasi-experimental designs, and studies how predictive algorithms used in hiring, lending, and criminal justice can amplify or correct discrimination. The work is mostly theoretical statistics with applied economic analysis on real administrative datasets.

b-russell-physics.s: Brooke Russell is an experimental neutrino physicist who joined MIT Physics in 2025 after a Pappalardo Fellowship. Her group works on the international DUNE experiment, where it aims to make high-precision measurements of how neutrinos oscillate between flavors and use those measurements to look for physics beyond the Standard Model — including the mass ordering of neutrinos and possible CP-violation in the lepton sector. Methods are large liquid-argon time projection chamber detectors, particle reconstruction algorithms, and data analysis on accelerator-neutrino beams.
b-russell-physics.notes: Original labs.json entry has only 'B. Russell' — actually Brooke Russell, joined MIT 2025.

barbara-imperiali-biology.s: The Imperiali lab works at the interface of chemistry and biology, focusing on protein glycosylation — the attachment of sugar chains that controls how proteins fold, are trafficked, and signal. They dissect the membrane-embedded enzyme machinery (oligosaccharyltransferases and glycosyltransferases) of bacterial pathogens, design small-molecule and peptide inhibitors as chemical probes, and develop fluorescent sensors that report on glycosylation and other cellular processes in real time. Methods span synthetic organic chemistry, enzymology, X-ray crystallography, and live-cell fluorescence imaging.

barbara-liskov-eecs.s: Liskov is a Turing Award winner whose group at CSAIL studies programming methodology, language design, and the theory and practice of fault-tolerant distributed systems. She designed CLU (the first language to support data abstraction) and Argus (the first to support distributed programming), and her group co-developed Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT), a replication algorithm that lets distributed systems keep working correctly even when some machines fail or behave maliciously. Current work centers on Byzantine fault-tolerant storage and programming-language support for reliable distributed computation.

barton-zwiebach-physics.s: Theoretical physicist working in string theory, with central contributions to string field theory — the early construction of the field theory of open strings and subsequent development of closed string field theory. Additional work covers D-branes with exceptional symmetry, tachyon condensation, and the AdS/CFT correspondence. Author of the undergraduate textbook A First Course in String Theory (Cambridge).

benedetto-marelli-cee.s: Engineers structural biopolymers — primarily silk fibroin — into bio-inspired materials operating at biotic/abiotic interfaces, spanning agriculture, food preservation, and environmental mitigation. Work includes edible silk-based coatings that extend the shelf life of perishable foods (spun out as Mori), silk micro-needles for fish vaccination, and silk seed coatings for drought-resilient crops. Methods combine biofabrication, self-assembly, biomineralization, and materials characterization.

bengt-holmström-economics.s: Paul A. Samuelson Professor of Economics (Emeritus), jointly appointed at MIT Sloan and the Department of Economics, and co-recipient of the 2016 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences for contributions to contract theory. Research shows how a principal should design an optimal contract for an agent whose actions are only partly observable, weighing risk against incentives (the informativeness principle and multitasking models). The theoretical framework underpins policy design from executive compensation to bankruptcy law and political institutions.

benjamin-olken-economics.s: Development economist whose research targets the performance of the public sector in developing countries — corruption, taxation, social protection, and governance — primarily through large randomized controlled trials (RCTs). Landmark work on Indonesian village road audits showed top-down monitoring substantially reduces corruption. Serves as Faculty Director of the Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL) and co-chair of its Social Protection Initiative.

benoit-forget-nse.s: Department Head of Nuclear Science and Engineering, leading the Computational Reactor Physics Group, which develops high-fidelity neutron transport simulations of fission reactors. The group originated and maintains OpenMC (continuous-energy Monte Carlo with constructive solid geometry) and OpenMOC (3D method-of-characteristics), both open-source and parallel-optimized. Research spans Monte Carlo and deterministic transport, multiphysics coupling, and uncertainty quantification for next-generation reactor design.

betar-m-gallant-meche.l: Gallant Energy and Carbon Conversion Lab
betar-m-gallant-meche.s: Develops advanced battery chemistries and materials for high-energy rechargeable and primary batteries, with emphasis on fluorinated cathode conversion reactions and lithium- and calcium-metal anodes and their interfaces. A second research thread integrates electrochemistry with CO2 capture, using carbon dioxide directly as a reactant in metal-gas primary batteries and conversion schemes. Methods include electrochemical characterization, solid-electrolyte interphase analysis, and in situ spectroscopy of battery interfaces.

bevin-p-engelward-bioe.s: Studies the interplay between DNA damage, repair, and downstream consequences (cell toxicity, mutagenesis, genomic instability, and disease), with particular focus on homologous recombination between sister chromatids and homologous chromosomes. The lab has developed a suite of high-throughput microfabricated assays — CometChip, HepaCometChip, NanoCometChip, EpiCometChip, MicroColonyChip, SpheroidChip — for quantifying DNA damage, methylation, cell growth, and hepatocyte toxicology. Engelward directs the MIT Superfund Research Program on environmental mutagens.

bilge-yildiz-nse.s: Investigates solid-state ionic-electronic materials and their surfaces/interfaces for next-generation energy conversion and information-processing devices, including solid oxide fuel cells, water and CO2 electrolysis, solid-state Li-ion batteries, memristive and brain-inspired analog computing, and corrosion-resistant films. Focuses on how perovskite-oxide cathodes and anodes evolve at the surface under electrochemical, thermal, and gas stimuli to elucidate degradation mechanisms. Methods include in situ X-ray spectroscopy, scanning probe microscopy, and first-principles (DFT) modeling.

bjorn-poonen-math.s: Distinguished Professor in Science working primarily in arithmetic geometry and number theory — developing methods to find rational points on algebraic varieties and to prove that others have none. Contributions include proofs that certain problems in number theory and algebraic geometry are undecidable, plus incidental work in probability and theoretical computer science. Four-time Putnam Competition winner and founding managing editor of Algebra & Number Theory.

bonnie-berger-math.l: Berger Lab — Computation and Biology Group (CSAIL)
bonnie-berger-math.s: Simons Professor of Mathematics and head of the Computation and Biology group at CSAIL, designing algorithms that extract biological insight from massive genomic and biomedical datasets. Research spans compressive genomics, network integration, structural bioinformatics, genomic privacy (secure multi-party computation on genomes), and medical genomics for disease mechanism discovery. Associate member of the Broad Institute and HST faculty.

bradford-h-hager-eaps.s: Cecil and Ida Green Professor of Earth Sciences studying the relationship between space-geodetic observations of surface deformation and the dynamical processes of Earth's interior, including mantle convection, plate tectonics, and the earthquake cycle. Research covers tectonic earthquakes in regional fault systems as well as deformation and induced seismicity from reservoir production, hydrocarbon extraction, and carbon sequestration. Serves on NASA's NISAR Science Definition Team; former director of the MIT Earth Resources Laboratory (ERL).

bradley-l-pentelute-chemistry.s: Combines peptide chemistry, molecular biology, and instrumentation to build peptide- and protein-based therapeutics and chemical biology tools. The lab built the world's fastest automated flow-based solid-phase peptide synthesizer — forming amide bonds in ~7 seconds and whole residues in ~40 seconds — enabling total chemical synthesis of proteins up to 164 amino acids over 327 consecutive reactions. Additional work develops selective cysteine/lysine bioconjugation (the pi-clamp) and engineered nontoxic anthrax protective-antigen pumps that deliver antibody mimics, mirror-image proteins, and antisense oligonucleotides into cells.

brandon-j-dekosky-koch-institute.s: Develops high-throughput single-cell platforms that pair antibody heavy- and light-chain sequencing with functional screens to map the genetic and functional diversity of adaptive immune responses. Applies these tools to discover biologic drugs and characterize disease immunity, with application focus on HIV-1 and malaria prevention, RSV vaccine design, and personalized cancer therapeutics. Joint appointment in Chemical Engineering and the Koch Institute, with core Ragon Institute affiliation.

brandon-weissbourd-biology.s: Uses the transparent, genetically tractable jellyfish Clytia hemisphaerica as a model organism to study nervous system evolution, development, regeneration, and function at the interface of systems and evolutionary neuroscience. Stable F1 transgenic Clytia lines support cell-type-specific ablation and whole-organism GCaMP calcium imaging of neuronal activity. Research asks how a simple, decentralized nervous system remains robust to continuous neuron turnover and large-scale injury.

brent-minchew-eaps.s: Investigates the mechanics of flowing ice and the interactions between climate, the cryosphere, and the solid Earth, focusing on glacier-bed mechanics, ice-ocean interactions, ice rheology, and fracture mechanics in ice. Observations rely on interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) and other geodetic techniques, combined with physics-based models to forecast ice-sheet contribution to sea-level rise, especially from Greenland and Antarctica. The group also studies how microscopic defects in ice grains influence large-scale glacier flow.

brett-a-mcguire-chemistry.s: Combines physical chemistry, high-resolution rotational (microwave/millimeter-wave) spectroscopy, and radio astronomy (GBT, ALMA) to detect and identify new molecules in the interstellar medium, particularly in the cold, dense dust clouds where stars and planets form. The group pioneered unbiased signal-processing and statistical methods for line identification and delivered the first detections of propylene oxide (first chiral molecule in space, 2016) and benzonitrile (first aromatic molecule in space, 2018). Research addresses how prebiotic chemical complexity emerges during star and planet formation.

brian-anthony-meche.s: Principal Research Scientist designing computational instruments and techniques for monitoring, measuring, and controlling complex physical systems across manufacturing, medical imaging, and autonomous platforms. Research spans computer vision, acoustic and ultrasonic imaging, large-scale simulation, optimization, metrology, and robotics — including the Medical Electronic Device Realization Center's wearable ultrasound and diagnostic devices. Directs MIT's Master of Engineering in Manufacturing and serves as Associate Director of MIT.nano.

brian-l-wardle-meche.s: Develops nanoengineered advanced composites and bulk nanostructured materials for aerospace structures and multifunctional applications — including aligned carbon nanotube (A-CNT) reinforced laminates, carbon-based material synthesis, and additively manufactured structures. Recent work on 'nanostitching' uses CNT arrays to toughen weak interlaminar regions in composites, producing lighter, more damage-tolerant aircraft skins. The group also builds power-MEMS devices (micro fuel cells, energy harvesters) and damage-sensing structural composites.

bruce-d-walker-biology.s: Investigates cellular immune responses in chronic human viral infection, with particular focus on HIV-specific CD8+ and CD4+ T-cell responses and mechanisms by which rare 'elite controllers' suppress HIV without antiretroviral therapy. Discoveries include the first descriptions of HIV-specific CD8+ and CD4+ T cells and the role of immune checkpoints in T-cell exhaustion during chronic infection. Founding Director of the Ragon Institute of MGH, MIT, and Harvard; HHMI Investigator; co-founder of the Africa Health Research Institute in KwaZulu-Natal.

bruce-tidor-bioe.s: Joint Biological Engineering/EECS group applying theoretical and computational biophysics, applied mathematics, and machine learning to molecular and systems biology. Molecular work dissects the electrostatic and structural determinants of protein folding, stability, and binding specificity, then designs inhibitors using the 'substrate envelope' hypothesis — constraining drugs to the shared substrate volume to forestall resistance mutations (HIV protease, kinases). Systems-level work models intracellular signaling and metabolic networks to guide therapeutic intervention in cancer and infectious disease.

bryan-d-bryson-bioe.s: Combines immunoengineering, molecular microbiology, and systems biology to study host-pathogen interactions in tuberculosis, viewed from both the macrophage and the Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) side. The group uses immunopeptidomics (MHC class I antigen presentation mapping), synthetic biology reporters, and single-cell transcriptomics to identify factors that determine infection outcome and vaccine targets. Core member of the Ragon Institute of MGH, MIT, and Harvard.

c-paus-physics.s: Experimental particle physicist and a lead MIT investigator on the CMS experiment at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, where he co-led the Higgs boson search that reported the 2012 discovery. Current interests include precision measurements of Higgs boson properties, searches for physics beyond the Standard Model, and dark-matter searches at collider energies. Serves as Principal Investigator of the CMS Tier-2 computing center at MIT and was named LPC Senior Distinguished Researcher at Fermilab in 2026.

c-smith-nse.s: KEPCO Professor of the Practice of Nuclear Science and Engineering focused on probabilistic risk assessment (PRA), reliability analysis, and human-reliability modeling for complex nuclear and aerospace systems. Helped develop the SAPHIRE risk-analysis software adopted by NASA after the Columbia accident and extended the SPAR-H human reliability method used by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission. Research emphasizes data-informed approaches for quantifying human actions in plant operation and accident sequences.

camilla-cattania-eaps.s: Seismologist studying earthquake physics and fault mechanics using numerical simulation, fracture mechanics, and statistical seismology. Research spans the behavior of small repeating earthquakes, aftershock triggering via static stress changes, magmatically driven swarms, dynamic triggering, and the interaction of seismic and aseismic slip on isolated fault asperities. Applied goal is operational earthquake forecasting that integrates physics-based understanding with real-time observations.

caroline-uhler-eecs.s: Works at the intersection of machine learning, statistics, and genomics — developing causal-inference algorithms for directed graphical models, representation learning, and scalable methods for learning gene regulatory networks from single-cell data. Characterizes fundamental statistical/computational limits of structure learning in Bayesian networks and builds provably correct algorithms used in disease and drug-response modeling. Core member of the Broad Institute and director of its Eric and Wendy Schmidt Center.

catherine-l-drennan-biology.s: Howard Hughes Medical Institute investigator using X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy to determine atomic-resolution structures of metalloenzymes and understand how protein scaffolds harness metal-based radical chemistry. Systems studied include ribonucleotide reductase (deoxyribonucleotide biosynthesis), glycyl radical enzymes, mononuclear iron enzymes, B12-dependent enzymes, and radical SAM enzymes. Famous early contribution: the first crystal structure of vitamin B12 bound to a protein.

charles-f-harvey-cee.s: Hydrologist and biogeochemist studying chemical transport and reaction in flowing groundwater, groundwater-seawater interaction, and redox-driven contaminant mobilization. Best known for field and modeling work tracing arsenic contamination of drinking water in Bangladesh and Vietnam to microbial reduction of iron oxides fueled by organic carbon leaching from excavated ponds. Field studies quantify how arsenic uptake reduces rice yields and what remediation strategies are viable in South Asian aquifers.

chris-a-kaiser-biology.s: Cell biologist (now emeritus/closed to new trainees) who used the budding yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae to dissect the secretory pathway — protein folding and quality control in the endoplasmic reticulum, vesicle trafficking between the ER and Golgi, and membrane protein sorting. Classical genetic screens of sec mutants and biochemical reconstitution identified distinct gene sets governing COPII vesicle formation and consumption, and delineated a vacuolar disposal route for misfolded soluble proteins. Combined yeast genetics with cell biology and biochemistry to assemble a temporal map of early secretion.

christian-k-wolf-economics.s: Macroeconomist and time-series econometrician developing new identification strategies for the causes of business cycles and for the effects of monetary and fiscal policy shocks. Work covers the 'missing intercept' problem in cross-sectional-to-aggregate demand translation, heterogeneous-agent macro models, and the econometrics of impulse-response estimation (local projections vs. VARs). Faculty Research Fellow at the NBER and 2024 Kiel Institute Excellence Award recipient.

christina-delimitrou-eecs.s: Computer-systems researcher focused on the performance, predictability, and resource efficiency of large-scale cloud and datacenter infrastructures. Research applies machine learning to cloud resource management, scheduling, and distributed debugging, and investigates hardware acceleration and microarchitecture for datacenter servers. Work also characterizes microservices and tail-latency behavior (see the DeathStarBench benchmark suite).

christoph-kehle-math.s: Mathematical analyst working on the Einstein field equations of general relativity, black hole dynamics, and singularity formation. Contributions include the first rigorous construction of an extremal Reissner-Nordström black hole as a threshold of gravitational collapse, disproofs of the third law of black hole thermodynamics, and progress on the Strong Cosmic Censorship conjecture via Diophantine properties of black hole parameters. Methods combine nonlinear PDE, harmonic analysis, and dynamical-systems techniques.

christoph-m-e-paus-physics.s: Experimental particle physicist and a lead MIT investigator on the CMS experiment at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, where he co-led the Higgs boson search that reported the 2012 discovery. Current interests include precision measurements of Higgs boson properties, searches for physics beyond the Standard Model, and dark-matter searches at collider energies. Serves as Principal Investigator of the CMS Tier-2 computing center at MIT and was named LPC Senior Distinguished Researcher at Fermilab in 2026.

christoph-paus-physics.s: Experimental particle physicist and a lead MIT investigator on the CMS experiment at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, where he co-led the Higgs boson search that reported the 2012 discovery. Current interests include precision measurements of Higgs boson properties, searches for physics beyond the Standard Model, and dark-matter searches at collider energies. Serves as Principal Investigator of the CMS Tier-2 computing center at MIT and was named LPC Senior Distinguished Researcher at Fermilab in 2026.

christopher-a-voigt-bioe.s: Engineers genetic circuits in bacterial, yeast, and mammalian cells that implement computational logic and respond to touch, light, and chemical signals. Developed Cello, a Verilog-like electronic-design-automation tool that compiles Boolean circuits into DNA sequences with characterized genetic 'gates.' Applications span nitrogen-fixing soil bacteria for agriculture (Pivot Bio), cell-based therapeutics, and industrial strain engineering; Voigt co-directs the MIT Synthetic Biology Center and MIT-Broad Foundry and heads MIT Biological Engineering.

christopher-b-burge-biology.s: Computational and experimental biologist decoding the regulatory logic of RNA processing — pre-mRNA splicing, alternative polyadenylation, and microRNA-mediated post-transcriptional control. Combines high-throughput RNA-seq, massively parallel splicing reporter assays, and machine-learning motif discovery to identify splicing enhancers/silencers and predict tissue-specific isoform choice. Lab also develops computational tools for ab initio gene finding and microRNA target prediction.

christopher-c-cummins-chemistry.s: Inorganic and organometallic chemist working on small-molecule activation by early-transition-metal complexes (Mo, Nb, W) and main-group elements. Signature achievements include cleaving the N≡N triple bond of dinitrogen at room temperature and ambient pressure using three-coordinate Mo(III) amides — enabling synthetic nitrogen fixation — plus related chemistry for CO2 reduction, P4 (white phosphorus) functionalization, and new phosphorus-element bond constructions. Henry Dreyfus Professor of Chemistry and 2017 ACS Linus Pauling Medalist.

chuchu-fan-lids.s: Builds safe autonomous systems by fusing formal verification, control theory, and machine learning — producing learned neural Lyapunov, barrier, and contraction certificates that come with provable safety and stability guarantees. Applications span drones, satellites, self-driving cars, and medical devices, where the group develops reachability analysis tools and safe reinforcement-learning controllers. Targets safety- and mission-critical deployment where empirical testing alone is insufficient.

connor-w-coley-eecs.s: Develops machine-learning and data-driven methods for small-molecule drug discovery, retrosynthesis planning, reaction outcome prediction, and structure elucidation from spectra. Methods include graph neural networks for molecular representation learning, template-free synthesis models trained on reactions mined from the USPTO patent corpus, model-guided Bayesian optimization, and de novo generative models. Leads the industry-backed Machine Learning for Pharmaceutical Discovery & Synthesis Consortium.

constantinos-daskalakis-eecs.s: Theoretical computer scientist working at the interface of algorithms, game theory, probability, and machine learning. Proved that computing Nash equilibria is PPAD-complete (resolving a central complexity-theory question, Nevanlinna Prize 2018) and developed optimal mechanisms for revenue-maximizing multi-item auctions. Other contributions cover testing high-dimensional distributions, the expectation-maximization algorithm, and statistical inference under dependence.

cullen-buie-bioe.l: Laboratory for Energy and Microsystems Innovation (LEMI)
cullen-buie-bioe.s: Studies microscale flow physics — microfluidics, electrokinetics, and electrochemistry — applied to microbiology and materials science. The group built hourglass-constriction microfluidic chips that use dielectrophoresis to measure bacterial polarizability (a proxy for electrogenic activity), enabling rapid identification of electricity-producing microbes for microbial fuel cells and bioremediation. Co-founder of Kytopen, which commercialized a flow-through 'Flowfect' electroporation technology for high-throughput engineering of primary immune cells.

césar-terrer-cee.s: Terrestrial ecosystem ecologist quantifying how elevated atmospheric CO2, warming, and nitrogen deposition modulate the carbon sink of global vegetation and soils. Uses synthesis of field FACE experiments, satellite remote sensing, and machine-learning meta-analysis to show that plant mycorrhizal type and soil nutrient availability control the magnitude of the CO2 fertilization effect. Recent fieldwork at a CO2-venting volcano in Costa Rica probes long-term CO2 fertilization under natural elevated-CO2 conditions.

d-m-anderson-koch-institute.s: Chemical engineer developing nanomaterials and devices for drug, mRNA, and CRISPR gene-editor delivery, with a focus on ionizable lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) for vaccines and gene therapies. The lab combines high-throughput combinatorial chemistry with machine learning to discover next-generation ionizable lipids (e.g., lipid 119-23) and encapsulated cell therapies for diabetes and cancer. Work underpins multiple biotech companies including CRISPR Therapeutics, Sigilon, oRNA, and Orna Therapeutics.
d-m-anderson-koch-institute.notes: Likely duplicate of daniel-g-anderson-koch-institute. No separate D. M. Anderson faculty found.

daniel-frey-meche.s: Mechanical engineer working on 'robust design' — methods that make engineered systems perform reliably despite manufacturing variation, wear, and environmental disturbance. Research contributions include adaptive experimental design, exploiting problem structure in parameter optimization, and the interplay between simulations and physical experiments. Heads the Design and Manufacturing Area in MIT MechE and has twice received an R&D 100 Award.

daniel-g-anderson-koch-institute.s: Chemical engineer developing nanomaterials and devices for drug, mRNA, and CRISPR gene-editor delivery, with a focus on ionizable lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) for vaccines and gene therapies. The lab combines high-throughput combinatorial chemistry with machine learning to discover next-generation ionizable lipids (e.g., lipid 119-23) and encapsulated cell therapies for diabetes and cancer. Work underpins multiple biotech companies including CRISPR Therapeutics, Sigilon, oRNA, and Orna Therapeutics.

daniel-h-rothman-eaps.s: Mathematical geophysicist using statistical and nonlinear physics to model Earth's carbon cycle, the co-evolution of life and the environment, and the origin of natural geometric forms (river networks, sediment patterns). Built a simple dynamical model of the ocean carbon cycle showing that rapid CO2 injection past a critical threshold can trigger runaway acidification linked to mass extinctions. Co-founded and co-directs the Lorenz Center, MIT's interdisciplinary climate-dynamics research center.

daniel-harlow-physics.s: Theoretical physicist working on quantum gravity and quantum information, especially how spacetime and gravity emerge from underlying quantum-mechanical degrees of freedom. Discovered a deep connection between the AdS/CFT holographic correspondence and quantum error-correcting codes, and (with Patrick Hayden) initiated the use of computational complexity in black-hole information theory. Current work addresses the black-hole information problem, entropy counting, and the quantum structure of cosmology.

daniel-j-lew-biology.s: Cell biologist using budding yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) to dissect how cells establish and maintain spatial polarity during budding, mating, and directed growth. Classic work identified a positive-feedback mechanism that concentrates active Cdc42 GTPase at a single cortical site, enabling symmetry breaking; recent studies use live-cell imaging and particle-based simulations to explain polarity-site competition and relocation. Combines yeast genetics, fluorescence microscopy, and mathematical modeling.

daniel-j-varon-idss.s: Atmospheric scientist using satellite remote sensing to quantify greenhouse gas and air-pollutant emissions, with a major focus on methane from oil-and-gas infrastructure. Techniques include point-source plume inversion from high-resolution imagers (Sentinel-2, PRISMA), regional mass-balance inversions from TROPOMI, real-time geostationary tracking of extreme methane releases, and continuous basin-scale emission estimation. Also developing new satellite methods for nitrogen oxide air-pollution monitoring.

daniel-l-m-suess-chemistry.s: Inorganic/bioinorganic chemistry lab probing the electronic structure and reactivity of iron-sulfur clusters — the polynuclear metallocofactors that catalyze nitrogen fixation, photosynthesis, and many redox steps in biology. Combines de novo synthesis of model clusters with protein biochemistry and advanced spectroscopy (EPR, Mössbauer, magnetic circular dichroism) to correlate Fe-Fe and Fe-S interactions with cluster reactivity in biogenic and synthetic systems. 2024 ACS Inorganic Chemistry Lectureship recipient.

danna-e-freedman-chemistry.s: Synthetic inorganic chemist designing molecular systems for quantum information science — single-molecule qubits whose electronic and nuclear spin states can be coherently manipulated in solution at accessible temperatures. Uses bottom-up synthesis to impose atomic-scale control of qubit-qubit and qubit-environment coupling for quantum sensing (magnetic/electric fields, dark-matter searches) and as building blocks for quantum communication. Characterization employs pulsed EPR, SQUID magnetometry, and low-temperature optical spectroscopy. 2022 MacArthur Fellow.

dara-entekhabi-cee.s: Hydrologist focused on land-atmosphere interactions, terrestrial water and energy cycles, and Earth remote sensing. Serves as science team leader for NASA's Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) satellite, which uses combined L-band radar and radiometer measurements to map global surface soil moisture and its freeze/thaw state. SMAP data improve forecasts of droughts, floods, hurricanes, and agricultural water stress.

darcy-l-mcrose-cee.s: Environmental microbiologist studying how microbial and plant secondary metabolites — especially iron-chelating siderophores — influence nutrient cycling, plant-microbe interactions, and soil fertility. Research combines molecular microbiology, analytical chemistry (LC-MS of metabolites), and geochemistry to ask why bacteria make multiple siderophores and how they are regulated by quorum sensing and iron availability (e.g., in Vibrio harveyi). 2025 Packard Fellow for Science and Engineering.

david-autor-economics.s: Labor economist studying how technological change, automation, and trade globalization reshape employment, wages, and inequality. With Dorn and Hanson, documented that U.S. exposure to Chinese import competition ('the China shock') lowered employment, labor-force participation, and wages in exposed local labor markets; earlier work with Katz and Krueger showed that computerization drove the growing wage premium for college-educated workers. Co-director of the NBER Labor Studies Program and the MIT Stone Center on Inequality and Shaping the Future of Work.

david-c-page-biology.s: Studies the genetics and biology of sex differences — how the X and Y chromosomes contribute to fundamental differences between male and female cells, tissues, and organs in health and disease. Produced the first genetic and physical maps of the human Y chromosome and its reference sequence, reshaping understanding of sex-chromosome evolution. Current work examines how the inactive X in females and the small set of Y-linked ancestral genes in males drive sex-biased gene expression across the body.

david-e-housman-biology.s: Uses genetics to dissect the molecular basis of human disease, with historical impact on positional cloning of disease genes. The lab helped establish the method of using restriction-fragment-length-polymorphism (RFLP) markers to map disease loci, which (with Gusella and the Hereditary Disease Foundation collaboration) localized the Huntington's disease gene HTT in 1983. Current work investigates modifier genes that determine age of onset for trinucleotide-repeat disorders and cardiovascular and cancer genetics.

david-jerison-math.s: Works in harmonic (Fourier) analysis and partial differential equations, with particular interest in free boundary problems and the geometry of solutions. With Carlos Kenig, produced foundational results on harmonic measure and Harnack inequalities in nonsmooth and fractal domains; with John M. Lee, proved sharp extremal inequalities on the Heisenberg group and solved the CR Yamabe problem. MacVicar Faculty Fellow and 2012 Bergman Prize recipient.

david-k-gifford-eecs.s: Develops machine-learning algorithms and experimental methods to model transcriptional regulatory networks and gene expression programs in living cells, with applications in regulatory genomics, epigenomics, and proteomics. Current focuses include motor neuron differentiation, single-cell perturbation screens, chromatin accessibility, antibody design, and MHC-peptide presentation. Methods range from deep sequence models to interpretable discrete combinatorial models of regulation.

david-mcgee-eaps.l: McGee Lab for Paleoclimate and Geochronology
david-mcgee-eaps.s: Reconstructs past rainfall, winds, and hydroclimate using precisely dated geological archives — cave stalagmites, lake sediments, and deep-sea cores — to ground-truth climate models and anticipate future change. Methods include uranium-thorium dating, trace-element and stable-isotope geochemistry (including triple oxygen isotopes) in speleothems, and synthesis against climate model output. Studies span the Sahara green periods, Great Basin lakes, tropical rainbelt shifts, and high-latitude permafrost during past warm intervals.

david-p-bartel-biology.s: Studies microRNAs and other small RNAs and how they regulate mRNA stability and translation in metazoans — work that helped establish microRNAs as pervasive post-transcriptional regulators of most human genes. The lab built TargetScan, the most widely used tool for microRNA target prediction, and continues to dissect the biogenesis, turnover (including two-factor decay signals), and functional targets of microRNAs. HHMI Investigator; Whitehead Member.

dean-eckles-idss.s: Associate Professor of Marketing at MIT Sloan studying social networks, peer effects, and causal inference — particularly how interventions and information spread through networked populations and how to design field experiments that control for interference between units. Methods work includes instrumental variables for peer effects, experimental designs that reduce bias from network interference, and applications to computer-mediated communication and persuasion. Associate Director of IDSS.

deepto-chakrabarty-physics.s: X-ray astrophysicist and Head of the Department of Physics working on the physics of neutron stars — especially accretion-powered millisecond pulsars in binary systems. Co-discovered accretion-powered millisecond X-ray pulsars using NASA's Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer and uses X-ray timing to measure neutron-star spin evolution, masses, radii, and dense-matter equations of state. Current work relies on NASA's NICER instrument aboard the ISS.

desirée-l-plata-cee.s: Environmental chemist focused on quantifying and mitigating industrial impacts, especially emissions of methane and other greenhouse gases from oil and gas infrastructure, coal mines, and agriculture. Developed catalytic zeolite-based materials (including modified cat-litter clays) that oxidize ambient-concentration methane to CO2, a less-potent greenhouse gas. Directs the MIT Methane Network and works on oil-spill remediation, carbon-nanotube environmental fate, and sustainability-by-design metrics for new materials.

devavrat-shah-eecs.s: Works at the intersection of statistical inference, machine learning, and networks — developing algorithms for inference on graphical models, ranking and recommender systems, crowdsourcing, causal inference from observational time series (synthetic controls), and resource allocation in communication networks. Teaches the flagship MIT courses 6.438 Algorithms for Inference and 6.401 Introduction to Statistical Data Analysis. Faculty Director of the Deshpande Center for Technological Innovation.

dina-katabi-eecs.s: Wireless and networks researcher who pioneered contactless, radio-frequency sensing of human physiology — devices (shaped like Wi-Fi routers) that use reflected RF signals and deep learning to monitor breathing, heart rate, sleep stages, gait, and even postures through walls (RF-Pose). These systems have been applied to detect and track progression of Parkinson's, ALS, Alzheimer's, lupus, and atopic dermatitis without wearables. Directs the MIT Center for Wireless Networks and Mobile Computing; National Academy of Medicine member.

dirk-englund-eecs.s: Builds quantum and optical technologies at the device level — controlling single photons and solid-state spin qubits in diamond nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers, quantum dots, and III-V photonic crystals for communication, computation, and sensing. Builds integrated photonic circuits, cryogenic programmable photonic processors, solid-state quantum memories, and graphene-based photodetectors, plus optical accelerators for machine learning inference. Spinouts include Lightmatter (photonic computing), QuEra (neutral-atom quantum computers), and DUST Identity (diamond NV authentication).

domitilla-del-vecchio-bioe.s: Engineers synthetic biology circuits in mammalian cells and bacteria using control-theory principles, designing genetic feedback controllers, load drivers, and resource-allocation modules to make engineered gene networks robust and modular. Methods combine nonlinear ODE modeling, contraction analysis, and stochastic simulation with experimental implementation in E. coli and CHO/HEK cells via plasmid and lentiviral delivery, flow cytometry, and time-lapse fluorescence microscopy. Recent work targets cell-fate control, epigenetic memory dynamics, and cybergenetic feedback for biomanufacturing.

dor-minzer-math.s: Theoretical computer scientist and discrete mathematician working on analysis of Boolean functions, probabilistically checkable proofs (PCPs), and hardness of approximation, including resolution of the 2-to-2 Games Conjecture with Khot, Khot, and Safra. Methods draw on Fourier analysis on the hypercube, expander and Grassmann graphs, hypercontractive inequalities, and Ramsey-type combinatorics. Also studies property testing, agreement testing, and direct-product testing relevant to PCP constructions.

douglas-a-lauffenburger-biology.s: Pioneers cell and molecular bioengineering by combining quantitative experimentation with computational modeling of receptor-ligand signaling, cytokine networks, and cell-cell communication, with applications in cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and immunology. Uses multiplexed mass spectrometry phosphoproteomics, Luminex cytokine panels, single-cell RNA-seq, partial-least-squares regression, and ODE/agent-based models to map signal-to-response relationships. Translational work includes systems pharmacology of TNF, EGFR, and TGF-β pathways and tissue-engineered intestinal organoid models.

dražen-prelec-economics.s: Behavioral economist and mathematical psychologist known for the Bayesian Truth Serum mechanism for eliciting honest opinions from crowds, work on hyperbolic time discounting, and the dual-self model of self-control. Combines axiomatic decision theory with laboratory experiments and fMRI studies of consumer choice and pain-of-paying in purchasing decisions. Also studies confidence calibration, prediction markets, and the neuroeconomics of credit cards versus cash.

drew-fudenberg-economics.s: Game theorist developing the foundations of repeated games, learning in games, and the theory of reputation, including the Folk Theorem with imperfect monitoring (Fudenberg-Maskin) and self-confirming equilibrium. Uses dynamical-systems analysis of fictitious play, stochastic approximation, and evolutionary dynamics, plus laboratory experiments testing equilibrium refinements. Recent work bridges machine learning and economic theory, including algorithmic predictions of human behavior and active learning.

e-smith-physics.s: Experimental particle physicist working on the LHCb experiment at CERN, studying rare decays of beauty and charm hadrons as precision tests of the Standard Model and probes of lepton flavor universality. Methods include silicon-tracker and RICH-detector calibration, multivariate event selection (BDTs, neural nets), and angular analyses of B → K*μμ and similar decays. Also contributes to LHCb upgrade hardware and to searches for hidden-sector and dark photon signatures.

earl-k-miller-bcs.s: Studies the neural basis of high-level cognition — working memory, attention, decision-making, and executive control — by recording from prefrontal, parietal, and striatal cortex in behaving non-human primates. Uses chronic multi-electrode arrays, local field potential and spike-field coherence analysis, and population-decoding techniques to characterize 'mixed selectivity' neurons and oscillatory gamma/beta dynamics underlying cognitive control. Recent work develops the theory that conscious thought arises from synchronized cortical traveling waves and tests it with simultaneous multi-area recordings.

edward-miller-bcs.s: Studies the neural basis of high-level cognition — working memory, attention, decision-making, and executive control — by recording from prefrontal, parietal, and striatal cortex in behaving non-human primates. Uses chronic multi-electrode arrays, local field potential and spike-field coherence analysis, and population-decoding techniques to characterize 'mixed selectivity' neurons and oscillatory gamma/beta dynamics underlying cognitive control. Recent work develops the theory that conscious thought arises from synchronized cortical traveling waves and tests it with simultaneous multi-area recordings.
edward-miller-bcs.notes: Duplicate of earl-k-miller-bcs. No distinct 'Edward Miller' in MIT BCS. Logged in duplicates.txt.

ed-boyden-bioe.s: Invents tools for analyzing and engineering brain circuits, including optogenetics (channelrhodopsin/halorhodopsin neural control), expansion microscopy for nanoscale fluorescence imaging of intact tissue, and genetically encoded voltage indicators for all-optical electrophysiology. Methods span protein engineering, two-photon and light-sheet microscopy, CRISPR delivery, and high-throughput in vivo screening in mouse and zebrafish models. Work supports mechanistic studies of memory, perception, and neuropsychiatric disease and underpins scalable connectomics.

edward-a-boyle-eaps.s: Marine geochemist and paleoceanographer reconstructing past ocean circulation, nutrient cycling, and atmospheric metal pollution from trace-metal and isotope records in foraminifera, corals, and seawater. Methods include high-precision isotope-dilution ICP-MS, trace-metal clean sampling protocols (he helped pioneer the GEOTRACES program), Cd/Ca and Ba/Ca paleoproxies, and lead isotope tracing of anthropogenic pollution. Studies span glacial-interglacial deepwater changes and modern dispersion of leaded-gasoline and coal-burning lead in the global ocean.

edward-gibson-bcs.s: Studies human language processing, comprehension, and the cognitive and communicative pressures shaping linguistic structure across the world's languages, with major contributions to dependency-locality theory and information-theoretic accounts of word order. Methods include self-paced reading and eye-tracking experiments, large-scale crowdsourced linguistic surveys, fieldwork with the Tsimané (Bolivia) and Pirahã (Amazon), and computational corpus analyses with information-theoretic measures. Recent work uses LLM behavioral comparisons to test theories of efficient communication.

edward-h-adelson-bcs.s: Studies human and machine vision, with foundational contributions to the steerable pyramid and motion-energy models of early vision, lightness and material perception, and computational models of mid-level vision. Has translated psychophysical insight into engineering: invented the GelSight tactile sensor, an elastomer-and-camera device that turns surface shape and slip into high-resolution images for robot manipulation. Methods include human psychophysics, computer-graphics simulation, deep-net image analysis, and elastomer/optics fabrication.

edward-s-boyden-bioe.s: Invents tools for analyzing and engineering brain circuits, including optogenetics (channelrhodopsin/halorhodopsin neural control), expansion microscopy for nanoscale fluorescence imaging of intact tissue, and genetically encoded voltage indicators for all-optical electrophysiology. Methods span protein engineering, two-photon and light-sheet microscopy, CRISPR delivery, and high-throughput in vivo screening in mouse and zebrafish models. Work supports mechanistic studies of memory, perception, and neuropsychiatric disease and underpins scalable connectomics.
edward-s-boyden-bioe.notes: Duplicate of ed-boyden-bioe. Same enrichment applied.

elchanan-mossel-math.s: Probabilist and theoretical computer scientist working at the interface of discrete probability, combinatorics, statistical inference, and theoretical CS, including the proof of 'Majority Is Stablest' and the implied UGC-optimality of the Goemans-Williamson MAX-CUT algorithm. Methods draw on Fourier analysis on the hypercube, hypercontractive inequalities, isoperimetry, and Markov random fields. Also studies phylogenetic reconstruction, social-choice theory, the stochastic block model, and the mathematical foundations of deep learning.

elfatih-a-b-eltahir-cee.s: Hydroclimatologist studying interactions between regional climate, land use, water availability, extreme heat, and vector-borne disease. The group develops and runs the MIT Regional Climate Model (MRCM) for downscaling future climate projections and the Hydrology, Entomology and Malaria Transmission Simulator (HYDREMATS) for coupled water-mosquito-population modeling. Recent work projects extreme wet-bulb heat over the Persian Gulf and South Asia and assesses irrigation, dam operation, and reforestation impacts on regional climate.

eliezer-calo-biology.s: Studies ribosome biogenesis, the nucleolus, and RNA metabolism, focusing on how dysfunction in these processes leads to tissue-specific developmental disorders (ribosomopathies such as Treacher Collins syndrome) and cancer. Combines CRISPR genetic screens, RNA-seq and proteomics, biochemical reconstitution of nucleolar condensates, and live-cell imaging in zebrafish and mammalian cell models, with a focus on the TCOF1 gene and pH-sensing dynamics of nucleolar phase separation. Maps how rDNA damage and ribosome assembly defects propagate to developmental phenotypes.

elizabeth-m-nolan-chemistry.s: Bioinorganic chemist studying the metal-centered chemistry of host-pathogen interactions and innate immunity, particularly the 'tug-of-war' for iron, zinc, and manganese between bacteria and the human host. Characterizes siderophores from Salmonella, E. coli, and other pathogens and the host metal-sequestering proteins calprotectin and lactoferrin using X-ray crystallography, ITC, EPR, ICP-MS, and synthetic chemistry. Develops siderophore-antibiotic conjugates ('Trojan-horse' antibiotics) and siderophore-based vaccines targeting drug-resistant bacteria.

ellen-t-roche-meche.l: Therapeutic Technology Design and Development Lab
ellen-t-roche-meche.s: Designs implantable biomedical devices that combine soft robotics, biomaterials, and image-based biomimicry, with a focus on cardiac assist devices and therapy delivery. Notable inventions include a soft robotic ventricular assist sleeve that mechanically squeezes a failing heart, the Therepi reservoir for sustained drug/cell delivery to damaged myocardium, and a biorobotic hybrid heart that integrates explanted tissue with synthetic actuation. Methods include elastomer fabrication, finite-element simulation, ex vivo and in vivo large-animal testing, and 4D MRI imaging.

elly-nedivi-biology.s: Studies molecular and cellular mechanisms of structural plasticity in the developing and adult mammalian visual cortex, including identification of activity-regulated genes (CPG15/neuritin) and their roles in dendritic and synaptic remodeling. Pioneered multi-color two-photon in vivo imaging to track inhibitory and excitatory synapses on individual neurons over days in awake mice during monocular deprivation paradigms. Combines mouse genetics, viral vectors, electron microscopy reconstruction, and chronic cranial-window imaging.

emery-n-brown-bcs.s: Anesthesiologist-statistician studying how general anesthetics produce loss of consciousness by altering thalamocortical and arousal-circuit dynamics, and develops signal-processing algorithms to read out brain state from EEG. Methods include simultaneous EEG/fMRI in human subjects, intracranial recordings, point-process state-space models for spike trains, and pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic modeling of propofol, ketamine, and dexmedetomidine. Translates findings into closed-loop EEG-guided anesthesia delivery.

eric-j-alm-bioe.s: Combines computational and experimental approaches to engineer the human gut microbiome and translate microbiome science into clinical and environmental applications. Methods include shotgun metagenomic and 16S rRNA sequencing, isolation and culturing of gut bacteria, longitudinal stool sampling, fecal microbiota transplant trials for IBD and C. difficile, and wastewater epidemiology (Biobot) for community-level pathogen surveillance. Also studies microbial evolution and horizontal gene transfer across global populations through the Global Microbiome Conservancy.

eric-s-lander-biology.s: Genomicist who led the public Human Genome Project and founded the Broad Institute; lab studies the molecular basis of rare and common human disease, cancer genomics, gene-regulatory mechanisms, and human population history. Methods include whole-genome and exome sequencing, single-cell RNA-seq, CRISPR genome-wide screens, GWAS and statistical genetics, and genome-wide linkage and haplotype analysis. Has developed general approaches for cancer-subtype discovery from expression data and identified driver mutations across tumor types.

erik-d-demaine-eecs.s: Theoretical computer scientist working on algorithms, data structures, and computational geometry, with celebrated results in geometric folding and unfolding of linkages, paper, and polyhedra (computational origami). Studies cache-oblivious and dynamic data structures, fixed-parameter tractability, and the computational complexity of puzzles and games (proving PSPACE/NP-hardness for many recreational problems). Methods are pure CS theory: combinatorial proofs, algorithm analysis, and reduction-based hardness arguments.

erin-kara-physics.s: Observational X-ray astrophysicist studying accreting supermassive and stellar-mass black holes, mapping the geometry of accretion disks and coronae through X-ray reverberation mapping — measuring time delays between direct and reflected X-ray photons to probe gas within a few gravitational radii of the event horizon. Uses NICER (on the ISS), XMM-Newton, NuSTAR, and the upcoming XRISM and AXIS missions; analyzes Fe Kα fluorescence lines, quasi-periodic eruptions, and tidal disruption events. Co-chairs the supermassive-black-hole working group of XRISM.

ernest-fraenkel-bioe.s: Computational systems biologist integrating multi-omic data — RNA-seq, proteomics, phosphoproteomics, ATAC-seq, metabolomics — to reconstruct disease-altered signaling and gene-regulatory networks in cancer, ALS, Huntington's disease, and diabetes. Develops network-optimization algorithms (Omics Integrator / Steiner-tree based methods) to find regulators connecting genetic variants to molecular and clinical phenotypes. Closely partners with Answer ALS to apply these methods to patient-derived iPSC-motor-neuron datasets.

esther-duflo-economics.l: Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL)
esther-duflo-economics.s: Development economist and 2019 Nobel laureate who pioneered the use of randomized controlled trials to evaluate anti-poverty interventions in education, health, microfinance, governance, and climate adaptation across low- and middle-income countries. Co-founded and co-directs J-PAL, a global network of 900+ affiliated researchers running field experiments. Methods are empirical microeconometrics: cluster-randomized trials, difference-in-differences, instrumental variables, and structural estimation.

ethan-peterson-nse.s: Nuclear engineer working on neutronics and radiation transport for fusion power plant design, leading the neutronics group at the MIT Plasma Science and Fusion Center. Develops and validates Monte Carlo neutron transport tools (especially OpenMC) for fusion applications, performing nuclear analysis for the SPARC tokamak, the LIBRA liquid-immersion blanket experiment, and tritium-breeding-blanket designs. Methods couple Monte Carlo transport with depletion/transmutation calculations and CAD-based geometry workflows.

eugene-a-fitzgerald-dmse.s: Studies lattice-mismatched semiconductor heteroepitaxy, defects, and dislocation dynamics, with a focus on integrating compound semiconductors (GaN, InGaP, GaAs) onto silicon for low-energy electronics, displays, and multi-junction photovoltaics. Pioneered relaxed SiGe-on-Si buffers that enabled commercial strained-silicon CMOS (commercialized via AmberWave). Methods include MOCVD and MBE growth, transmission electron microscopy of dislocations, X-ray diffraction, and device fabrication and characterization.

evelina-fedorenko-bcs.s: Studies the cognitive and neural architecture of human language, mapping the high-level language network and its functional dissociation from systems for music, math, theory of mind, and computer code, using individual-subject fMRI 'localizer' methods. Combines fMRI, MEG/EEG, intracranial ECoG and direct cortical stimulation in neurosurgical patients, behavioral psycholinguistics, large-scale crowdsourced studies, and computational modeling with LLMs to test theories of efficient communication. Recent work identifies neuronal populations with distinct temporal integration windows for linguistic input.

evelyn-n-wang-meche.s: Studies micro/nanoscale heat and mass transfer for thermal management, water harvesting, desalination, and solar thermal energy conversion.
