Notable work includes a metal-organic-framework (MOF)-based atmospheric water harvester that pulls drinkable water from desert air, a passive multistage solar still for high-efficiency desalination, and nanostructured surfaces for boiling/condensation enhancement and electronics cooling. Methods combine micro/nanofabrication, in-situ ESEM imaging, and continuum heat-transfer modeling.

eytan-modiano-lids.s: Studies the design and analysis of communication networks — wireless, satellite, and optical — drawing on network optimization, queueing theory, graph theory, and reinforcement learning. Develops cross-layer resource-allocation algorithms for cross-layer routing/scheduling, age-of-information minimization, and stability of dynamic networks. Methods are mathematical: Lyapunov drift-plus-penalty, stochastic network optimization, MDP/RL for control of partially-observable network state.

f-zhang-bioe.s: Develops and applies CRISPR-based tools for genome and transcriptome engineering, having engineered SpCas9 for mammalian genome editing and discovered/characterized Cas12, Cas13, and other class 2 CRISPR effectors via computational mining of microbial genomes. Built the SHERLOCK nucleic-acid diagnostic platform (Cas13-based) and REPAIR/RESCUE RNA base editors, plus AAV-deliverable compact CRISPR systems for in vivo therapy. Methods include structural biology of Cas effectors, directed evolution, deep mutational scanning, and large-scale CRISPR screens in primary cells and organoids.

facundo-d-batista-biology.s: Studies B-lymphocyte biology — B-cell receptor signaling and dynamics, germinal-center selection, and antibody affinity maturation — and applies the findings to rational vaccine design against HIV, malaria, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2. Builds humanized and BCR-knock-in mouse models for preclinical immunogen testing and uses two-photon intravital imaging of lymph nodes, single-cell BCR sequencing, flow cytometry, and biochemistry of receptor-cytoskeleton interactions. Recent work probes B-cell metabolism (autophagy, mitochondrial flux) as a driver of humoral immunity.

fadel-adib-eecs.s: Invents wireless and sensing technologies that perceive the physical world through walls, water, and skin, with applications in health monitoring, robotics, and ocean IoT. Builds RF-based systems that track human motion, breathing, heart rate, and emotion through walls using WiFi/mmWave reflections; battery-free underwater backscatter sensors using piezoelectric resonators for ocean exploration; and RFID-based localization and inventory tracking. Methods span wireless channel modeling, MIMO signal processing, and hardware/antenna prototyping.

fang-wang-bcs.s: Studies neural circuits governing bidirectional brain-body interactions — the sensory and affective dimensions of pain and addiction, central pain-suppression by general anesthesia, and sensorimotor circuits for active touch, eating, drinking, and movement. Methods include monosynaptic rabies-virus circuit tracing, AAV-based intersectional genetic targeting, two-photon and miniscope calcium imaging in behaving mice, optogenetic and chemogenetic manipulation, and fiber photometry. Co-directs the K. Lisa Yang and Hock E. Tan Center for Molecular Therapeutics.
fang-wang-bcs.notes: Likely misnaming of Fan Wang (BCS/McGovern). No 'Fang Wang' found at MIT BCS.

frank-wang-bcs.s: Studies neural circuits governing bidirectional brain-body interactions — the sensory and affective dimensions of pain and addiction, central pain-suppression by general anesthesia, and sensorimotor circuits for active touch, eating, drinking, and movement. Methods include monosynaptic rabies-virus circuit tracing, AAV-based intersectional genetic targeting, two-photon and miniscope calcium imaging in behaving mice, optogenetic and chemogenetic manipulation, and fiber photometry. Co-directs the K. Lisa Yang and Hock E. Tan Center for Molecular Therapeutics.
frank-wang-bcs.notes: Likely misnaming of Fan Wang (BCS/McGovern). No 'Frank Wang' found at MIT BCS.

farnaz-niroui-eecs.s: Develops hybrid nanofabrication techniques to build sub-10-nm structures that exploit nanoscale physics for new electronic, optoelectronic, and quantum devices. Studies excitonic and plasmonic light-matter interactions in halide-perovskite nanocrystals for on-demand single-photon sources, neuromorphic metamaterials, and electromechanical NEMS. Methods combine electron-beam lithography, self-assembly, surface chemistry, and in-situ TEM/AFM characterization of charge transport and exciton dynamics.

feng-zhang-bioe.s: Develops and applies CRISPR-based tools for genome and transcriptome engineering, having engineered SpCas9 for mammalian genome editing and discovered/characterized Cas12, Cas13, and other class 2 CRISPR effectors via computational mining of microbial genomes. Built the SHERLOCK nucleic-acid diagnostic platform (Cas13-based) and REPAIR/RESCUE RNA base editors, plus AAV-deliverable compact CRISPR systems for in vivo therapy. Methods include structural biology of Cas effectors, directed evolution, deep mutational scanning, and large-scale CRISPR screens in primary cells and organoids.

frank-schilbach-economics.s: Behavioral development economist studying previously neglected correlates of poverty — substance abuse, physical pain, sleep deprivation, depression — and their effects on cognition, decision-making, and worker productivity in low-income populations. Runs randomized field experiments through the Behavioral Development Lab in India, with measurements of worker output, lab-in-the-field decision tasks, actigraphy for sleep, and structured psychiatric assessments. Co-affiliated with J-PAL and NBER.

g-fournier-eaps.s: Geobiologist using molecular phylogenetics to reconstruct the evolutionary histories of microbial genes and genomes across geological timescales, with a focus on horizontal gene transfer events and their use as molecular clocks for the early biosphere. Combines phylogenomic analysis of bacterial and archaeal lineages with the geochemical and isotopic record to date events such as the Great Oxidation Event and the origin of methanogens. Also explores biosignatures relevant to astrobiology.

g-lusztig-math.s: Foundational figure in modern representation theory, working on representations of finite reductive groups, p-adic groups, Hecke algebras, quantum groups, and Weyl groups. Introduced Kazhdan-Lusztig polynomials, character sheaves, Deligne-Lusztig varieties, canonical bases for quantum groups, and the theory of total positivity in algebraic groups. Methods are pure algebra and algebraic geometry: ℓ-adic cohomology of varieties over finite fields, perverse sheaves, and combinatorics of Coxeter groups.

g-m-innocenti-physics.s: Experimental high-energy nuclear physicist studying the quark-gluon plasma in ultra-relativistic heavy-ion collisions at the CMS experiment at the LHC, with emphasis on heavy-flavor (charm and beauty) production and jet substructure as probes of the QGP. Also leads a program on photon-photon and photon-nucleus scattering in ultra-peripheral collisions to constrain low-x gluon distributions, and develops CMOS monolithic silicon trackers for the future ePIC experiment at the Electron-Ion Collider. Methods include multivariate jet-flavor tagging, BDT/ML event selection, and precision tracker hardware design.

gian-michele-innocenti-physics.s: Experimental high-energy nuclear physicist studying the quark-gluon plasma in ultra-relativistic heavy-ion collisions at the CMS experiment at the LHC, with emphasis on heavy-flavor (charm and beauty) production and jet substructure as probes of the QGP. Also leads a program on photon-photon and photon-nucleus scattering in ultra-peripheral collisions to constrain low-x gluon distributions, and develops CMOS monolithic silicon trackers for the future ePIC experiment at the Electron-Ion Collider. Methods include multivariate jet-flavor tagging, BDT/ML event selection, and precision tracker hardware design.

gabriele-farina-eecs.s: Theoretical computer scientist working on computational game theory, online learning, and equilibrium computation in large-scale imperfect-information games such as poker and security games. Develops scalable Nash and correlated-equilibrium solvers, no-regret learning dynamics in extensive-form games, and trembling-hand equilibrium refinements; recent work casts LLM decoding as an imperfect-information signaling game ('equilibrium ranking'). Methods combine convex optimization, regret minimization, and tensor-decomposition algorithms.

gang-chen-meche.s: Studies nanoscale energy transport — phonon, electron, and photon — and applications in thermoelectrics, solar thermal energy conversion, water purification, and thermal management. Notable contributions include solving the phonon Boltzmann transport equation in nanostructures, experimentally extracting phonon mean-free-path distributions in solids, and developing high-thermal-conductivity polyethylene nanofibers and high-ZT nanocomposite thermoelectrics. Methods combine first-principles DFT, ballistic-diffusive heat transport theory, ultrafast pump-probe thermoreflectance, and nanofabrication.

gareth-h-mckinley-meche.s: Studies the rheology and dynamics of complex fluids — polymers, gels, surfactants, suspensions, food and consumer products — under shear and especially extension. Develops extensional rheometry instruments (CaBER, FiSER), microrheology, and field-responsive (electro/magneto) rheology; also studies super-hydrophobic/omniphobic surfaces and droplet impact on nanostructured substrates. Methods couple high-speed imaging and microfluidics with constitutive-model continuum mechanics for non-Newtonian fluids.

genewei-li-biology.s: Studies the quantitative principles of bacterial gene expression — how cells precisely tune transcription, translation, and RNA processing to set protein synthesis rates. Pioneered ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) refinements and developed Rend-seq to map mRNA boundaries genome-wide; uses single-molecule fluorescence to track ribosome initiation kinetics in living B. subtilis and E. coli. Recent work characterizes 'runaway transcription' — uncoupling of transcription from translation — across diverse bacterial species.

george-b-benedek-physics.s: Biophysicist studying phase transitions, self-assembly, and aggregation of biological macromolecules and their connection to disease — most famously cataract (lens crystallin condensation), Alzheimer's amyloid fibril formation, and cholesterol gallstone nucleation. Pioneered quasi-elastic (dynamic) light scattering as a tool to measure protein diffusion, size, and critical phenomena in protein solutions. Methods combine static and dynamic light scattering, NMR, and statistical-mechanics theory of condensed phases.

george-barbastathis-meche.s: Studies computational imaging and 3D optics, combining the physics of light propagation (digital holography, volume holography, gradient-index media, transformation optics) with deep-learning methods for inverse problems. Develops phase-retrieval and tomographic reconstruction algorithms based on physics-informed neural networks for X-ray, electron, and visible-light microscopy. Also designs metasurface-based achromatic lenses and computational lensless cameras.

gigliola-staffilani-math.s: Mathematical analyst working on dispersive nonlinear partial differential equations, especially the nonlinear Schrödinger and Korteweg-de Vries equations. Co-developed (with Colliander, Keel, Takaoka, Tao) the 'I-method' of almost-conserved energies and proved global well-posedness for energy-subcritical and quintic defocusing NLS, frequency-cascade behavior on the 2-torus, and symplectic non-squeezing for periodic KdV. Methods combine harmonic analysis (Strichartz estimates, multilinear restriction) with analytic number theory and probabilistic PDE.

gioele-zardini-cee.s: Engineering researcher working on the mathematical co-design of complex autonomous and mobility systems, formalizing the joint optimization of hardware, software, and infrastructure choices for self-driving vehicles, robots, and urban transportation networks. Uses applied category theory (compositionality of design problems), monotone optimization on partial orders, game theory, and reinforcement learning to make system-level trade-offs computationally tractable. Applications include AV fleet sizing, sensor selection, and policy design.

giovanni-traverso-meche.s: Physician-engineer developing ingestible electronics, robotic capsules, long-acting drug-delivery depots, and bioresorbable implants to improve medication adherence and enable closed-loop sensing/therapy in the GI tract. Notable inventions include a star-shaped gastric-retentive pill that resides in the stomach for days, a self-orienting millimeter-scale applicator (SOMA) that injects insulin and biologics through the gastric mucosa, ingestible vital-sign sensors, and triggerable tough hydrogels. Methods combine elastomer/polymer fabrication, mechanical testing, large-animal preclinical studies, and clinical translation.

gloria-b-choi-bcs.s: Studies the bidirectional interactions between the immune system and the brain, asking how cytokines (IL-17a, IL-1β, IFN-γ) act as neuromodulators that shape neural circuits underlying social behavior, mood, and sickness behavior. Recent work mapped how IL-17a from gut Th17 cells reshapes mouse cortical S1DZ activity to alter sociability in maternal immune-activation autism models, and how IL-1β acting on the dorsal raphe drives self-imposed social isolation during sickness. Methods include mouse genetics, optogenetics, in vivo two-photon imaging, EEG, and behavioral assays.

gregory-p-fournier-eaps.s: Geobiologist using molecular phylogenetics to reconstruct the evolutionary histories of microbial genes and genomes across geological timescales, with a focus on horizontal gene transfer events and their use as molecular clocks for the early biosphere. Combines phylogenomic analysis of bacterial and archaeal lineages with the geochemical and isotopic record to date events such as the Great Oxidation Event and the origin of methanogens. Also explores biosignatures relevant to astrobiology.

guoping-feng-bcs.s: Studies the molecular and circuit mechanisms of synaptic communication and how their disruption causes neurodevelopmental and psychiatric disorders, especially autism spectrum disorder, OCD, schizophrenia, and depression. Generates and analyzes mouse and macaque models of human disease genes (SHANK3, MECP2, CNTNAP2, PTCHD1) using CRISPR knock-in, optogenetics, two-photon imaging, and electrophysiology, with emphasis on the thalamic reticular nucleus and striatum. Demonstrated that adult restoration of SHANK3 expression can rescue autism-like phenotypes in mice.

guy-bresler-eecs.s: Theoretical computer scientist working at the interface of statistics, information theory, probability, and theoretical CS, studying the relationship between combinatorial structure and computational tractability in high-dimensional inference. Develops and analyzes algorithms for learning graphical models (Ising and Markov random fields), low-rank matrix completion and recommendation systems, community detection in stochastic block models, and statistical-computational gaps. Methods include information-theoretic lower bounds, low-degree polynomial frameworks, and reductions from planted-clique.

h-f-lodish-biology.s: Cell biologist studying the molecular biology of erythropoiesis — how hematopoietic stem cells become mature red blood cells — and broader signaling and trafficking biology of mammalian cells. Notable contributions include cloning the erythropoietin receptor and characterizing its JAK2/STAT5 signaling, discovery of erythroid-specific microRNAs and lncRNAs, and identification of glucocorticoid- and oxygen-regulated pathways that expand BFU-E progenitors for therapy of refractory anemias. Lead author of the textbook Molecular Cell Biology.

harvey-lodish-biology.s: Cell biologist studying the molecular biology of erythropoiesis — how hematopoietic stem cells become mature red blood cells — and broader signaling and trafficking biology of mammalian cells. Notable contributions include cloning the erythropoietin receptor and characterizing its JAK2/STAT5 signaling, discovery of erythroid-specific microRNAs and lncRNAs, and identification of glucocorticoid- and oxygen-regulated pathways that expand BFU-E progenitors for therapy of refractory anemias. Lead author of the textbook Molecular Cell Biology.

h-robert-horvitz-biology.s: Geneticist who used the nematode C. elegans to identify the genetic pathway controlling programmed cell death (apoptosis), discovering ced-3 (a caspase), ced-4 (Apaf-1 ortholog), and ced-9 (Bcl-2 ortholog) — the conserved core of the apoptosis machinery. Awarded the 2002 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine. Continues to use forward genetics in C. elegans to dissect neuronal development, synapse function, behavior, and the genetic basis of neurodegeneration; methods include mutagenesis screens, single-cell laser ablation, RNAi, and CRISPR.

hal-abelson-eecs.s: Computer scientist focused on computer-science education, civic technology, and the intersection of computing with intellectual-property and free-software policy. Co-author (with Gerald Sussman) of the Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP) textbook based on the Scheme dialect of Lisp, and creator/director of MIT App Inventor — a visual blocks-based programming environment that has been used by over a million people to build Android apps. Founding director of Creative Commons and the Free Software Foundation.

harold-abelson-eecs.s: Computer scientist focused on computer-science education, civic technology, and the intersection of computing with intellectual-property and free-software policy. Co-author (with Gerald Sussman) of the Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (SICP) textbook based on the Scheme dialect of Lisp, and creator/director of MIT App Inventor — a visual blocks-based programming environment that has been used by over a million people to build Android apps. Founding director of Creative Commons and the Free Software Foundation.

hamsa-balakrishnan-aeroastro.l: Dynamics, Infrastructure Networks, and Mobility (DINaMo) Group
hamsa-balakrishnan-aeroastro.s: Studies control, optimization, and machine learning for large-scale cyber-physical infrastructure, particularly air transportation: airport surface congestion, airspace resource allocation, runway scheduling, weather-impacted flight delay prediction, and environmental impact mitigation. Methods include integer and stochastic optimization, queueing-network analysis of air traffic flow, MDP/RL for taxi-out and gate-hold control, and ML/regression on operational FAA datasets. Also works on counter-UAS, drone airspace integration, and orbital debris management.

harikesh-s-wong-biology.s: Studies how immune cells communicate through cytokine and chemokine signaling to discriminate self from non-self and control the magnitude and spatial extent of inflammatory responses in tissues. Uses high-resolution intravital and lattice light-sheet fluorescence microscopy in mouse lymph nodes and infected tissues, single-cell RNA-seq, mathematical modeling of feedback/feedforward circuits, and CRISPR perturbation of cytokine signaling components. Lab is jointly based at the MIT Department of Biology and the Ragon Institute.

haruko-wainwright-nse.s: Develops integrated environmental monitoring systems for nuclear waste sites, contaminated groundwater, and climate-impacted ecosystems by fusing in-situ sensors, geophysical surveys (electrical resistivity, GPR), satellite remote sensing, reactive-transport simulations, and machine learning. Co-leads the DOE ALTEMIS project and has applied this paradigm to legacy DOE sites and Fukushima radioactive contamination. Methods include Bayesian data assimilation, Gaussian processes for spatiotemporal interpolation, and uncertainty quantification.

haynes-miller-math.s: Algebraic topologist working in stable and chromatic homotopy theory, with foundational results that helped establish chromatic homotopy theory as a dominant framework. Solved the Sullivan conjecture on maps from classifying spaces of finite p-groups, opening homotopy-theoretic generalizations of Lie-group theory; with Mike Hopkins lifted deformation theory and elliptic moduli into stable homotopy. Methods are pure algebraic topology: spectral sequences, formal groups, and the Adams-Novikov machinery.

heather-j-kulik-chemistry.s: Computational chemist developing electronic-structure methods and machine-learning workflows for the discovery of transition-metal complexes, metal-organic frameworks, and metalloenzymes. Builds neural-network and graph-based models trained on high-throughput DFT data to predict spin state, redox potential, and catalytic activity, and develops corrections (DFT+U, range-separation) for open-shell systems. Applications include single-atom catalysts, MOFs for gas separation/CO2 capture, and design of artificial metalloenzymes.

heidi-nepf-cee.s: Environmental fluid mechanician studying how aquatic vegetation — seagrasses, salt marsh, mangroves, kelp — interacts with currents, waves, sediment transport, and nutrient/contaminant fluxes in coastal and riverine ecosystems. Combines laboratory flume experiments with arrays of model canopies, particle image velocimetry, and field campaigns to quantify drag, vortex shedding, and shear-layer dynamics, then develops scaling laws used in coastal-protection and stream-restoration design. Work supports management of blue-carbon habitats.

hernandez-moura-silva-biology.s: Studies how tissue-resident macrophages and other immune cells maintain organ homeostasis and physiology, beyond their canonical roles in pathogen defense. Focuses on macrophage subsets in adipose tissue, gut, and lung, characterizing their molecular circuits via single-cell RNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, conditional mouse genetics, and metabolic flux assays. Goal is to identify immune-mediated pathways that can be tuned to treat metabolic, inflammatory, and tissue-degenerative disease.

hong-liu-physics.s: Theoretical physicist applying string-theoretic and holographic-duality methods (AdS/CFT, gauge/gravity duality) to study quantum gravity, the quantum nature of black holes, and strongly-coupled quantum matter. Uses holography to compute transport coefficients of the quark-gluon plasma, non-Fermi-liquid behavior in strongly correlated electron systems, hydrodynamics, entanglement entropy, and out-of-equilibrium thermalization. Methods are pure analytic high-energy theory: classical and supergravity solutions, conformal field theory, large-N gauge theory.

hugh-herr-mcgovern.s: Develops bionic limbs and exoskeletons that interface with the human nervous system to restore natural movement and proprioception in people with limb amputation, stroke, or paralysis. Co-invented the powered ankle-foot prosthesis (commercialized via iWalk/BiOM) and the Agonist-Antagonist Myoneural Interface (AMI), a surgical procedure that preserves muscle pairs at amputation sites so that patients can both control and feel their bionic limbs. Methods combine mechatronic actuator design, surgical innovation, neural interfacing, and biomechanical modeling of human gait.

iain-hunter-meche.s: Develops novel scientific instruments and biomedical devices, including needle-free jet drug injectors driven by Lorentz-force motors, miniature confocal and Raman microscopes, miniaturized mass spectrometers, soft robotic actuators, and conducting-polymer artificial-muscle systems. Methods span precision mechatronics, electromagnetics, control systems, and electroactive polymer chemistry. Holds 150+ patents and has founded 25+ companies translating instrumentation research.

ian-w-hunter-meche.s: Develops novel scientific instruments and biomedical devices, including needle-free jet drug injectors driven by Lorentz-force motors, miniature confocal and Raman microscopes, miniaturized mass spectrometers, soft robotic actuators, and conducting-polymer artificial-muscle systems. Methods span precision mechatronics, electromagnetics, control systems, and electroactive polymer chemistry. Holds 150+ patents and has founded 25+ companies translating instrumentation research.

iain-m-cheeseman-biology.s: Studies the molecular machinery of cell division, especially the kinetochore — the multi-protein structure (~100 proteins) that links chromosomes to spindle microtubules and ensures faithful chromosome segregation. Identified the Dam1 complex (yeast) and the KMN network (metazoans), the conserved core microtubule-binding interface, and characterizes how kinetochore composition is rewired across cell types and physiological states. Methods combine biochemistry of large protein complexes, cryo-EM, fluorescence microscopy of mitotic cells, and CRISPR knock-in/AID-degron tagging.

iain-w-stewart-physics.s: Theoretical particle and nuclear physicist, co-inventor of the Soft-Collinear Effective Theory (SCET) — an effective field theory that systematically separates soft and collinear modes in high-energy processes involving energetic quarks and gluons. SCET enables precision calculations of jet cross-sections, B-meson decays, quarkonium production, and event-shape variables for the LHC, B-factories, and the Electron-Ion Collider. Director of the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics. Methods are perturbative QCD, factorization theorems, and renormalization-group resummation.

ian-r-wickersham-mcgovern.s: Engineers viral vectors — especially modified rabies viruses — to enable monosynaptic circuit tracing, long-term in vivo labeling, and gene delivery in defined classes of neurons. Pioneered the original ΔG-rabies monosynaptic tracer that lights up cells one synapse upstream of a starter neuron, and develops second/third-generation non-toxic, replication-deficient rabies vectors that allow chronic imaging and functional manipulation. Methods include molecular cloning of rhabdovirus genomes, AAV helper vectors, and combinatorial gene-cassette design.

ila-fiete-bcs.s: Theoretical and computational neuroscientist studying how neural circuits represent, store, and manipulate information, with foundational work on grid cells in the entorhinal cortex as a high-capacity residue-number-system code for spatial position and memory. Builds attractor-network and continuous-attractor models of head direction, integrator circuits, and working memory, and analyses neural recordings to test these models. Recent work explores learning rules, memory consolidation, and parallels between neural and machine-learning self-supervised representation learning.

irmgard-bischofberger-meche.s: Experimental soft-matter and fluid-dynamics researcher studying pattern formation, instabilities, and rheology of complex fluids, colloidal gels, and porous-media flows. Investigates Saffman-Taylor viscous fingering at miscible/immiscible interfaces, drying-induced cracks and patterns, and the mechanics of attractive colloidal suspensions and hydrogels. Methods combine microfluidics, PIV/PTV, rheometry, and high-speed optical imaging.

isaac-l-chuang-physics.s: Experimental quantum-information scientist who built some of the earliest few-qubit quantum computers — first using liquid-state NMR (factoring 15 with Shor's algorithm in a 7-qubit system) and later trapped-ion systems on chip-scale ion traps. Current work develops scalable surface ion traps, integrated photonic interconnects, sympathetic cooling, and quantum-error-correction primitives. Co-author with Michael Nielsen of the standard textbook Quantum Computation and Quantum Information.

iván-werning-economics.s: Macroeconomic theorist working on optimal taxation and fiscal policy, monetary policy, unemployment insurance, and the dynamics of business cycles, inflation, and inequality. Has produced influential streamlined proofs and theorems on tax smoothing, optimal capital and inheritance taxation, and Pareto-efficient income taxation, often with co-author Emmanuel Farhi. Methods are dynamic mechanism design, recursive contracts, and continuous-time stochastic optimal control.

iwnetim-abate-dmse.s: Develops sustainable materials and electrochemical systems for energy storage, computing, and resource extraction, integrating electrochemistry, condensed-matter physics, and earth science. Designs Earth-abundant Na-ion battery cathodes (Mn/Fe-based layered oxides) as alternatives to Li/Co/Ni chemistries, ion-insertion-tuned electronic and magnetic states for neuromorphic computing, and methods for stimulating geologic-hydrogen production from iron-rich subsurface rocks. Methods include in-situ X-ray and neutron scattering, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and DFT calculations.

j-a-formaggio-physics.s: Experimental neutrino physicist measuring the absolute mass of the electron neutrino through precision tritium beta-decay endpoint spectroscopy. Member of the KATRIN experiment (Karlsruhe), which set the current direct upper limit on the neutrino mass at 0.8 eV using magnetic adiabatic collimation with electrostatic filtering, and a leader of Project 8, which uses cyclotron radiation emission spectroscopy (CRES) — measuring the cyclotron frequency of single beta-decay electrons in a magnetic trap — to push toward sub-eV sensitivity. Also works on coherent neutrino-nucleus scattering.

j-chen-biology.s: Immunologist studying T-cell, NK-cell, and macrophage biology with applications to cancer immunotherapy and infectious-disease immunity. Develops chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) NK-cell platforms for adoptive cancer therapy, reprograms tumor-associated macrophages to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, and uses humanized mouse models to study influenza and SARS-CoV-2 host-pathogen interactions. Methods combine mouse genetics, CRISPR cellular engineering, single-cell RNA-seq, and in vivo tumor models.

j-g-checkelsky-physics.s: The Checkelsky Group studies the synthesis and electronic properties of quantum materials, including topological semimetals, kagome lattice metals, and moiré superlattices. The lab grows single crystals and thin films via flux, vapor-transport, and MBE methods, and probes them with magnetotransport, Hall and Nernst effect measurements, torque magnetometry, and quantum oscillations. Targets include flat-band ferromagnets (Co3Sn2S2), Weyl/Dirac semimetals, and frustrated magnets exhibiting anomalous Hall and chiral spin states.

j-j-thaler-physics.s: Jesse Thaler is a theoretical particle physicist at the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics and director of the NSF AI Institute for Artificial Intelligence and Fundamental Interactions (IAIFI). His group develops jet substructure observables (e.g., N-subjettiness, soft drop) and applies machine learning — including equivariant neural networks, normalizing flows, and optimal-transport methods — to LHC collider data. Research spans QCD, effective field theories, dark matter model building, and the interface between theoretical physics and ML.

j-m-conrad-physics.s: Janet Conrad's group at the MIT Laboratory for Nuclear Science searches for sterile neutrinos and studies neutrino oscillations and cross sections. The group is a leading collaborator on MicroBooNE, MiniBooNE, and the Short-Baseline Neutrino Program at Fermilab, and contributes to IceCube sterile-neutrino searches. They build liquid-argon TPC and Cherenkov detector hardware and develop reconstruction techniques for low-energy neutrino interactions.

j-m-stark-bioe.s: The Stark Lab at MIT BioE and the Koch Institute develops cell-free synthetic biology platforms for glycoscience and point-of-care diagnostics. They engineer paper-based BioBits kits, lectin-based biosensors, and glycan-binding protein arrays to detect cancer-associated glycosylation changes and infectious disease biomarkers. Methods include cell-free protein synthesis, lectin engineering, glycan microarrays, and lateral-flow assay design.

j-n-hewitt-physics.s: Jacqueline Hewitt is a radio astronomer studying the Epoch of Reionization, gravitational lensing, and transient radio sources. She leads MIT's involvement in the Murchison Widefield Array (MWA) and the Hydrogen Epoch of Reionization Array (HERA), using low-frequency radio interferometry to detect the redshifted 21 cm hydrogen line from the early universe. Methods include power-spectrum analysis, foreground subtraction, and calibration of dense radio arrays.

j-stubbe-chemistry.s: JoAnne Stubbe (emerita, MIT Chemistry & Biology) is best known for elucidating the radical mechanism of ribonucleotide reductases (RNRs), the enzymes that convert RNA to DNA precursors. Her lab dissected proton-coupled electron transfer over 35 Å in class I RNRs, characterized the diferric–tyrosyl radical cofactor, and studied mechanism-based inhibitors used as cancer chemotherapeutics (e.g., gemcitabine). Methods include EPR spectroscopy, rapid-quench kinetics, site-specific unnatural amino acid incorporation, and X-ray crystallography.

j-w-belcher-physics.s: John Belcher is a space plasma physicist who served as principal investigator for the Voyager 1 and 2 Plasma Science Experiment (PLS), characterizing the solar wind and the plasma environments of Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. He co-discovered Alfvén waves in the solar wind and has analyzed Voyager's observations of the heliosheath and termination shock. He is also widely known at MIT for the TEAL (Technology Enabled Active Learning) project for introductory electromagnetism.

j-zhao-cee.s: Jinhua Zhao directs the JTL Urban Mobility Lab in MIT CEE/DUSP, studying travel behavior, public transit, and the integration of autonomous vehicles and shared mobility into cities. The lab uses discrete choice modeling, smart-card and GPS trace analysis, large-scale stated-preference surveys, and machine learning on mobility data from systems like the MBTA, Beijing, and Singapore. Research themes include mode choice, equity, behavioral interventions (nudges), and mobility-as-a-service.

jacob-andreas-eecs.s: Jacob Andreas's group in MIT CSAIL studies natural language processing, language grounding, and the use of language to structure learning in neural models. Projects include neural module networks for compositional reasoning, probing and interpretability of large language models, language-conditioned reinforcement learning, and learning from natural-language instructions and explanations. Methods span transformer architectures, program synthesis, RL, and behavioral/probing experiments on LLMs.

jacob-moscona-economics.s: Jacob Moscona is a development and environmental economist whose research examines agricultural innovation, the directed nature of technical change, and adaptation to climate change. He uses historical and contemporary datasets — including crop genetics, pesticide use, and patent records — combined with quasi-experimental and instrumental variable methods to estimate how environmental shocks shape innovation and global agricultural productivity. Recent work covers the directed evolution of crop varieties, antibiotic resistance, and the political economy of innovation.

jacopo-borga-math.s: Jacopo Borga is a probabilist in the MIT Mathematics department working on random permutations, permutons, and their connections to random planar maps and Liouville quantum gravity. His work establishes scaling limits of pattern-avoiding and uniform random permutations, characterizes Brownian-type permuton limits, and connects random sorting networks to SLE/LQG. Methods include combinatorial bijections, martingale arguments, and stochastic analysis on random surfaces.

jacopo-buongiorno-nse.s: Jacopo Buongiorno directs the Center for Advanced Nuclear Energy Systems (CANES) and studies thermal-hydraulics, nanofluids, and advanced reactor design in MIT NSE. His group investigates critical heat flux enhancement using engineered surfaces and nanoparticle suspensions, performs high-pressure two-phase flow experiments, and analyzes economics and safety of small modular reactors and offshore floating nuclear plants. Methods include high-resolution infrared thermometry, CFD, and Monte Carlo neutronics.

jacqueline-a-lees-biology.s: Jacqueline Lees's lab at the Koch Institute studies the retinoblastoma (Rb) tumor suppressor pathway and the E2F family of transcription factors that control cell cycle entry, apoptosis, and differentiation. Using mouse genetic models, the lab dissects how Rb/E2F dysregulation drives osteosarcoma, retinoblastoma, and intestinal cancers, and how E2Fs intersect with metabolism and stem-cell fate. Methods include conditional knockouts, ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, and patient-derived organoid models.

jacquin-c-niles-bioe.s: The Niles Lab in MIT BioE develops genetic and chemical tools to study Plasmodium parasites and combat malaria. They built TetR-aptamer-based conditional knockdown systems for Plasmodium falciparum, perform CRISPR/Cas9 editing of asexual blood stages and gametocytes, and use these tools to identify essential parasite genes and antimalarial drug targets. Methods include parasite culture, RNA aptamer engineering, flow cytometry, and chemical genetics screens.

jahandar-ramezani-eaps.s: Jahandar Ramezani is a research scientist in MIT EAPS who runs the Bowring Isotope Lab and applies high-precision U-Pb zircon geochronology (ID-TIMS, CA-TIMS) to calibrate Earth history. His work has tightened the timing of the end-Permian and end-Triassic mass extinctions, the Siberian Traps and CAMP large igneous provinces, the rise of dinosaurs, and the assembly of the Sierra Nevada batholith. Methods include single-crystal zircon chemical abrasion, isotope dilution thermal ionization mass spectrometry, and integration of radiometric dates with chemostratigraphic and paleontological records.

james-j-collins-bioe.s: Jim Collins is a founder of synthetic biology whose lab at MIT BioE/IMES, the Broad, and the Wyss Institute engineers programmable genetic circuits and applies machine learning to antibiotic discovery. The group built the original genetic toggle switch, RNA toehold switches, paper-based cell-free diagnostics (deployed for Zika and SARS-CoV-2), and used deep learning to discover halicin and other broad-spectrum antibiotics. Methods include circuit design with sigma factors and CRISPR-based regulators, cell-free protein synthesis on paper, and graph neural networks trained on chemical screening libraries.

james-m-poterba-economics.s: James Poterba is the Mitsui Professor of Economics at MIT and President of the National Bureau of Economic Research, working in public finance and household finance. His research uses tax-return microdata, household surveys (HRS, SCF), and reduced-form and structural empirical methods to study how taxes affect saving behavior, retirement asset decumulation, capital gains realizations, and corporate investment. He has long directed NBER's Public Economics program and edits the annual Tax Policy and the Economy volume.

jean-jacques-slotine-meche.s: Jean-Jacques Slotine directs the Nonlinear Systems Laboratory in MIT MechE, BCS, and IDSS, studying nonlinear control, adaptive control, and contraction theory and their application to robotics, computational neuroscience, and machine learning. The lab develops sliding-mode and adaptive controllers for underwater and aerial robots, uses contraction analysis to prove stability of large coupled networks, and studies synchronization in oscillator and neuron populations. Slotine co-authored the standard graduate textbooks Robot Analysis and Control and Applied Nonlinear Control.

jeehwan-kim-meche.s: Jeehwan Kim's group at MIT MechE/DMSE develops 2D-material electronics, remote epitaxy, and neuromorphic computing devices. They invented remote epitaxy of single-crystal III–V and oxide films through monolayer graphene for layer-transfer of electronic devices, fabricated SiGe-based artificial synapses with engineered dislocations, and built brain-inspired memristor crossbar arrays for analog AI inference. Methods span MOCVD, transmission electron microscopy, and integration of crystalline membranes onto flexible substrates.

jeff-gore-physics.l: Gore Laboratory for Ecological Systems Biology
jeff-gore-physics.s: Jeff Gore's biophysics lab in MIT Physics uses laboratory microbial communities (yeast, soil bacteria) as tractable model systems for population dynamics, evolutionary game theory, and community ecology. Experiments probe early-warning signs of population collapse, alternative stable states, cooperator–cheater dynamics in public-goods scenarios, and assembly rules of bottom-up assembled multispecies microcosms. Methods include automated chemostat and droplet culture, flow cytometry, fluorescent strain tracking, and stochastic dynamical-systems modeling.

jeffrey-c-grossman-dmse.s: Jeffrey Grossman's group in MIT DMSE uses density functional theory, molecular dynamics, and Monte Carlo simulations together with experiment to design materials for energy conversion, energy storage, separations, and clean water. Notable projects include molecular solar thermal fuels (azobenzene-based heat storage), nanoporous graphene desalination membranes, conversion of coal to electronic-grade carbon, and computationally guided design of thermoelectrics and photovoltaics. Methods include first-principles DFT, ab initio MD, machine-learning-accelerated screening, and lab synthesis of polymer and 2D-material membranes.

jeffrey-i-steinfeld-chemistry.s: Jeffrey I. Steinfeld is a professor of chemistry at MIT (now emeritus-active) whose research used time-resolved laser spectroscopy to obtain rate constants and energy-transfer data for gas-phase molecules. His lab studied infrared multiphoton excitation, vibrational energy redistribution, and the reaction kinetics of atmospheric and combustion-relevant species, and his later work shifted toward Earth-system science and climate education. He authored the widely used textbooks Molecules and Radiation: An Introduction to Modern Molecular Spectroscopy and Chemical Kinetics and Dynamics.

jeremiah-a-johnson-chemistry.s: Jeremiah Johnson's group at MIT Chemistry, the Koch Institute, and the Broad develops new polymer architectures for medicine and materials, especially bottlebrush and polymer-network systems. Projects include bottlebrush prodrug platforms (BASP/BAM) that carry multiple cancer drugs to tumors, light-switchable iCVD polymer networks, MOF-polymer composites, and metal–organic cages for catalysis and drug delivery. Methods rely on ROMP and ATRP polymerization, click chemistry, photoredox catalysis, and small-angle X-ray scattering for network characterization.

jesse-h-kroll-cee.s: Jesse Kroll's group in MIT CEE/ChemE studies the chemistry of organic compounds in the atmosphere and their role in air pollution and climate. They oxidize volatile organic compounds in environmental chambers and flow tubes (using OH, ozone, NO3, and chlorine radicals) to track the formation of secondary organic aerosol (SOA), and they participate in field campaigns measuring biomass-burning emissions, urban air, and forested regions. Methods include high-resolution chemical ionization mass spectrometry (CIMS), aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS), and oxidation flow reactors.

jesse-thaler-physics.s: Jesse Thaler is a theoretical particle physicist at the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics and director of the NSF AI Institute for Artificial Intelligence and Fundamental Interactions (IAIFI). His group develops jet substructure observables (e.g., N-subjettiness, soft drop) and applies machine learning — including equivariant neural networks, normalizing flows, and optimal-transport methods — to LHC collider data. Research spans QCD, effective field theories, dark matter model building, and the interface between theoretical physics and ML.
jesse-thaler-physics.notes: Duplicate of j-j-thaler-physics — same person (Jesse Thaler). Logged in duplicates.txt.

jessika-e-trancik-idss.s: Jessika Trancik leads the Trancik Lab in MIT IDSS, studying the dynamic costs, performance, and environmental impacts of energy technologies to inform climate policy. The group quantifies experience curves for solar PV, wind, batteries, and electrolyzers; models electric-vehicle charging needs and lifetime emissions; and builds open tools (e.g., carboncounter.com) comparing vehicle costs and emissions. Methods combine techno-economic modeling, patent and publication data mining, life-cycle assessment, and time-series analysis of grid and mobility data.

jianshu-cao-chemistry.s: Jianshu Cao's theoretical chemistry group at MIT develops methods for condensed-phase quantum dynamics and applies them to photosynthetic light harvesting, organic semiconductors, molecular junctions, and quantum heat engines. Methods include centroid molecular dynamics, path-integral Monte Carlo, the transfer-tensor method, polaron transformations, and master-equation treatments of energy and charge transfer in open quantum systems. The group also works on non-equilibrium statistical mechanics of biochemical networks and single-molecule kinetics.

jianzhu-chen-biology.s: Immunologist studying T-cell, NK-cell, and macrophage biology with applications to cancer immunotherapy and infectious-disease immunity. Develops chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) NK-cell platforms for adoptive cancer therapy, reprograms tumor-associated macrophages to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, and uses humanized mouse models to study influenza and SARS-CoV-2 host-pathogen interactions. Methods combine mouse genetics, CRISPR cellular engineering, single-cell RNA-seq, and in vivo tumor models.
jianzhu-chen-biology.notes: Duplicate of j-chen-biology — same person (Jianzhu Chen). Logged in duplicates.txt.

jing-kong-eecs.s: Jing Kong's group in MIT EECS/RLE develops chemical vapor deposition (CVD) synthesis and characterization of carbon nanotubes, graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides (MoS2, WSe2), and hexagonal boron nitride. They engineer CVD reactor kinetics to grow wafer-scale 2D crystals, transfer them onto arbitrary substrates, and build field-effect transistors, photodetectors, and flexible electronics from the resulting films. Methods include Raman spectroscopy, XPS, TEM, and device-level electrical transport measurements.

jinghua-zhao-cee.s: Jinhua Zhao directs the JTL Urban Mobility Lab in MIT CEE/DUSP, studying travel behavior, public transit, and the integration of autonomous vehicles and shared mobility into cities. The lab uses discrete choice modeling, smart-card and GPS trace analysis, large-scale stated-preference surveys, and machine learning on mobility data from systems like the MBTA, Beijing, and Singapore. Research themes include mode choice, equity, behavioral interventions (nudges), and mobility-as-a-service.
jinghua-zhao-cee.notes: Duplicate of j-zhao-cee — same person (Jinhua Zhao). Logged in duplicates.txt.

joanne-stubbe-chemistry.s: JoAnne Stubbe (emerita, MIT Chemistry & Biology) is best known for elucidating the radical mechanism of ribonucleotide reductases (RNRs), the enzymes that convert RNA to DNA precursors. Her lab dissected proton-coupled electron transfer over 35 Å in class I RNRs, characterized the diferric–tyrosyl radical cofactor, and studied mechanism-based inhibitors used as cancer chemotherapeutics (e.g., gemcitabine). Methods include EPR spectroscopy, rapid-quench kinetics, site-specific unnatural amino acid incorporation, and X-ray crystallography.
joanne-stubbe-chemistry.notes: Duplicate of j-stubbe-chemistry — same person (JoAnne Stubbe). Logged in duplicates.txt.

john-d-e-gabrieli-bcs.s: John Gabrieli's lab in MIT BCS and the McGovern Institute studies the neural bases of human memory, learning, emotion regulation, and neurodevelopmental/psychiatric disorders. They use structural and functional MRI, diffusion tensor imaging, and behavioral testing to identify biomarkers for dyslexia, ADHD, autism, and depression, and to predict individual responses to educational and clinical interventions. Gabrieli also directs the Martinos Imaging Center at McGovern and the MIT Integrated Learning Initiative (MITili).

john-h-lienhard-meche.s: John H. Lienhard V directs the Lienhard Research Group in MIT MechE, focused on energy-efficient water purification, desalination, and thermal-fluid sciences. The group studies humidification-dehumidification (HDH) desalination, membrane distillation, reverse-osmosis fouling and scaling, electrodialysis, nanofiltration, zero-liquid-discharge brine management, and solar-thermal desalination. Methods include bench-scale thermodynamic cycle experiments, computational heat and mass transfer modeling, and field deployment in India through the Tata Center and J-WAFS.

john-j-leonard-meche.s: John Leonard's Marine Robotics Group at MIT MechE/CSAIL pioneered simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) for autonomous underwater, ground, and aerial robots. The group develops factor-graph-based SLAM (iSAM, GTSAM), object-level and semantic SLAM, lifelong mapping in dynamic environments, and multi-robot cooperative navigation using sonar, LiDAR, and vision. Platforms include AUVs and self-driving car research done in collaboration with Toyota and Oxford.

john-m-essigmann-chemistry.s: John Essigmann's lab at MIT Chemistry and BioE studies how structurally defined DNA lesions formed by environmental toxins, tobacco carcinogens, and chemotherapeutic drugs cause mutation, cell death, and cancer. Using site-specifically modified oligonucleotides introduced into bacterial and mammalian shuttle vectors, they quantify lesion bypass, mutagenic fingerprints (e.g., G→T transversions from aflatoxin B1), and interactions with DNA repair pathways. The lab also designs "lethal mutagenesis" antiviral drugs that accelerate viral error catastrophe.

john-n-tsitsiklis-eecs.s: John Tsitsiklis is the Clarence J. Lebel Professor in EECS and LIDS, working on stochastic systems, optimization, reinforcement learning theory, and computational complexity of decision problems. He and Dimitri Bertsekas coauthored the foundational textbook Neuro-Dynamic Programming, which laid out the theory of function approximation in reinforcement learning; his paper on asynchronous stochastic approximation gave the convergence proof for Q-learning. Other work covers distributed consensus, queueing network analysis, and statistical learning.

john-w-m-bush-math.s: John W. M. Bush directs the Applied Math Laboratory in MIT Mathematics, studying surface-tension-driven flows, biological fluid mechanics, and hydrodynamic quantum analogues. His group has shown that millimetric droplets bouncing on a vibrating silicone-oil bath exhibit pilot-wave dynamics that reproduce quantum phenomena such as tunneling, single-particle diffraction, and quantized orbits, and has studied insect locomotion on water, water strider jumping, and respiratory disease transmission (fluid dynamics of COVID-19 airborne transmission). Methods include high-speed imaging, interfacial flow experiments, and asymptotic/PDE modeling.

jonathan-gruber-economics.s: Jonathan Gruber is the Ford Professor of Economics at MIT and chair of the MIT Economics Department, working in public finance and health economics. His research uses tax return data, insurance claims, and quasi-experimental evaluation of tax and transfer programs to study Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, employer-sponsored insurance, and smoking and obesity taxes. He was an architect of the 2006 Massachusetts health reform and the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate and exchange design, and directed the NBER Health Care program.

jonathan-p-how-lids.s: Jonathan How directs the Aerospace Controls Laboratory (ACL) in MIT AeroAstro/LIDS, developing decision-making, planning, and control algorithms for autonomous aircraft, spacecraft, and ground vehicles under uncertainty. The group builds distributed task-assignment algorithms for multi-UAV teams (Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm), robust MPC and MADER collision avoidance for drones, deep RL for multi-agent policies, and estimation/navigation for GPS-denied flight. Work spans UAV swarms, spacecraft rendezvous, and autonomous driving under uncertainty.

jonathan-s-weissman-biology.s: Jonathan Weissman's lab at the Whitehead Institute and MIT Biology studies protein folding, translation, and genome organization using large-scale functional genomics tools that the lab invented or scaled. They developed ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq) for genome-wide measurement of translation, CRISPRi and CRISPRa for programmable gene repression/activation with dCas9, Perturb-seq for pooled CRISPR screens read out by single-cell RNA-seq, and DNA Typewriter/ENGRAM lineage recorders. Applications include quality control of membrane and mitochondrial proteins, cancer dependencies, and neurodegeneration.

joseph-casamento-dmse.s: Joseph Casamento (assistant professor, MIT DMSE, joined 2024) studies nitride and related wide-bandgap semiconductors and ferroelectrics for RF electronics, power devices, and acoustic resonators. His work uses molecular beam epitaxy and metal-organic CVD to grow group-III nitride heterostructures (AlN, ScAlN, GaN) and explores ferroelectric nitrides for non-volatile memory and neuromorphic computing. Methods include thin-film epitaxy, X-ray diffraction, piezoresponse force microscopy, and device fabrication and testing.

joseph-h-davis-biology.s: Joey Davis's lab in MIT Biology uses cryo-electron microscopy, cryo-electron tomography, and quantitative mass spectrometry to dissect how macromolecular machines — especially bacterial and eukaryotic ribosomes — are assembled, regulated, and degraded. They developed cryoDRGN, a deep-learning method for heterogeneous cryo-EM reconstruction, and cryoPRISM to resolve dozens of ribosomal intermediates in situ. Other projects include the autophagy machinery and selective protein degradation pathways in E. coli and human cells.

joshua-b-tenenbaum-bcs.s: Josh Tenenbaum's CoCoSci group in MIT BCS and CSAIL builds computational models of human perception, learning, and common-sense reasoning and uses them to design more human-like AI. The group develops probabilistic generative models, probabilistic programming languages (Church, Gen), Bayesian program learning, and neuro-symbolic "game engines in the head" for intuitive physics and intuitive psychology. Methods combine behavioral experiments in adults and children with Monte Carlo inference and program synthesis.

joshua-d-angrist-economics.s: Joshua Angrist is the Ford Professor of Economics at MIT and shared the 2021 Nobel Memorial Prize in Economics for methodological contributions to the analysis of causal relationships. His work pioneered the use of instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and other quasi-experimental designs in labor and education economics — including the returns-to-schooling identification using quarter-of-birth instruments and the local average treatment effect (LATE) framework with Guido Imbens. He co-directs MIT Blueprint Labs, which applies these methods to K-12 school choice, charter schools, and health care access.

julien-tailleur-physics.s: Julien Tailleur's group in MIT Physics develops non-equilibrium statistical mechanics for active matter — systems of self-propelled units such as bacterial swarms, molecular motors, Janus colloids, and animal flocks. The group is known for discovering motility-induced phase separation (MIPS), showing that purely repulsive active particles can spontaneously condense despite having no attractive interactions. Methods include stochastic differential equations, field theory, large-deviation theory, and agent-based simulations of run-and-tumble and active Brownian particle models.

jun-li-nse.s: Ju Li is the Battelle Energy Alliance Professor of Nuclear Science and Engineering and Professor of Materials Science and Engineering at MIT, leading a group that studies the mechanical, electrochemical, and radiation behavior of materials. They develop new structural materials for fission and fusion reactors, use in situ TEM nanomechanical testing to observe dislocation and twinning mechanisms under stress, and design elastic-strain-engineered and "material by design" systems including Li-ion batteries, hydrogen storage, and CO2 capture sorbents. Methods combine first-principles DFT, molecular dynamics, and high-resolution electron microscopy.

k-cahoy-eaps.l: STAR Lab (Space Telecommunications, Astronomy, and Radiation)
k-cahoy-eaps.s: Kerri Cahoy is a full professor of AeroAstro at MIT with a joint appointment in EAPS, and leads the STAR Lab developing small satellites and CubeSats for weather sensing, free-space laser communications, and exoplanet detection. Her group has flown MicroMAS (microwave atmospheric sounder), DeMi (deformable-mirror CubeSat for coronagraph testing), CLICK (CubeSat Laser Infrared CrosslinK), and AEROS (ocean observation), and develops MEMS deformable mirrors and wavefront control for high-contrast exoplanet imaging. Methods span spacecraft bus design, optical communications hardware, GPS radio occultation, and atmospheric remote sensing.
k-cahoy-eaps.notes: Duplicate of kerri-cahoy-eaps — same person. Name corrected from 'K. Cahoy' to 'Kerri Cahoy'. Logged in duplicates.txt.

k-dane-wittrup-bioe.s: Dane Wittrup's lab at MIT BioE/ChemE and the Koch Institute develops protein engineering tools for cancer immunotherapy, including directed evolution of antibodies, cytokines, and receptors by yeast surface display. The group pioneered quantitative methods for measuring antibody affinity and specificity, engineered long-acting cytokine fusions (IL-2, IL-12) for anti-tumor responses, and designed antibody-drug conjugates and bispecifics to enhance tumor targeting. Methods include flow-cytometric library screening, pharmacokinetic modeling, and syngeneic mouse tumor models.

karl-k-berggren-eecs.s: Karl Berggren leads the Quantum Nanostructures and Nanofabrication Group (QNN) in MIT EECS/RLE, developing nanofabrication at the few-nanometer length scale and applying it to superconducting electronics, quantum photonics, and electron optics. The group is a leading developer of superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) with low jitter, picosecond timing, and near-unity efficiency, used in quantum communication, deep-space laser links, and biological imaging. Other projects include superconducting nanowire logic (nanocryotrons), electron-beam lithography, and single-electron sources.

kate-e-galloway-koch-institute.s: Kate Galloway's lab in MIT ChemE (with ties to the Koch Institute) integrates synthetic genetic circuits with mammalian cell biology to study and control cellular reprogramming — especially direct conversion between differentiated states, stem-cell fate transitions, and cancer-related plasticity. Methods include viral-vector engineering, synthetic transcription factors, single-cell RNA-seq, live-cell imaging, and mathematical modeling of gene regulatory network dynamics. Applications target programmable cell therapies and regenerative medicine.

katharina-ribbeck-bioe.s: Katharina Ribbeck's lab in MIT BioE studies mucus as an active biomaterial, dissecting how mucins — the heavily glycosylated polymers that form mucus — regulate microbial behavior and protect against infection. They have shown that mucus suppresses biofilm formation and virulence in Candida albicans, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Vibrio cholerae, identified cervical mucus properties that predict preterm birth, and engineered synthetic mucins for therapeutic and cosmetic applications. Methods include rheology, particle tracking microrheology, glycan analysis, and microbial growth assays.

keith-a-nelson-chemistry.s: Keith Nelson's group in MIT Chemistry develops coherent nonlinear optical and terahertz spectroscopies and uses them to study collective excitations in complex materials. They pioneered impulsive stimulated Raman scattering (ISRS) as a way to generate tunable narrowband THz radiation and two-dimensional terahertz magnetic resonance, and they apply intense THz fields to drive phase transitions in ferroelectrics, metal-insulator transitions, and coherent magnon control in antiferromagnets. Methods include amplified Ti:sapphire laser systems, lithium niobate tilted-pulse-front THz generation, and 2D spectroscopy.

kerri-cahoy-eaps.l: STAR Lab (Space Telecommunications, Astronomy, and Radiation)
kerri-cahoy-eaps.s: Kerri Cahoy is a full professor of AeroAstro at MIT with a joint appointment in EAPS, and leads the STAR Lab developing small satellites and CubeSats for weather sensing, free-space laser communications, and exoplanet detection. Her group has flown MicroMAS (microwave atmospheric sounder), DeMi (deformable-mirror CubeSat for coronagraph testing), CLICK (CubeSat Laser Infrared CrosslinK), and AEROS (ocean observation), and develops MEMS deformable mirrors and wavefront control for high-contrast exoplanet imaging. Methods span spacecraft bus design, optical communications hardware, GPS radio occultation, and atmospheric remote sensing.

kevin-b-burdge-physics.s: Kevin Burdge is an observational astrophysicist in MIT Physics/Kavli Institute searching for ultracompact binary systems — pairs of white dwarfs, neutron stars, and black holes — as electromagnetic counterparts to LISA gravitational-wave sources. Using wide-field optical time-domain surveys including the Zwicky Transient Facility, TESS, and the upcoming Vera Rubin Observatory, he has discovered the shortest-period eclipsing binaries known and measured general-relativistic orbital decay. Methods include high-cadence photometry, difference imaging, and multi-wavelength follow-up on Keck, Gemini, and Chandra.

koroush-shirvan-nse.s: Koroush Shirvan's group in MIT NSE develops accident-tolerant fuels, advanced reactor designs, and high-temperature structural materials for next-generation light-water and non-LWR reactors. Projects include silicon carbide–clad uranium fuels that can survive Fukushima-style loss-of-coolant heat-up, Cr-coated zircaloy cladding, FCM TRISO-based fuels for micro-reactors, and thermal-hydraulic and economic analyses of small modular reactors. Methods combine out-of-pile corrosion testing, CFD, Monte Carlo neutronics (MCNP/OpenMC), and nuclear fuel performance codes (BISON).

kripa-k-varanasi-meche.s: Kripa Varanasi's group in MIT MechE develops nanoengineered surfaces and coatings that manipulate wetting, adhesion, phase change, and fouling at interfaces. They invented LiquiGlide (slippery liquid-infused porous surfaces) that enables complete release of viscous liquids from containers, developed superhydrophobic and icephobic coatings for power generation and aviation, and demonstrated charged-droplet condensation enhancements and electrostatic spray dispersion reduction for agriculture. Methods include contact-angle and droplet-impact experiments, high-speed imaging, and surface chemistry engineering (SLIPS, hierarchical textures).

krishna-rajagopal-physics.s: Krishna Rajagopal is the William A. M. Burden Professor of Physics at MIT, working on theoretical QCD and the physics of dense nuclear matter and the quark–gluon plasma (QGP). He is known for predicting color-superconducting phases (including color-flavor-locked and crystalline phases) of dense quark matter, and for developing the "hybrid model" used to interpret jet energy loss, quenching, and wake phenomena in heavy-ion collisions at RHIC and the LHC. Methods span gauge/string duality (AdS/CFT), perturbative QCD, relativistic hydrodynamics, and lattice-informed effective theory.

kristin-bergmann-eaps.s: Kristin Bergmann's carbonate research lab in MIT EAPS uses the chemistry and sedimentology of carbonate rocks to reconstruct deep-time ocean chemistry, climate, and the co-evolution of life and environments. The group specializes in clumped-isotope (Δ47) thermometry, bulk and in situ stable isotopes, trace-element proxies, and field sedimentology from Precambrian to Phanerozoic carbonates around the world. Applications include Neoproterozoic snowball-Earth intervals, the rise of animals, and Mesozoic greenhouse climates.

kwanghun-chung-bcs.s: Kwanghun Chung's group at MIT ChemE/IMES and the Picower Institute develops tissue-clearing and expansion technologies for whole-organ molecular, cellular, and connectomic imaging. He co-invented CLARITY (lipid removal via hydrogel embedding), and his lab developed SWITCH, SHIELD, MAP (magnified analysis of proteome), and eFLASH multiplexed immunolabeling to preserve antigens and enable high-throughput proteomic and structural mapping of intact mouse brains, human brain slabs, and other organs. The technologies are paired with light-sheet microscopy and machine-learning image analysis.

l-guarente-biology.l: Glenn Center for Biology of Aging (Guarente Lab)
l-guarente-biology.s: Leonard Guarente is the Novartis Professor of Biology at MIT and director of the Glenn Center for Biology of Aging Research, and is best known for discovering the NAD+-dependent deacetylase activity of sirtuin (SIR2) proteins and their role in lifespan extension. His lab showed that SIR2/SIRT1 activity links caloric restriction to longevity in yeast, worms, and mice, and studies mammalian sirtuins (SIRT1–SIRT7) in metabolism, neurodegeneration, and Alzheimer's disease. Methods include mouse genetics, biochemistry of NAD+ metabolism, and screens for sirtuin activators (STACs).
l-guarente-biology.notes: Duplicate of leonard-guarente-biology — same person (Leonard Guarente). Name fix applied. Logged in duplicates.txt.

leonard-guarente-biology.l: Glenn Center for Biology of Aging (Guarente Lab)
leonard-guarente-biology.s: Leonard Guarente is the Novartis Professor of Biology at MIT and director of the Glenn Center for Biology of Aging Research, and is best known for discovering the NAD+-dependent deacetylase activity of sirtuin (SIR2) proteins and their role in lifespan extension. His lab showed that SIR2/SIRT1 activity links caloric restriction to longevity in yeast, worms, and mice, and studies mammalian sirtuins (SIRT1–SIRT7) in metabolism, neurodegeneration, and Alzheimer's disease. Methods include mouse genetics, biochemistry of NAD+ metabolism, and screens for sirtuin activators (STACs).

l-a-winslow-physics.s: Lindley Winslow leads a neutrino and dark-matter group in MIT Physics/LNS, searching for neutrinoless double-beta decay and axion-like dark matter. She is a core member of the CUORE (Cryogenic Underground Observatory for Rare Events) bolometer experiment at Gran Sasso and the KamLAND-Zen liquid-scintillator experiment, probing whether the neutrino is a Majorana particle. She founded the ABRACADABRA detector, a broadband toroidal-magnet search for axion dark matter.

l-h-royden-eaps.s: Leigh Royden is the Cecil and Ida Green Professor of Geology and Geophysics in MIT EAPS, studying the dynamics of continental collisions, subduction zones, and the coupling of deep mantle processes to surface tectonics. Her influential work on Tibet proposed lower-crustal flow (the "topographic ooze" model) to explain the flat eastern Tibetan Plateau, and her subduction research has quantified slab rollback and trench migration in the Mediterranean and western Pacific. Methods include analytical mechanical modeling, geologic field mapping, and integration of GPS, seismic, and stratigraphic data.

l-ju-physics.s: Long Ju's group in MIT Physics studies light–matter interactions and strongly correlated electron phases in atomically thin van der Waals materials and moiré superlattices. Using gated devices of ABC trilayer graphene, twisted bilayer/trilayer graphene, and transition-metal dichalcogenides, they have observed correlated insulators, fractional Chern insulators (fractional quantum Hall at zero magnetic field), and topological phases. Methods include dry-transfer heterostructure fabrication, infrared/Raman magneto-spectroscopy, and low-temperature electrical transport.

larry-guth-math.s: Larry Guth is the Claude Shannon Professor of Mathematics at MIT, working in metric geometry, harmonic analysis, and extremal combinatorics. He is known for introducing the polynomial method into combinatorial geometry — including (with Nets Katz) the near-complete resolution of the Erdős distinct distances problem — and for deep progress on Kakeya-type inequalities and restriction estimates in Fourier analysis. He also strengthened Gromov's systolic inequality for essential manifolds.

laura-l-kiessling-chemistry.s: Laura Kiessling is the Novartis Professor of Chemistry at MIT and a member of the Koch Institute and the Broad; her lab uses chemical biology to elucidate protein–carbohydrate interactions on the cell surface. They designed multivalent glycan ligands and glycopolymers to probe lectin clustering, developed fluorogenic substrates to image mycobacterial cell-wall biosynthesis, and studied galectins, selectins, and glycan-binding receptors in immunology and embryonic stem cells. Methods include organic synthesis, ring-opening metathesis polymerization, fluorescence microscopy, and glycan arrays.

laura-schulz-bcs.s: Laura Schulz directs the Early Childhood Cognition Lab in MIT BCS, studying how infants and young children build commonsense theories of the physical and social world, reason about causal structure, and plan exploration. Her group runs behavioral experiments with children ages 18 months to 6 years (in lab, in-museum, and online via Children Helping Science and Lookit) and builds Bayesian and probabilistic-program models of how kids select informative experiments, update beliefs, and trade off play against instruction. Research themes include causal learning, curiosity, explanation, and rational constructivism.

laurent-demanet-math.l: Imaging and Computing Group (ICG) / Earth Resources Laboratory
laurent-demanet-math.s: Laurent Demanet is Professor of Applied Mathematics at MIT with a joint appointment in EAPS, where he directs the Earth Resources Laboratory and leads the Imaging and Computing Group. His research addresses inverse problems and wave propagation for seismic imaging — full-waveform inversion, extended-domain migration, sparse reconstruction, and fast numerical methods for the wave equation — with applications to environmental monitoring, CO2 sequestration, and geothermal exploration. Methods draw on harmonic analysis, microlocal analysis, compressed sensing, and machine learning.

laurie-a-boyer-biology.s: Laurie Boyer's lab in MIT Biology and BioE studies the gene regulatory and chromatin mechanisms that control cardiac cell fate, heart development, and congenital heart disease. They discovered Braveheart, the first long non-coding RNA required for early cardiac commitment, and map how ATP-dependent chromatin remodelers, histone modifications, and 3D genome organization direct cardiomyocyte differentiation from human ES and iPS cells. Methods include ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, Hi-C, RNA-seq, and engineered cardiac tissue models.

leonid-a-mirny-physics.l: Mirny Lab (Architecture and Physics of the Genome)
leonid-a-mirny-physics.s: Leonid Mirny's lab at MIT IMES and Physics studies the 3D architecture and dynamics of chromosomes by combining polymer physics, simulations, and Hi-C chromosome-conformation-capture data. The group proposed and developed the loop-extrusion model, showing that SMC motor complexes (cohesin, condensin) actively extrude DNA loops to fold chromosomes and produce features like TADs, CTCF boundaries, and mitotic condensation. Methods include polymer MD simulations, analysis of Hi-C and Micro-C data, and the open-source cooltools and polychrom software.

leonid-levitov-physics.s: Leonid Levitov is a professor in the condensed-matter theory group of MIT Physics who works on quantum transport, mesoscopic physics, and electronic properties of graphene. He introduced full counting statistics of charge transport (the Levitov formula) and predicted atomic collapse, electron hydrodynamics, and topological valley currents in graphene — all subsequently observed experimentally. Other topics include quantum noise, cold-atom systems, solid-state quantum computing, and pattern formation.

leslie-pack-kaelbling-eecs.s: Leslie Pack Kaelbling co-leads the Learning and Intelligent Systems (LIS) group in MIT CSAIL (with Tomás Lozano-Pérez), working on integrated task-and-motion planning, learning, and decision making under uncertainty for robots operating in human environments. Her work pioneered reinforcement learning for embedded control and adapted partially-observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) for AI and robotics, and recent work combines symbolic planning, learned skills, and probabilistic perception for long-horizon manipulation. She founded and was the editor-in-chief of the Journal of Machine Learning Research.

li-huei-tsai-bcs.s: Li-Huei Tsai directs the Picower Institute for Learning and Memory and leads a lab studying the molecular and circuit basis of learning, memory, and neurodegeneration — especially Alzheimer's disease. Her group discovered that 40 Hz "gamma" sensory stimulation (GENUS — Gamma Entrainment Using Sensory Stimuli via flickering light and clicking sound) reduces amyloid and tau pathology and improves cognition in mouse Alzheimer's models, and is now in human clinical trials. Methods include mouse genetics, in vivo electrophysiology, single-cell multiomics, and chromatin studies of CDK5 and HDAC2 in neurons.

lihuei-tsai-bcs.s: Li-Huei Tsai directs the Picower Institute for Learning and Memory and leads a lab studying the molecular and circuit basis of learning, memory, and neurodegeneration — especially Alzheimer's disease. Her group discovered that 40 Hz "gamma" sensory stimulation (GENUS — Gamma Entrainment Using Sensory Stimuli via flickering light and clicking sound) reduces amyloid and tau pathology and improves cognition in mouse Alzheimer's models, and is now in human clinical trials. Methods include mouse genetics, in vivo electrophysiology, single-cell multiomics, and chromatin studies of CDK5 and HDAC2 in neurons.
lihuei-tsai-bcs.notes: Duplicate of li-huei-tsai-bcs — same person (Li-Huei Tsai). Logged in duplicates.txt.

liang-fu-physics.s: Liang Fu is a professor of physics at MIT working on theoretical condensed-matter physics — especially topological phases of matter and their material realizations. He predicted 3D topological insulators (with Charles Kane), topological crystalline insulators in IV-VI semiconductors like SnTe, and odd-parity topological superconductors that host Majorana zero modes. Other work includes proposals for Majorana-based topological quantum computing and theories of correlated phases in moiré graphene.

lina-necib-physics.s: Lina Necib is an assistant professor of physics at MIT Kavli Institute working on theoretical and data-driven astroparticle physics, with a focus on dark matter and the Milky Way. She combines cosmological hydrodynamic simulations (FIRE), Gaia stellar catalogs, and machine learning to reconstruct the local dark matter velocity distribution, identify tidal debris from past mergers, and predict signals in direct-detection experiments. She discovered the Nyx stellar stream and received the 2023 APS Valley Prize.

linda-g-griffith-bioe.s: Linda Griffith directs the MIT Center for Gynepathology Research and the Human Physiome on a Chip project, developing microphysiological systems ("organs on chips") for studying human diseases. Her lab built the first liver-chip model and multi-organ perfusion platforms that link liver, gut, and endometrium, and applies them to endometriosis, where the group combines patient-derived endometrial organoids in synthetic PEG hydrogels with multi-omics and immune cell co-culture to model disease lesions. Methods span hydrogel and scaffold design, microfluidics, organoid biology, and systems biology.

lindsay-b-case-biology.s: Lindsay Case's lab in MIT Biology and the Koch Institute studies how liquid–liquid phase separation and multivalent protein interactions drive the spatial organization of signal transduction at the plasma membrane. Focusing on focal adhesions — integrin-based cell-matrix junctions — they show that phase-separated condensates concentrate LAT, Nck, N-WASP, and actin-regulatory proteins to trigger downstream signaling, and study how loss of condensate integrity contributes to cancer progression. Methods include TIRF and super-resolution microscopy, reconstitution of purified proteins on supported lipid bilayers, and biophysical measurements of condensate properties.

luca-daniel-eecs.s: Luca Daniel is a professor in MIT EECS/RLE working on computational methods for electronic design automation and, more recently, robustness certification of deep neural networks. His group develops integral-equation solvers, parameterized model-order reduction, uncertainty quantification, and robust optimization for RF circuits, silicon photonics, MEMS, and MRI scanners, and recently produced CROWN and other formal-verification tools for certifying adversarial robustness of deep networks. He is an IEEE Fellow and received the DAC Best Paper Award for guaranteed passive balancing transformations.

lydia-bourouiba-meche.l: Fluid Dynamics of Disease Transmission Laboratory
lydia-bourouiba-meche.s: Lydia Bourouiba directs the Fluid Dynamics of Disease Transmission Laboratory at MIT MechE/CEE/IMES, studying the multi-phase fluid mechanics of sneezes, coughs, rain drops, and plant-leaf splashes that carry pathogens. Her high-speed imaging showed that sneezes produce a multiphase turbulent gas cloud that suspends droplets and carries them up to 8 meters, overturning the classical large-vs-small droplet dichotomy and reshaping ventilation guidance during COVID-19. Methods include high-speed videography, flow visualization, mathematical modeling of nonlinear fluid fragmentation, and biophysics of pathogen survival.

m-a-ivanov-physics.s: Mikhail M. Ivanov is an assistant professor in the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics (CTP), working at the interface of cosmology and effective field theory. His group develops the Effective Field Theory of Large-Scale Structure (EFTofLSS) and applies it to galaxy clustering data from surveys such as BOSS, eBOSS, and DESI to measure cosmological parameters, constrain neutrino masses, and probe inflation, dark matter, and dark energy. He received the 2024 New Horizons in Physics Breakthrough Prize.
m-a-ivanov-physics.notes: labs.json had 'M. A. Ivanov' but the MIT CTP professor is Mikhail M. Ivanov. Middle initial was wrong in source.

m-a-kastner-physics.s: Marc A. Kastner is the Donner Professor of Physics Emeritus at MIT and a pioneer of mesoscopic electron transport. His experimental group studied correlated electron motion in nanometer-size semiconductor structures and discovered the single-electron transistor (Coulomb blockade and single-electron charging in quantum dots), which became a foundational device for nanoelectronics and quantum information. He also served as MIT Physics Department head, Dean of the School of Science, and director of the Science Philanthropy Alliance.
m-a-kastner-physics.notes: labs.json had k.g=PHK_EmkAAAAJ but Kastner's real Scholar ID is U9hsGSIAAAAJ. Cleared wrong link per user guidance.
