m-chen-physics.s: Min Chen is Professor Emeritus of Physics at MIT, with a long career in experimental high-energy particle physics spanning the discoveries of the J/psi meson (1974, Samuel Ting collaboration) and of the gluon (1979, TASSO experiment at PETRA). More recently he has worked on left-handed metamaterials and experimentally demonstrated reversed Cherenkov radiation in negative-index media. He received the 1995 EPS prize for the gluon discovery.
m-chen-physics.notes: labs.json had k.g=6xFvyJwAAAAJ but could not verify this is actually Min Chen (common name; Web search could not confirm the ID is his). Cleared per user guidance.

m-j-evans-physics.s: Matthew Evans develops the precision optical and quantum-measurement technology that drives gravitational-wave interferometers at LIGO, Virgo, and the proposed next-generation Cosmic Explorer observatory. His group works on low-thermal-noise mirror coatings, frequency-dependent squeezed-light injection, and control of quantum-optical states to push interferometer sensitivity below the standard quantum limit. He leads the US Cosmic Explorer effort, designing a much larger ground-based detector to map the gravitational-wave sky out to the earliest black hole mergers.
m-j-evans-physics.notes: Name fix: labs.json had 'M. J. Evans'. Real name Matthew Evans. Filled in Scholar ID from search result (labs.json had fallback search URL).

m-mcdonald-physics.s: Michael McDonald studies the co-evolution of massive galaxies, their supermassive black holes, and the hot intracluster medium in the universe's largest gravitationally bound structures — galaxy clusters. His group discovered and continues to study the Phoenix Cluster, the only known galaxy cluster hosting a full-blown cooling flow fueling extreme star formation in its central galaxy. The group observes clusters across the electromagnetic spectrum using X-ray (Chandra, XMM), optical/IR (Magellan, HST, JWST), submm (ALMA, SPT), and radio telescopes to track AGN feedback and cluster formation back into the early universe.
m-mcdonald-physics.notes: Name fix: 'M. McDonald' -> Michael A. McDonald. Existing k.g was fallback search URL; cleared to avoid wrong-PI link (common name).

m-williams-physics.s: Mike Williams leads the MIT LHCb group and searches for physics beyond the Standard Model using the LHCb experiment at CERN, with a focus on dark photons, hidden-sector particles, and precision measurements of heavy-flavor hadron decays. His group develops real-time data-processing and machine-learning trigger algorithms for LHCb and also works on the GlueX experiment at Jefferson Lab to probe emergent QCD phenomena such as exotic hadrons. Williams is Deputy Director of the NSF AI Institute for Artificial Intelligence and Fundamental Interactions (IAIFI) and co-chairs MIT's interdisciplinary PhD program in physics, statistics, and data science.
m-williams-physics.notes: Name fix: 'M. Williams' -> Mike Williams. Existing k.g was fallback search URL; cleared (common name).

manolis-kellis-eecs.s: Manolis Kellis leads MIT's Computational Biology Group in CSAIL and the Broad Institute, developing machine-learning and comparative-genomics methods to interpret the non-coding genome, regulatory circuitry, and epigenomic landscapes across human tissues. The group builds pipelines that integrate ENCODE/Roadmap epigenomic data, single-cell transcriptomics and chromatin accessibility, and GWAS variants to map disease variants to the cell types, regulators, and target genes they perturb, with major applications in Alzheimer's, obesity, cardiovascular disease, and cancer. Kellis also works on genome-wide identification of regulatory motifs, chromatin-state HMMs (ChromHMM), and evolutionary analysis of vertebrate and Drosophila genomes.

mark-bathe-bioe.l: Bathe BioNanoLab (Laboratory for Nucleic Acid Nanotechnology)
mark-bathe-bioe.s: Mark Bathe engineers DNA and RNA nanostructures as programmable materials for therapeutic delivery, vaccines, molecular data storage, and quantum sensing. The lab uses DNA origami and wireframe nucleic acid assemblies to display antigens mimicking viral capsids (HIV, flu), to target siRNA/mRNA/CRISPR payloads in vivo, and to organize chromophores and color centers for room-temperature quantum devices. The group combines computational design tools (DAEDALUS, PERDIX, ATHENA), cryo-EM and AFM characterization, and in vivo delivery assays to bridge structural DNA nanotechnology and translational biomedicine.

mark-f-bear-bcs.s: Mark Bear studies experience-dependent synaptic plasticity in the visual cortex and hippocampus, using mice to dissect the molecular mechanisms of LTP, LTD, and ocular-dominance plasticity. His lab discovered that excess mGluR5-driven protein synthesis underlies Fragile X syndrome pathology in Fmr1-knockout mice (the 'mGluR theory of fragile X'), and that similar plasticity mechanisms can be harnessed for amblyopia recovery. Methods include in vivo and slice electrophysiology, two-photon imaging, pharmacology, and genetic mouse models of autism and intellectual disability.

mark-t-harnett-bcs.s: Mark Harnett studies how the biophysical properties of individual neurons — ion channels, dendritic arbors, synaptic inputs — enable single-cell computation in mammalian cortex. The lab was the first to characterize the electrical properties of human cortical dendrites, showing that signal attenuation along long human dendrites compartmentalizes them into independent computing units. Methods include patch-clamp recordings from dendrites in rodent and human acute brain slices, two-photon imaging and glutamate uncaging, viral circuit tracing, and biophysical modeling.

mark-vogelsberger-physics.s: Mark Vogelsberger runs large-scale cosmological hydrodynamics simulations of galaxy formation and dark-matter physics, and is a principal developer of the Illustris and IllustrisTNG projects using the moving-mesh AREPO code. IllustrisTNG follows millions of galaxies in ~300 Mpc volumes, modeling gas cooling, star formation, stellar and AGN feedback, and magnetic field amplification by small-scale turbulent dynamos. His group also simulates self-interacting and warm dark matter to test alternatives to cold dark matter against observations of dwarf galaxies and substructure.

markus-j-buehler-meche.l: Laboratory for Atomistic and Molecular Mechanics (LAMM)
markus-j-buehler-meche.s: Markus Buehler studies the structure, deformation, and failure of biological and synthetic protein materials — collagen, silk, keratin, amyloid — across length scales using atomistic and multiscale molecular-dynamics simulations coupled with experimental mechanical testing. The lab builds graph-based and multi-agent AI models that translate protein sequence and hierarchical structure into predictions of mechanical function, and uses generative models to design de novo materials and musical representations of protein architecture. Applications span bio-inspired structural materials, sustainable polymers, and AI-driven scientific discovery.

martin-w-zwierlein-physics.s: Martin Zwierlein traps and images strongly interacting ultracold Fermi gases of lithium-6 and potassium-40 atoms at nanokelvin temperatures to study fermionic superfluidity, BEC–BCS crossover, the unitary Fermi gas, and analog models of neutron-star matter and high-Tc superconductors. The group builds quantum gas microscopes that resolve individual fermions on optical lattices, recently producing the first direct microscope images of fermion pair correlations. They also create and control ultracold polar NaK and NaLi molecules to explore dipolar many-body physics and quantum chemistry at ultralow temperatures.
martin-w-zwierlein-physics.notes: Filled in Scholar ID from search result (labs.json had fallback search URL).

martin-z-bazant-math.s: Martin Bazant develops mathematical models of transport phenomena in electrochemical systems, microfluidics, and porous electrodes, with major applications to lithium-ion batteries, electrokinetic water treatment, and fuel cells. The group derived nonlinear phase-field and nonequilibrium thermodynamic theories of electrochemical kinetics that predict lithium intercalation dynamics in LiFePO4 and other battery cathodes, and developed induced-charge electro-osmosis theory for microfluidic pumping. Methods include asymptotic analysis, continuum PDE modeling, and machine-learning-assisted fitting to operando battery and microfluidic experiments.

mary-gehring-biology.s: Mary Gehring studies how DNA methylation and other epigenetic marks control gene expression, genomic imprinting, and seed development in the flowering plant Arabidopsis thaliana. The lab uses whole-genome bisulfite sequencing, RNA-seq of maternal and paternal alleles in endosperm, and mutants of DNA methyltransferases and demethylases (DME, MET1, CMT) to map imprinted genes and track how methylation is reprogrammed during reproduction. They have also shown that small noncoding RNAs and transposable elements help direct heritable methylation changes, with implications for epigenetic inheritance and plant hybrid vigor.

marzyeh-ghassemi-eecs.s: Marzyeh Ghassemi studies machine learning for healthcare, with emphasis on robustness, fairness, privacy, and interpretability of clinical prediction models. The group works on medical imaging (chest X-ray, dermatology), EHR-based risk prediction, and clinical language models, exposing and mitigating demographic performance disparities and shortcut learning. Methods include causal inference, adversarial robustness, federated and differentially private training, and multimodal foundation models for clinical data.

mathias-kolle-meche.s: Mathias Kolle designs bio-inspired soft photonic materials that mimic the structural color of butterfly wings, beetle elytra, limpet shells, and chiton eyes, where periodic micro/nanostructures generate color without pigments. The group fabricates stretchable photonic fibers, multilayer Bragg reflectors, and chiral cellulose nanocrystal films that change color in response to strain, humidity, or chemistry for wearable sensors, anti-counterfeiting, and optical displays. Methods span nanoimprint lithography, self-assembly of block copolymers, roll-to-roll film processing, and optical spectroscopy.

matthew-a-wilson-biology.s: Matthew Wilson records from large ensembles of hippocampal and neocortical neurons in freely behaving and sleeping rats to study memory encoding, consolidation, and 'replay' of experience. His lab pioneered the discovery that hippocampal place cells re-express the firing sequences of recently traversed trajectories during sleep, and that these replay events are coordinated with visual cortex — implicating sleep replay as a mechanism for memory consolidation. Methods include chronic multi-tetrode and silicon-probe recordings, closed-loop decoding, genetic and pharmacological manipulation, and behavioral maze tasks.

matthew-c-jones-biology.s: Matthew G. Jones develops computational and single-cell lineage-tracing methods to trace how tumors evolve over space and time, with a focus on extrachromosomal DNA (ecDNA), copy-number instability, and phenotypic plasticity. The lab combines CRISPR-based molecular recording (e.g., GESTALT/Cas9-scar lineage barcodes), single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, and phylogenetic inference to reconstruct tumor cell lineages and the rules governing clonal selection. Work is done in lung and pancreatic cancer mouse models and patient samples to identify new therapeutic targets exposed by tumor-evolution dynamics.
matthew-c-jones-biology.notes: Name fix: 'Matthew C. Jones' -> Matthew G. Jones (new MIT Bio/Koch/IMES assistant prof as of 2026). Cleared existing k.g (was fallback).

matthew-d-shoulders-chemistry.s: Matthew Shoulders dissects the 'proteostasis network' — the ~1,000-component cellular machinery that chaperones, folds, and degrades proteins — and uses it to develop new directed-evolution and protein-engineering platforms. A major focus is collagen folding, where the lab studies how defects in folding machinery drive ~50 connective-tissue diseases, and influenza evolution, where host chaperones shape which mutations viruses can tolerate. Methods include chemical-biology probes of the heat shock response and UPR, DRIVE/T7-based directed evolution in mammalian cells, mass-spec proteomics, and biophysical characterization of triple-helical collagen.
matthew-d-shoulders-chemistry.notes: Existing k.g was fallback search URL; cleared.

matthew-g-vander-heiden-biology.s: Matthew Vander Heiden studies how cancer cells rewire central carbon, amino-acid, and nucleotide metabolism to meet the biosynthetic demands of proliferation — a research program that helped re-establish the Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis) as a druggable target. His group showed that the PKM2 isoform of pyruvate kinase is a regulatory node that cancer cells use to divert glucose flux into macromolecular precursors, and has worked on metabolic vulnerabilities of IDH-mutant tumors. Methods include stable-isotope tracing (13C/15N flux analysis), mass spectrometry metabolomics, GEMMs of lung and pancreatic cancer, and in vivo tumor metabolism studies. He directs the Koch Institute for Integrative Cancer Research.
matthew-g-vander-heiden-biology.notes: Existing k.g was fallback search URL; cleared.

max-a-metlitski-physics.s: Max Metlitski is a condensed matter theorist studying strongly correlated quantum matter, topological phases, and deconfined quantum criticality. His work has developed dualities relating different 2+1D gauge theories (boson/fermion dualities), reshaped the understanding of the composite-fermion metal at filling factor 1/2 in the quantum Hall regime, and illuminated surface states of symmetry-protected topological insulators. Methods are field-theoretic: renormalization group, conformal field theory, and non-perturbative arguments constraining phases and phase transitions in quantum many-body systems.
max-a-metlitski-physics.notes: Filled in Scholar ID from search result.

max-tegmark-physics.s: Max Tegmark works at the intersection of precision cosmology, AI interpretability, and AI safety. His cosmology research used SDSS galaxy clustering and CMB data to constrain the matter content and geometry of the universe, and he is known for proposing the Mathematical Universe Hypothesis. In recent years the group focuses on mechanistic interpretability of neural networks — probing LLM internals, identifying emergent circuits, and developing tools like Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks — as part of the NSF Institute for AI and Fundamental Interactions (IAIFI).

mehran-kardar-physics.s: Mehran Kardar is a theoretical statistical physicist whose work spans non-equilibrium growth, disordered systems, biophysics, and Casimir/fluctuation-induced forces. He co-derived the Kardar–Parisi–Zhang (KPZ) equation describing universal scaling of driven rough interfaces, and has developed theories of directed polymers in random media, tethered surfaces, and polymer/DNA mechanics. Methods are analytical: field theory, renormalization group, replica methods, and mappings between stochastic growth and quantum/disordered systems.

mehrdad-jazayeri-bcs.s: Mehrdad Jazayeri studies how the brain builds internal models of the world to support timing, prediction, planning, and inference, using non-human primates performing psychophysical tasks. His lab has shown that the frontal cortex encodes elapsed time via low-dimensional neural trajectories whose speed is Bayesian-tuned by prior time intervals, providing a mechanistic account of adaptive temporal control. Methods include multi-electrode and Neuropixels recordings from frontal and parietal cortex, recurrent-neural-network modeling, closed-loop behavioral tasks, and dimensionality-reduction analyses of population activity.

mei-hong-chemistry.s: Mei Hong develops and applies high-resolution magic-angle-spinning (MAS) solid-state NMR spectroscopy to determine the structure, dynamics, and lipid interactions of biological macromolecules that resist crystallization. Major targets include the influenza M2 proton channel, SARS/MERS/SARS-CoV-2 envelope (E) proteins, amyloid-beta and tau fibrils from Alzheimer's disease, bacterial multidrug-resistance transporters, and plant cell-wall polysaccharides. The lab pioneered dipolar-recoupling and dynamic-nuclear-polarization (DNP) techniques that push ssNMR sensitivity to micrograms of isotope-labeled protein in native-like lipid environments.

meng-li-nse.s: Mingda Li studies quantum materials and their neutron/X-ray scattering signatures, with a focus on phonon-electron coupling, topological materials, and AI-accelerated discovery of radiation-tolerant materials for nuclear applications. The group combines inelastic neutron scattering (at SNS, NIST, HFIR) and X-ray scattering with first-principles DFT, machine-learning surrogate models, and graph neural networks that learn from scattering data. Applications span topological semimetals, magnetoresistive thermoelectrics, and neutron-irradiation-resistant structural alloys for fission and fusion reactors.
meng-li-nse.notes: Name fix: labs.json 'Meng Li' -> Mingda Li (actual MIT NSE faculty). Cleared existing k.g (hidHQ5sAAAAJ was likely a different Meng Li, not Mingda). See also jun-li-nse/junghwan-kim-meche for similar NSE/MechE mislabeling.

michael-artin-math.s: Michael Artin is a foundational algebraic geometer whose work transformed the field in the 1960s–80s. He introduced étale cohomology with Grothendieck, developed étale homotopy theory with Barry Mazur, proved the Artin approximation theorem in local algebra, and founded the theory of algebraic spaces and algebraic stacks. In his later career he has focused on noncommutative algebraic geometry, studying noncommutative projective schemes, Sklyanin algebras, and graded ring theory. Professor emeritus; Wolf Prize (2013), National Medal of Science (2016).
michael-artin-math.notes: Pure mathematician — no lab group name.

michael-b-yaffe-biology.s: Michael Yaffe studies how protein kinase signaling networks govern cell-cycle checkpoints, DNA damage responses, and mitotic stress, with a focus on translating these circuits into cancer therapy. His group discovered phosphoserine/threonine-binding domains (14-3-3, BRCT, FHA, Polo-box) that read phospho-codes set by kinases like ATM, ATR, Chk1/2, and Plk1, and has explored how p38MAPK–MK2 signaling governs DNA damage responses in p53-mutant tumors. Methods span systems-biology mass spectrometry phosphoproteomics, CRISPR screens, biochemistry, structural biology of signaling domains, and mouse cancer models. Yaffe also practices as a surgical intensivist at Beth Israel Deaconess.

michael-carbin-eecs.s: Michael Carbin studies the semantics, design, and implementation of programming systems — programming languages, compilers, and runtime systems — with recent emphasis on systems that train and deploy large neural networks. With Jonathan Frankle he introduced the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis, showing that dense neural networks contain small sparse subnetworks that train to full accuracy from the same initialization. The group also works on approximate and probabilistic computing (Rely, FlexJava), verified program transformations, and compilers and runtime systems for sparse/pruned model training and inference.

michael-e-birnbaum-bioe.s: Michael Birnbaum studies how T cell receptors (TCRs) recognize peptide–MHC antigens in cancer and infection, and engineers immune-recognition tools for immunotherapy. The lab developed yeast-display and reprogrammed-virus screens that map TCR specificity and cross-reactivity across millions of peptides, and designs soluble TCRs and chimeric antigen receptors for adoptive cell therapy. Methods include protein engineering, deep mutational scanning, structural biology of TCR/pMHC complexes, and single-cell immune repertoire sequencing of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes.

michael-f-howland-cee.s: Michael Howland studies atmospheric boundary-layer turbulence and applies it to the design, control, and optimization of wind farms and other renewable-energy systems. His group developed a unified rotor aerodynamics model and demonstrated that coordinated wake-steering (yaw misalignment) of upstream turbines can raise total wind-farm power output, a result now tested in field trials. Methods include large-eddy simulation on supercomputers, reduced-order analytical models, SCADA data analysis from operating wind farms, and field measurements of atmospheric stratification and turbulence over land and ocean.

michael-j-cima-dmse.s: Michael Cima develops advanced materials and microfabrication techniques for implantable medical devices, drug delivery, and diagnostics. He is a co-inventor of MIT's three-dimensional printing (3DP) process and has pioneered MEMS-based drug-delivery implants that release stored doses on wireless command; his intravesical device TAR-200 for gemcitabine delivery was FDA-approved for bladder cancer in 2025. The lab also develops implantable NMR and optical sensors for continuous physiological monitoring, and ceramic/polymer processing for complex device geometries.

michael-j-follows-eaps.s: Mick Follows builds numerical models of marine microbial ecosystems to understand how physical, chemical, and biological processes shape ocean biogeochemistry and the global carbon cycle. The Darwin Project uses 'self-assembling' ecosystem models in which thousands of virtual phytoplankton types with randomized traits compete in a general circulation model, spontaneously recovering observed biogeographies of Prochlorococcus, Synechococcus, and diatoms. The group leads MIT's role in the Simons Collaboration CBIOMES, combining global ocean models with satellite ocean-color data, flow cytometry, and metagenomics to connect microbial diversity to nutrient and carbon cycling.

michael-mcdonald-physics.s: Michael McDonald studies the co-evolution of massive galaxies, their supermassive black holes, and the hot intracluster medium in the universe's largest gravitationally bound structures — galaxy clusters. His group discovered and continues to study the Phoenix Cluster, the only known galaxy cluster hosting a full-blown cooling flow fueling extreme star formation in its central galaxy. The group observes clusters across the electromagnetic spectrum using X-ray (Chandra, XMM), optical/IR (Magellan, HST, JWST), submm (ALMA, SPT), and radio telescopes to track AGN feedback and cluster formation back into the early universe.
michael-mcdonald-physics.notes: DUPLICATE of m-mcdonald-physics — same person (Michael A. McDonald, MIT Physics/Kavli, Phoenix Cluster discoverer). Cleared existing k.g (OWuBry8AAAAJ) — could not verify it belongs to this McDonald under new scholar rule.

michael-p-short-nse.s: Michael Short studies radiation damage, corrosion, and microstructural evolution of structural materials in nuclear reactors (fission and fusion) and molten-salt environments. The lab developed a transient-grating-spectroscopy (TGS) platform that lets them watch materials in real time while under ion-beam irradiation, yielding ~10^6× throughput gains over post-mortem characterization. They also develop nanocalorimetric forensic signatures of uranium enrichment history for nuclear safeguards, and study cold fusion/LENR claims using calibrated calorimetry.

michael-t-hemann-biology.s: Michael Hemann uses mouse models of hematopoietic malignancies — principally Eµ-Myc lymphoma — combined with pooled shRNA and CRISPR screens to identify genes that modulate response and resistance to chemotherapy. The lab has defined mechanisms by which bone-marrow stromal cells provide survival signals to drug-tolerant leukemia cells and has identified new drug combinations that preempt resistance evolution. Methods span in vivo RNAi/CRISPR barcoding, high-throughput compound screening in syngeneic mouse tumors, and single-cell transcriptomics of drug-tolerant persister populations.

michael-t-laub-biology.s: Michael Laub studies how bacteria process information and regulate growth, with a focus on the design principles and evolution of two-component signal-transduction pathways (histidine kinase–response regulator). Using Caulobacter crescentus and E. coli, the lab has mapped how kinase–regulator specificity is encoded at the residue level, how orthogonal pathways avoid crosstalk, and how evolutionary rewiring of these circuits proceeds. More recent work systematically identifies anti-phage defense systems — toxin–antitoxin, CBASS, retrons, and mRNA-modifying systems — and dissects their mechanisms. HHMI Investigator.

michale-s-fee-bcs.s: Michale Fee studies the neural circuit basis of learned sequential behavior using the zebra finch songbird, a tractable vertebrate model of vocal learning homologous to human speech acquisition. The lab showed that premotor nucleus HVC generates a sparse sequential clock that drives song and that the anterior forebrain pathway (including Area X, a basal ganglia homolog) implements a reinforcement-learning-like system that gradually tunes song via LMAN-driven motor variability. Methods include chronic electrode recordings and calcium imaging in singing birds, targeted lesions, optogenetic silencing, and biophysical/RL modeling of cortico-basal-ganglia circuits.

mick-follows-eaps.s: Mick Follows builds numerical models of marine microbial ecosystems to understand how physical, chemical, and biological processes shape ocean biogeochemistry and the global carbon cycle. The Darwin Project uses 'self-assembling' ecosystem models in which thousands of virtual phytoplankton types with randomized traits compete in a general circulation model, spontaneously recovering observed biogeographies of Prochlorococcus, Synechococcus, and diatoms. The group leads MIT's role in the Simons Collaboration CBIOMES, combining global ocean models with satellite ocean-color data, flow cytometry, and metagenomics to connect microbial diversity to nutrient and carbon cycling.
mick-follows-eaps.notes: DUPLICATE of michael-j-follows-eaps (Michael/'Mick' Follows same person). Cleared k.g (was fallback).

mikhail-m-ivanov-physics.s: Mikhail M. Ivanov is a theoretical cosmologist working on large-scale structure, effective field theories of cosmological perturbations, and primordial non-Gaussianity. His group develops the effective field theory of large-scale structure (EFTofLSS) and applies it to galaxy clustering data from BOSS, eBOSS, and DESI to constrain dark energy, neutrino masses, and the Hubble tension. Work includes analytical perturbation theory, N-body simulations for calibration, and Bayesian inference pipelines for cosmological parameter estimation.

min-chen-physics.s: Min Chen is an experimental particle physicist at MIT working on collider searches for the Higgs boson and physics beyond the Standard Model as part of the CMS collaboration at the LHC. His group contributed to detector hardware, trigger electronics, and data analyses leading to the 2012 Higgs discovery, and has worked historically on the L3 experiment at LEP and the AMS cosmic-ray detector. Common name — see also m-chen-physics for duplicate entry.
min-chen-physics.notes: DUPLICATE of m-chen-physics. Cleared existing k.g (6xFvyJwAAAAJ unverifiable for correct Min Chen).

ming-guo-meche.s: Ming Guo studies the mechanics of living cells and tissues — how cytoplasm viscoelasticity, cortical tension, osmotic pressure, and cell size/shape jointly control physiological processes like embryogenesis, tissue morphogenesis, and cancer metastasis. The lab develops optical-tweezer-based active microrheology and 3D traction-force microscopy to measure cell mechanics in situ, and has shown that tumors with softer, larger cells at their periphery are more invasive. They also engineer muscle/tendon repair scaffolds from aligned living cells.

minghao-guo-meche.notes: Not a faculty lab — Minghao Guo is a PhD candidate in MechE (polymer informatics / ML for materials). Likely mislabeled as a lab in source data. Recommend delete or reassign. Not enriched.

mingyu-li-nse.notes: No MIT NSE faculty named 'Mingyu Li'. The actual MIT NSE Li is Mingda Li (see meng-li-nse). Likely mislabeled student entry. Not enriched. Recommend delete.

moe-z-win-eecs.l: Wireless Information and Network Sciences Laboratory (WINS Lab)
moe-z-win-eecs.s: Moe Win founded and directs MIT's Wireless Information and Network Sciences Lab, working on the fundamental limits and algorithms for network localization, navigation, and wireless communications. The group developed the theory of network localization and navigation (NLN), proving optimal bounds for cooperative GPS-denied positioning and building ultra-wide-bandwidth systems for indoor and through-wall localization. Other work spans network interference exploitation, intrinsic wireless secrecy, and quantum sensing/communication.

mohammad-alizadeh-eecs.s: Mohammad Alizadeh studies computer networks and systems, with a current focus on applying machine learning to system design — congestion control, video streaming, job scheduling, and database query optimization. Prior to MIT he co-designed the Data Center TCP (DCTCP) and pFabric protocols that shaped modern datacenter transport, and he continues to work on network algorithms, programmable switches, and low-latency datacenter systems. Methods include reinforcement-learning-based policies (Park), large-scale network emulation, and theoretical analysis of scheduling and queuing.

mohammad-movassaghi-chemistry.s: Mohammad Movassaghi develops new strategies and reactions for the total synthesis of complex alkaloid natural products, including biogenetically inspired dimeric indoles, epipolythiodiketopiperazines (ETPs), and communesins. The group has pioneered directed heterodimerization methodology that allows two complex bulky indoline fragments to be joined in a single step, enabling concise syntheses of (+)-11,11'-dideoxyverticillin A, haouamine, and related targets. Methods: modern organic synthesis, catalytic asymmetric C–C bond formation, free-radical chemistry, and late-stage functionalization.

mohammad-reza-alizadeh-eecs.s: Mohammad Alizadeh studies computer networks and systems, with a current focus on applying machine learning to system design — congestion control, video streaming, job scheduling, and database query optimization. Prior to MIT he co-designed the Data Center TCP (DCTCP) and pFabric protocols that shaped modern datacenter transport, and he continues to work on network algorithms, programmable switches, and low-latency datacenter systems. Methods include reinforcement-learning-based policies (Park), large-scale network emulation, and theoretical analysis of scheduling and queuing.
mohammad-reza-alizadeh-eecs.notes: DUPLICATE of mohammad-alizadeh-eecs — same person (full name Mohammad Reza Alizadeh, goes by Mohammad). Cleared existing k.g (ZaW8ZbsAAAAJ is a different M.Reza Alizadeh per search).

monty-krieger-biology.s: Monty Krieger studies lipoprotein biology and cell-surface cholesterol transport, with a particular focus on Scavenger Receptor class B type I (SR-BI), the primary high-density-lipoprotein (HDL) receptor he co-discovered. The lab uses mouse genetics, biochemistry, cell biology, and chemical-biology probes to dissect how SR-BI mediates selective cholesterol uptake from HDL, and has established its roles in atherosclerosis, coronary heart disease, red-cell maturation, and — unexpectedly — female fertility. Earlier work defined macrophage scavenger receptors and LDL-receptor trafficking in CHO-cell mutants.

moungi-g-bawendi-chemistry.s: Moungi Bawendi won the 2023 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for developing the hot-injection synthesis of monodisperse colloidal semiconductor quantum dots — nanocrystals whose size controls their emission wavelength via quantum confinement. His group continues to develop near-IR and visible quantum-dot emitters for biological imaging (including through tissue), photovoltaics, displays, and single-photon sources, and to probe single-nanocrystal photophysics. Methods include wet-chemistry nanocrystal synthesis, surface-ligand engineering, ultrafast and single-particle spectroscopy, and in vivo fluorescence imaging.

mriganka-sur-bcs.s: Mriganka Sur studies the development, plasticity, and dynamics of cerebral cortex circuits, especially visual cortex, combining in vivo two-photon imaging, electrophysiology, and optogenetics in mouse models of typical and atypical brain development. The lab's landmark 'rewiring' experiments in ferrets showed that visual input redirected to the auditory thalamus produces functional 'visual' responses in auditory cortex, demonstrating cortical plasticity's limits. Current work dissects astrocyte–neuron interactions, gene networks underlying Rett syndrome and autism, and the cortical basis of attention and visuomotor transformation. Sur founded the Simons Center for the Social Brain at MIT.

n-catherine-hogan-meche.notes: No MIT MechE/BCS faculty named 'N. Catherine Hogan'. MechE staff directory lists a Catherine Anne Forrest (chogan@mit.edu) as staff, not a faculty PI. Neville Hogan is the actual MechE/BCS PI (see neville-hogan-meche). Likely an auto-scrape error. Not enriched. Recommend delete.

n-mavalvala-physics.s: Nergis Mavalvala develops quantum-optical and precision-measurement techniques to improve the sensitivity of the LIGO gravitational-wave detectors. Her group produces squeezed states of light and injects them into LIGO's kilometre-scale Michelson interferometers to beat the standard quantum limit, and was the first to demonstrate squeezed-light generation via optomechanics at room temperature. She is a longtime LIGO collaboration member who helped develop detector technologies used in the 2016 first detection of gravitational waves, and is now Dean of MIT's School of Science. MacArthur Fellow, National Academy of Sciences.
n-mavalvala-physics.notes: DUPLICATE of nergis-mavalvala-physics. Cleared k.g (fallback). Name fix 'N. Mavalvala' -> Nergis Mavalvala.

nancy-kanwisher-bcs.s: Nancy Kanwisher uses functional MRI and related imaging methods to map the functional organization of the human brain, discovering cortical regions each specialized for a single high-level category of perception or cognition. Her 1997 discovery of the fusiform face area (FFA), which responds selectively to faces, anchors a broader portfolio of category-selective regions her lab has identified — the parahippocampal place area (PPA) for scenes, the extrastriate body area (EBA) for bodies, and regions for visual word forms and music. Current work asks how these functional specializations develop, how they depend on visual experience (even congenital blindness), and how they relate to deep-network models of perception.

nancy-lynch-eecs.s: Nancy Lynch is a foundational theorist of distributed and concurrent computing. With Fischer and Paterson she proved the FLP impossibility result: no deterministic consensus protocol can tolerate even a single crash failure in an asynchronous system — a cornerstone negative result shaping blockchain and datacenter protocols. Her TDS group studies dynamic and wireless distributed algorithms, formal modeling with I/O automata and timed I/O automata, and more recently distributed algorithms inspired by biology (ant colonies, developing embryos, neural circuits). Author of the widely used textbook Distributed Algorithms.

nathaniel-hendren-economics.s: Nathaniel Hendren uses administrative tax, education, and insurance-market data to measure economic opportunity, intergenerational mobility, and the welfare impacts of government policies. With co-authors he developed the 'marginal value of public funds' (MVPF) framework — a unified benefit-to-cost metric — and applied it to 133 US policies spanning education, transfers, housing, and taxation, arguing that investments in children have the highest returns. He co-founded Opportunity Insights and Policy Impacts, and studies adverse selection and private-information frictions in insurance markets.

navid-azizan-meche.s: Navid Azizan works on principled machine learning, optimization, and control for large-scale and safety-critical autonomous systems. His research spans stochastic optimization algorithms (mirror-descent variants and their implicit-regularization properties), reliable uncertainty quantification for neural networks, and data-driven control with formal safety guarantees for robotics and power grids. Applications include autonomous driving, robot learning, and assessing the reliability of general-purpose AI models before deployment.

nergis-mavalvala-physics.s: Nergis Mavalvala develops quantum-optical and precision-measurement techniques to improve the sensitivity of the LIGO gravitational-wave detectors. Her group produces squeezed states of light and injects them into LIGO's kilometre-scale Michelson interferometers to beat the standard quantum limit, and was the first to demonstrate squeezed-light generation via optomechanics at room temperature. She is a longtime LIGO collaboration member who helped develop detector technologies used in the 2016 first detection of gravitational waves, and is now Dean of MIT's School of Science. MacArthur Fellow, National Academy of Sciences.

netta-engelhardt-physics.s: Netta Engelhardt is a theoretical physicist working on quantum gravity, holography, and the black-hole information paradox. Using AdS/CFT and techniques such as the quantum extremal surface formula (co-developed with Wall), she helped derive the Page curve for Hawking radiation entropy purely from gravity — strong evidence that black hole evaporation unitarily returns information rather than destroying it. Related work develops the holographic entropy cone, the outer entropy, and constraints on cosmological singularities. Recipient of the 2021 New Horizons in Physics Prize.

neville-hogan-meche.l: Eric P. and Evelyn E. Newman Laboratory for Biomechanics and Human Rehabilitation
neville-hogan-meche.s: Neville Hogan studies the physics and neural control of human movement and builds robots that physically cooperate with humans for rehabilitation after stroke, cerebral palsy, and other neurological injury. His lab developed the impedance-control framework for physical human–robot interaction and pioneered robotic therapy devices — MIT-Manus (upper extremity) and the Anklebot (lower extremity) — now in clinical use. Methods include biomechanics, motor-control experiments (reaching, locomotion), dynamical systems modeling, and design/control of back-drivable robotic manipulanda.

nicolas-g-hadjiconstantinou-meche.s: Nicolas Hadjiconstantinou develops kinetic-theory-based and multiscale methods to study nanoscale and rarefied gas and heat transport, where classical continuum Navier–Stokes and Fourier laws break down. His group works on deviational Monte Carlo solvers for the Boltzmann transport equation (gases and phonons), moment methods, and multiscale couplings between molecular dynamics and continuum solvers, with applications to MEMS, thermoelectrics, nanoscale heat conduction in semiconductors, and reentry aerothermodynamics. He co-directs MIT's Center for Computational Science and Engineering.

nidhi-seethapathi-bcs.s: Nidhi Seethapathi builds computational predictive models of human motor control and locomotion using a combination of nonlinear dynamics, optimal control, and machine learning, validated on naturalistic movement data. The lab formalizes the objectives humans trade off during movement — energy, stability, and time — and uses those principles to design autonomous robot-aided neuromotor rehabilitation for stroke and cerebral palsy. Methods include data-driven system identification on motion-capture and wearable-sensor data, trajectory-optimization-based inverse-optimal-control models, and RL-based robotic therapy policies.

nike-sun-math.s: Nike Sun works at the intersection of probability, statistical physics, combinatorics, and theoretical computer science, studying phase transitions in random discrete structures. With collaborators she proved the existence of the satisfiability threshold in random k-SAT (for large k), resolving a long-standing conjecture from the statistical-physics analysis of constraint satisfaction. Methods include the cavity method, second-moment arguments, small-subgraph conditioning, and coupling techniques applied to random graphs, random CSPs, and spin-glass models on locally tree-like graphs. Doeblin Prize (2020), NSF CAREER (2018).
nike-sun-math.notes: Existing k.g was fallback; cleared. Pure mathematician — no lab group name.

nikta-fakhri-physics.s: Nikta Fakhri studies non-equilibrium and active-matter physics, probing how living and engineered systems break detailed balance to generate motion and self-organization. Her lab developed fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotubes as stable single-molecule probes to track motor-driven cytoskeletal dynamics in live cells and reconstituted actomyosin systems. Recent work identified odd-elastic and chiral active phases, and used stochastic thermodynamics and entropy-production estimators to quantify how far living matter sits from equilibrium. Methods span fluorescence microscopy, microrheology, reconstituted biophysics, and statistical-mechanics theory.

noelle-e-selin-eaps.s: Noelle Selin builds and applies atmospheric chemistry and chemical-transport models (especially GEOS-Chem) to inform policy on air pollution, mercury, persistent organic pollutants, and climate change. She developed one of the first global 3D models of atmospheric mercury, tracing emissions from coal combustion and artisanal gold mining through oxidation, deposition, and bioaccumulation in fish. Current work links chemistry modeling with integrated assessment and decision analysis to evaluate the health and ecosystem benefits of the Minamata Convention, US EPA rules, and air-quality co-benefits of climate policy. Selin directs MIT's Center for Sustainability Science and Strategy.

o-hen-physics.s: Or Hen studies short-range correlations (SRCs) — brief, high-momentum pairs of nucleons within atomic nuclei — using high-energy electron, photon, proton, and neutrino scattering at Jefferson Lab, Brookhaven, Mainz, and Fermilab. SRCs account for ~20% of nucleons but carry 60–70% of the nuclear kinetic energy, and his measurements linked SRCs quantitatively to the EMC effect (modification of quark distributions in bound nucleons) and to the nuclear symmetry energy relevant for neutron stars. Methods include coincidence spectrometer experiments, wide-angle neutrino interaction measurements in argon, and development of dedicated detectors.

oliver-jagoutz-eaps.s: Oliver Jagoutz uses field geology, petrology, and geochemistry in collision zones like the Himalaya, Kohistan arc, and Oman ophiolite to understand how continental crust is built in subduction zones and how arc-continent collisions drive long-term climate change. His group proposed that exposure of large tracts of mafic arc crust near the equator — during the Gondwana–Laurasia collision — accelerated silicate weathering and drew down atmospheric CO2, triggering Cenozoic cooling and glaciation. Current work also targets natural hydrogen generation from serpentinized peridotites and in-situ CO2 mineralization for geologic carbon sequestration.

olivia-corradin-biology.s: Olivia Corradin studies how non-coding genetic variants — changes in regulatory DNA outside protein-coding regions — drive disease risk for multiple sclerosis, opioid use disorder, and other complex disorders. The lab developed the concept of 'outside variants' and methods that combine 3D chromatin contacts (Hi-C) with GWAS data to identify the cell types in which disease-linked regulatory elements act, pinpointing oligodendrocytes as an MS-relevant brain cell type. Methods span ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, Hi-C, epigenome editing, iPSC-derived neural cultures, and statistical-genetics pipelines.

ore-gottlieb-physics.s: Ore Gottlieb is a theoretical/computational astrophysicist studying the most extreme transients in the universe: gamma-ray bursts, core-collapse supernovae, tidal disruption events (TDEs), and compact-object mergers. His group runs large 3D general-relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) simulations of relativistic jets launched by rotating black holes and neutron stars, predicting electromagnetic and gravitational-wave counterparts to kilonovae and long GRBs. Work connects simulations directly to multi-messenger observations from LIGO/Virgo, Swift, Fermi, and JWST.

otto-x-cordero-cee.s: Otto Cordero studies the ecology and evolution of microbial communities, with a focus on bacterial consortia that assemble on sinking marine particles to degrade complex polysaccharides from dead algae and zooplankton. The lab combines high-throughput isolation of natural marine microbes, defined community assays, stable-isotope tracing, and metagenomics to identify simple 'assembly rules' that predict which taxa coexist and how metabolic cross-feeding structures degradation guilds. Findings link microscale interactions on marine particles to global ocean carbon cycling.

p-a-sharp-koch-institute.s: Phillip Sharp shared the 1993 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for the co-discovery of split genes and RNA splicing — the finding that eukaryotic genes contain introns that must be spliced out of pre-mRNA. His MIT/Koch lab now focuses on small non-coding RNAs, including microRNAs and a class of short RNAs produced near transcription start sites, and how these regulate gene expression in development, angiogenesis, and cellular stress. Methods span biochemistry of RNA processing, genome-wide RNA sequencing, and mammalian cell-culture loss-of-function screens. Founding director of the McGovern Institute.
p-a-sharp-koch-institute.notes: Name fix: 'P A Sharp' -> Phillip A. Sharp (1993 Nobel laureate). Cleared k.g (fallback).

p-a-lee-physics.s: Patrick A. Lee is a theoretical condensed-matter physicist who has made foundational contributions to disordered electronic systems, mesoscopic physics, and strongly correlated electrons. He introduced the concept of universal conductance fluctuations in mesoscopic conductors, developed resonating-valence-bond (RVB) and gauge-theory descriptions of high-temperature cuprate superconductors and quantum spin liquids, and analyzed the Mott transition and topological phases of matter. Awards include the Oliver E. Buckley Prize, the Dirac Medal of ICTP, and the 2022 Larkin Award in Theoretical Physics.
p-a-lee-physics.notes: Name fix: 'P. A. Lee' -> Patrick A. Lee.

p-harris-physics.s: Philip Harris is an experimental particle physicist on the CMS experiment at the LHC, searching for dark matter, exotic Higgs decays, and physics beyond the Standard Model — including model-independent anomaly detection using deep learning. He co-founded the Fast Machine Learning collaboration, developing ultra-low-latency neural-network inference (hls4ml) that runs on FPGAs in the CMS hardware trigger to make real-time decisions at 40 MHz collision rates. The group also develops jet-substructure taggers and autoencoder/normalizing-flow anomaly searches.

pablo-a-parrilo-math.s: Pablo Parrilo works on convex optimization, semidefinite programming, and their applications to control, machine learning, and quantum information. He pioneered sum-of-squares (SOS) programming — a systematic way to certify polynomial non-negativity via semidefinite programs — enabling tractable relaxations of otherwise NP-hard polynomial-optimization problems. His group develops SOSTOOLS, algebraic techniques for systems and control (robust stability, Lyapunov functions), and semidefinite approximations of matrix functions used in quantum information theory. Associate Director of MIT LIDS.

b-k-berger-math.notes: Beverly K. Berger is a relativist at Oakland Univ / former NSF Gravitational Physics PD. No MIT Math affiliation found. Recommend delete.

e-cary-brown-bcs.notes: E. Cary Brown was an MIT Economics professor (public finance), died 2007. Wrongly tagged BCS. Recommend delete.

parag-a-pathak-economics.s: Parag Pathak's group applies market design, mechanism design, and causal inference methods to school choice, affirmative action, and social policy evaluation. Research produces deferred-acceptance and top-trading-cycles assignment algorithms deployed in Boston, New York, Chicago, Washington DC, and other districts, and uses randomized lotteries as natural experiments to estimate the effects of charter schools, exam schools, and voucher programs. The lab combines theoretical matching theory with large-scale administrative-data econometrics.

patrick-a-lee-physics.s: Patrick A. Lee's group develops theoretical models of strongly correlated electronic systems, including high-temperature cuprate superconductors, doped Mott insulators, spin liquids, and quantum spin ice. The lab introduced the concept of universal conductance fluctuations and made foundational contributions to mesoscopic physics and disordered electron systems. Methods include slave-boson mean-field theory, gauge-theory treatments of doped antiferromagnets, and renormalization-group analysis.
patrick-a-lee-physics.notes: Duplicate of p-a-lee-physics; same enrichment applied.

patrick-jaillet-cee.s: Patrick Jaillet's group studies online and data-driven optimization, stochastic combinatorial problems, and sequential decision-making under uncertainty, with applications in transportation, logistics, energy markets, and internet systems. Work includes online traveling salesman problems with rejection, online stochastic matching, and dynamic vehicle routing. Methods draw on competitive-ratio analysis, primal-dual online algorithms, reinforcement learning, and large-scale linear/integer programming.

patrick-s-doyle-koch-institute.s: Patrick Doyle's lab works on soft matter engineering, microfluidics, polymer physics, and rheology, with a focus on hydrogel microparticles for multiplexed bioassays, drug delivery, and pharmaceutical manufacturing. Projects include stop-flow lithography for particle synthesis, DNA stretching in nanofluidic devices for single-molecule biophysics, and layered hydrogel depots for subcutaneous monoclonal-antibody delivery. Techniques combine photolithography, optical tweezers, light/neutron scattering, and microrheology.

paul-c-blainey-bioe.s: Paul Blainey's group builds microfluidic, optical, and molecular tools for quantitative single-cell and single-molecule biology, with applications in DNA-protein interaction analysis, drug screening, and genomic discovery. Key technologies include virtual microfluidics using hydrogel entrapment for high-throughput single-cell amplification, nanoliter-droplet arrays for high-content drug screens, and optical pooled CRISPR screening that combines pooled genetic perturbations with image-based phenotyping. Instrumentation spans custom fluorescence microscopy, droplet microfluidics, and next-generation sequencing.

paula-t-hammond-koch-institute.s: Paula Hammond's lab pioneers layer-by-layer (LbL) electrostatic self-assembly of polymer thin films for drug delivery, implant coatings, and nanoparticle-based cancer therapeutics. Projects include LbL-coated nanoparticles for targeted siRNA/mRNA delivery, controlled-release films for bone regeneration and wound healing, and polyelectrolyte multilayer energy-storage materials. Methods include dip/spray LbL deposition, click-chemistry functionalization, QCM and ellipsometry film characterization, and in vivo tumor delivery studies.

pavel-etingof-math.s: Pavel Etingof works at the intersection of representation theory and mathematical physics, with emphasis on quantum groups, tensor categories, Cherednik algebras, integrable systems, and deformation quantization. His research covers representations of Lie groups and Lie algebras, Hecke algebras, and holonomic systems, and he runs the MIT PRIMES high-school research program. Methods are algebraic and category-theoretic, with connections to conformal field theory and Yang–Baxter equations.

peter-a-fisher-physics.s: Peter Fisher's group searches for dark matter and primordial black holes using novel detector concepts, including the Dark Matter Time Projection Chamber for directional WIMP detection, and participates in the Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer (AMS-02) experiment on the International Space Station measuring cosmic-ray positron, antiproton, and helium spectra. Additional work probes the classical/quantum interface of electrodynamics. Instruments include gaseous TPCs, silicon trackers, transition radiation detectors, and superconducting magnets.

peter-c-dedon-bioe.s: Peter Dedon's group investigates epitranscriptomics and epigenomics in bacteria, cancer, and the microbiome, discovering phosphorothioate and 7-deazaguanine DNA modifications and mapping tRNA modification patterns that reprogram the genetic code under cellular stress. Projects connect codon-biased translation to stress response and infectious disease, and develop antimicrobials and biotech tools exploiting these enzymes. Methods include HPLC-coupled mass spectrometry of ribonucleoside modifications, single-molecule real-time (SMRT) sequencing, ribosome profiling, and comparative genomics.

peter-t-c-so-bioe.s: Peter So's group develops biophotonic imaging and fabrication technologies centered on multiphoton (two-photon) fluorescence microscopy, multifocal parallelized excitation, and optical tissue clearing for deep-tissue and in vivo imaging. Applications span protein biophysics, brain tissue mapping, cancer diagnostics, and high-content cellular phenotyping. Instrumentation includes femtosecond Ti:sapphire lasers, custom scanning microscopes, spatial light modulators, and FLIM/FRET detection.

peter-w-reddien-biology.s: Peter Reddien's group studies the cellular and molecular basis of whole-body regeneration using the planarian flatworm Schmidtea mediterranea as a model, focusing on neoblast adult stem cells that drive replacement of lost tissues and organs. Work identifies genes required for regeneration, defines positional-information signaling by muscle cells that guides tissue patterning, and dissects neoblast differentiation hierarchies. Techniques include RNAi screening, single-cell RNA-seq, whole-mount in situ hybridization, transplantation, and lineage tracing.

peter-w-shor-math.s: Peter Shor works on quantum computation, quantum information theory, and combinatorics, and is the inventor of Shor's polynomial-time quantum algorithm for integer factorization and discrete logarithms. His contributions include quantum error-correcting codes (the 9-qubit Shor code) and fault-tolerant quantum computation. Methods are theoretical: quantum circuit analysis, phase estimation, number theory, and algebraic coding theory.

philip-harris-physics.s: Philip Harris's group uses the CMS experiment at the LHC to search for new physics, including dark matter, anomalous Higgs decays, and exotic signatures, while building real-time machine-learning triggers that run on FPGA hardware at MHz collision rates. The lab co-founded the Fast Machine Learning organization and applies deep learning to multi-messenger astronomy including gravitational-wave event classification. Methods include hls4ml FPGA ML inference, graph neural networks for jet tagging, and anomaly detection.
philip-harris-physics.notes: Duplicate of p-harris-physics; same enrichment applied.

philippe-rigollet-math.s: Philippe Rigollet works on mathematical statistics, machine learning, and optimization, with emphasis on statistical optimal transport, high-dimensional estimation, and the theoretical foundations of transformers. His research includes statistical-versus-computational tradeoffs, aggregation theory, and Wasserstein-gradient-flow methods for sampling. Methods are theoretical: concentration inequalities, minimax lower bounds, convex geometry, and stochastic analysis.

phillip-a-sharp-koch-institute.s: Phillip Sharp's laboratory studies RNA biology, including the biogenesis and function of small regulatory RNAs (microRNAs and siRNAs), alternative splicing, and transcriptional regulation in cancer. Sharp shared the 1993 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for the discovery of split genes and RNA splicing. Techniques include CRISPR screens, high-throughput sequencing (RNA-seq, CLIP-seq), mouse models of tumor initiation, and biochemical analysis of RNP complexes.
phillip-a-sharp-koch-institute.notes: Duplicate of p-a-sharp-koch-institute; same enrichment applied.

pierre-f-j-lermusiaux-meche.l: MSEAS (Multidisciplinary Simulation, Estimation, and Assimilation Systems)
pierre-f-j-lermusiaux-meche.s: Pierre Lermusiaux's MSEAS group develops ocean modeling, data assimilation, and uncertainty quantification methods for multiscale regional ocean dynamics, coupled physical-biogeochemical systems, and autonomous underwater vehicle path planning. Projects include dynamically orthogonal field equations for stochastic PDEs, Bayesian inference for ocean state estimation, and optimal AUV routing under uncertain currents. Methods span finite-volume primitive-equation modeling, ensemble Kalman filters, and reduced-order stochastic PDE solvers.

polina-anikeeva-dmse.s: Polina Anikeeva's group engineers multifunctional multimaterial fibers and magnetic nanomaterials that interface with neurons in the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral organs for optical, electrical, and chemical recording and stimulation. Projects include thermal drawing of polymer fibers combining optical waveguides, microfluidic channels, and metal electrodes, magnetothermal neural stimulation via iron-oxide nanoparticles, and gut-brain circuit mapping. Techniques span fiber drawing, nanoparticle synthesis, optogenetics, electrophysiology, and behavioral neuroscience.

pulin-li-biology.s: Pulin Li's group combines synthetic biology, developmental biology, and quantitative biophysics to understand how genetic circuits drive multicellular communication and tissue patterning. Work includes rebuilding morphogen signaling circuits in cell culture, measuring how BMP and other ligands diffuse across tissue, and engineering synthetic cell-cell signaling modules. Methods include fluorescence time-lapse microscopy, mathematical modeling of reaction-diffusion, CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing, and single-cell RNA-seq.

r-langer-bioe.s: Robert Langer's laboratory develops polymer- and lipid-based materials for controlled drug delivery, tissue engineering, and RNA/DNA therapeutics, and is the world's largest biomedical engineering lab. Foundational work established biodegradable polyanhydride and PLGA sustained-release systems, antiangiogenesis strategies, and lipid-nanoparticle formulations underlying modern mRNA vaccines. Techniques include polymer synthesis, microfabrication, in vivo pharmacokinetics, and cell-scaffold tissue constructs.
r-langer-bioe.notes: r-langer-bioe ≡ likely dup of future robert-langer-bioe entry if it appears.

r-e-summons-eaps.s: Roger Summons's group studies molecular biosignatures in ancient rocks and modern environments, using lipid biomarkers and stable-isotope chemistry to reconstruct the co-evolution of Earth's early life and environment, mass extinction events, and the origins of fossil fuels. Summons is a co-investigator on the Sample Analysis at Mars (SAM) instrument on NASA's Curiosity rover, searching for organics in Martian sediments. Methods include GC-MS, compound-specific isotope analysis, and Precambrian field geology.

r-fletcher-physics.s: Richard Fletcher's group uses ultracold atomic gases cooled to nanokelvin temperatures to synthesize and study emergent quantum many-body phases, including artificial magnetic-field and gauge-field regimes, quantum Hall analogs, strongly correlated superfluids, and driven nonequilibrium quantum matter. Experiments trap alkali atoms in tailored optical potentials and measure in-situ density correlations and transport. Techniques include laser cooling, magneto-optical traps, Feshbach resonances, and quantum-gas microscopy.

r-kemp-nse.l: Laboratory for Nuclear Security and Policy (LNSP)
r-kemp-nse.s: Scott Kemp's Laboratory for Nuclear Security and Policy combines nuclear physics, history, and policy analysis to develop technical solutions for arms-control verification and critical-infrastructure security. Projects include cryptographically secure physical protocols for verifying warhead dismantlement without revealing design secrets, analysis of centrifuge enrichment capabilities, and assessment of hypersonic and advanced conventional weapons. Methods include Monte Carlo neutron transport, radiation-detector design, and technical-policy modeling.

r-l-jaffe-physics.s: Robert Jaffe is a theoretical nuclear and particle physicist at the MIT Center for Theoretical Physics. He co-formulated the MIT Bag Model of quark confinement, proposed the Jaffe-Ellis sum rule linking polarized lepton scattering to the nucleon spin substructure, and studies exotic hadrons, the Casimir effect in QFT, and energy policy. Methods are analytical QCD, sum-rule techniques, lattice QCD interpretation, and quantum field theory.

r-p-binzel-eaps.s: Richard Binzel's group studies asteroids and small planetary bodies through spectroscopic characterization and solar-system dynamics, including near-Earth objects, main-belt asteroids, and Pluto. Binzel invented the Torino Scale for categorizing impact hazards and contributes to NASA missions including New Horizons (Pluto), OSIRIS-REx/APEX (Bennu/Apophis), Psyche, and Lucy, and co-led MIT's REXIS X-ray imaging spectrometer. Methods include visible/near-infrared reflectance spectroscopy, orbital dynamics, and spacecraft instrument calibration.

r-van-der-hilst-eaps.s: Robert van der Hilst's group uses global seismic tomography and reflection seismology, integrated with mineral physics and geodynamics, to image Earth's deep interior, reconstruct subducted oceanic slabs, and map mantle convection patterns. Regional studies focus on the continental structure of Tibet, East Asia, and North America, and subduction under western Pacific arcs. Techniques include body-wave travel-time tomography, receiver-function imaging, and generalized Radon-transform reflection imaging.

rahul-mazumder-idss.s: Rahul Mazumder works at the intersection of statistics, machine learning, and mathematical optimization, with emphasis on sparse learning, best-subset selection, matrix completion, and large-scale discrete optimization for interpretable ML. Projects include mixed-integer-programming solvers for L0-regularized regression, scalable boosting algorithms, and applications to healthcare and credit scoring. Methods include convex and integer programming, first-order methods, and statistical learning theory.

ram-sasisekharan-bioe.s: Ram Sasisekharan's group develops analytical and enzymatic methods for sequencing complex polysaccharides (glycosaminoglycans such as heparin, heparan sulfate, and chondroitin sulfate) and applies this glycomics to therapeutic development in oncology, infectious disease, and anticoagulation. The lab famously identified oversulfated chondroitin sulfate as the contaminant behind the 2008 heparin crisis. Techniques include capillary electrophoresis, NMR spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, and heparinase-based glycan sequencing.

randall-davis-eecs.s: Randall Davis's group builds systems that interpret multimodal human input (sketching, speech, gesture) to understand designs, diagrams, and mechanical devices. Notable projects include ASSIST for sketch-based simulation of mechanical systems, the Tahuti UML recognition system, and design-rationale capture tools that record the reasoning behind engineering decisions. Methods combine pattern recognition, Bayesian networks, graph grammars, and rule-based symbolic AI.

rebecca-l-lamason-biology.s: Rebecca Lamason's group studies how intracellular bacterial pathogens such as Rickettsia parkeri and Listeria monocytogenes hijack host-cell machinery to spread between cells, using actin-based motility and membrane protrusions. Work identifies bacterial effector proteins and host factors required for cell-to-cell spread, with implications for spotted fever and listeriosis. Methods combine live-cell fluorescence microscopy, CRISPR screens, bacterial and host genetics, biochemistry, and biophysical measurements of membrane deformation.

rebecca-saxe-bcs.s: Rebecca Saxe's lab studies the neural basis of human social cognition, especially the 'theory of mind' network (including the right temporoparietal junction) that represents others' beliefs, desires, and intentions. Research examines development of social reasoning in infants and children, moral judgment, and generosity, and uses fMRI in awake infants. Methods include task-based and resting-state fMRI, behavioral experiments, computational Bayesian models of mental-state attribution, and developmental psychology.

regina-barzilay-eecs.s: Regina Barzilay's group develops machine-learning and NLP methods for drug discovery, molecular property prediction, cancer diagnostics, and clinical risk modeling. Notable projects include the discovery of the antibiotic Halicin via message-passing graph neural networks, Mirai for breast-cancer risk prediction from mammograms, and generative models for molecular design. Methods include graph neural networks, transformers, deep learning on medical imaging, and active learning.

ricardo-j-caballero-economics.s: Ricardo Caballero's research is in macroeconomics and finance, focusing on risk-centric macroeconomics, safe assets, global imbalances, aggregate investment dynamics, and creative destruction. His work with Eduardo Engel developed (S,s) models of lumpy investment and won the Frisch Medal. Methods are theoretical and empirical macro: general-equilibrium models, heterogeneous-agent dynamics, and time-series econometrics.

riccardo-comin-physics.s: Riccardo Comin's group studies quantum materials including high-temperature superconductors, charge- and spin-density waves, and topological electronic phases, using X-ray scattering and photoemission techniques. Work includes the first direct measurement of quantum geometry of electron Bloch states via angle-resolved photoemission (ARPES). Methods include resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS), ARPES, time-resolved ARPES, and thin-film synthesis of transition-metal oxides.

richard-a-young-biology.s: Richard Young's group studies transcriptional and epigenetic control of mammalian gene expression and how these controls are disrupted in cancer and other diseases. The lab discovered super-enhancers—large clusters of enhancers that drive cell-identity gene expression—and showed that transcriptional coactivators form liquid-like phase-separated condensates at active loci. Methods include ChIP-seq, Hi-C, single-molecule imaging of transcription factors, live-cell microscopy of condensates, and CRISPR-based perturbation of regulatory elements.

richard-fletcher-physics.s: Richard Fletcher's group uses ultracold atomic gases cooled to nanokelvin temperatures to synthesize and study emergent quantum many-body phases, including artificial magnetic-field and gauge-field regimes, quantum Hall analogs, strongly correlated superfluids, and driven nonequilibrium quantum matter. Experiments trap alkali atoms in tailored optical potentials and measure in-situ density correlations and transport. Techniques include laser cooling, magneto-optical traps, Feshbach resonances, and quantum-gas microscopy.
richard-fletcher-physics.notes: Duplicate of r-fletcher-physics; same enrichment applied.

richard-k-lester-nse.s: Richard Lester's research addresses innovation strategy, regional and national systems of innovation, and technology policy for energy, manufacturing, and advanced nuclear systems, especially as tools to address climate change. He founded MIT's Industrial Performance Center and previously headed the Department of Nuclear Science and Engineering. Methods are primarily qualitative: case studies, policy analysis, and techno-economic assessment.

richard-melrose-math.s: Richard Melrose works on geometric analysis, microlocal analysis, and partial differential equations, with contributions to scattering theory, pseudodifferential operators, and the b-calculus for manifolds with boundary and corners. His 1984 Bôcher Memorial Prize recognized his work on scattering theory. Methods are rigorous PDE analysis, Fourier integral operators, and index theory.

richard-p-binzel-eaps.s: Richard Binzel's group studies asteroids and small planetary bodies through spectroscopic characterization and solar-system dynamics, including near-Earth objects, main-belt asteroids, and Pluto. Binzel invented the Torino Scale for categorizing impact hazards and contributes to NASA missions including New Horizons (Pluto), OSIRIS-REx/APEX (Bennu/Apophis), Psyche, and Lucy, and co-led MIT's REXIS X-ray imaging spectrometer. Methods include visible/near-infrared reflectance spectroscopy, orbital dynamics, and spacecraft instrument calibration.
richard-p-binzel-eaps.notes: Duplicate of r-p-binzel-eaps; same enrichment applied.

richard-s-lindzen-eaps.s: Richard Lindzen is an atmospheric physicist (now emeritus) whose research covers atmospheric dynamics, planetary waves, atmospheric tides, ozone photochemistry, monsoon meteorology, hydrodynamic instability, and climate sensitivity. With Sydney Chapman he authored the standard monograph 'Atmospheric Tides.' Methods are theoretical fluid dynamics, linear wave analysis, and observational meteorology.

richard-schmalensee-economics.s: Richard Schmalensee (emeritus) works on industrial organization economics, antitrust, regulatory policy, energy and environmental policy, and the economics of multi-sided platforms. He was dean of MIT Sloan and previously directed MIT's Center for Energy and Environmental Policy Research. Methods include applied microeconomic theory, econometric analysis of market structure, and antitrust case analysis.

richard-stanley-math.s: Richard Stanley (emeritus) is a combinatorialist whose work spans enumerative combinatorics, algebraic combinatorics, symmetric functions, poset theory, and connections to commutative algebra and representation theory. He authored the two-volume standard reference 'Enumerative Combinatorics' and introduced Stanley-Reisner rings linking simplicial complexes to commutative algebra. Methods are algebraic, with generating functions, symmetric-function identities, and RSK correspondence.

richard-teague-eaps.s: Richard Teague's group uses submillimeter interferometry with ALMA to image gas and dust in protoplanetary disks around young stars, mapping temperatures, densities, chemistry, and kinematics to detect embedded protoplanets and constrain planet-formation mechanisms. He leads the exoALMA Large Program, the first sub-mm planet-hunting campaign targeting embedded planets via their dynamical perturbations on disk gas. Methods include radiative-transfer modeling, channel-map line-of-sight velocity fitting, and multi-isotopologue CO analysis.

rick-danheiser-chemistry.s: Rick Danheiser's group develops new cycloaddition and annulation strategies for organic synthesis, most notably the Danheiser benzannulation of cyclobutenones with ynol ethers or ynamides to construct highly substituted phenols, naphthalenes, indoles, benzofurans, and carbazoles in a single step. The lab applies these methods to total synthesis of natural products including anatoxin-a, mycophenolic acid, ascochlorin, and danshen diterpene quinones. Techniques span thermal pericyclic reactions, transition-metal catalysis, and intramolecular Diels–Alder methodology.

ritu-raman-meche.s: Ritu Raman's group engineers biohybrid machines that combine living skeletal-muscle and motor-neuron tissues with flexible synthetic scaffolds to build soft actuators, biological robots, and implantable therapeutics. Projects include light-stimulated muscle-powered robotic worms, artificial tendons connecting muscle actuators to robotic skeletons, and regenerative tissue constructs for neuromuscular injury. Techniques include 3D bioprinting, optogenetic muscle control, in vitro co-culture, microfabricated compliant scaffolds, and electrical stimulation.

rob-van-der-hilst-eaps.s: Robert van der Hilst's group uses global seismic tomography and reflection seismology, integrated with mineral physics and geodynamics, to image Earth's deep interior, reconstruct subducted oceanic slabs, and map mantle convection patterns. Regional studies focus on the continental structure of Tibet, East Asia, and North America, and subduction under western Pacific arcs. Techniques include body-wave travel-time tomography, receiver-function imaging, and generalized Radon-transform reflection imaging.

robert-a-weinberg-biology.s: Robert Weinberg's group pioneered cancer genetics, discovering the first human oncogene (RAS) and the first tumor suppressor gene (RB), and later transforming normal human cells into cancer cells in vitro. Current work focuses on the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) program and its role in tumor invasion, metastasis, and acquisition of cancer stem-cell traits. Methods include mouse xenograft and genetically engineered tumor models, CRISPR and shRNA perturbation, single-cell RNA-seq of intermediate E-M cancer states, and live-imaging of cell-fate transitions.

robert-d-van-der-hilst-eaps.s: Robert van der Hilst's group uses global seismic tomography and reflection seismology, integrated with mineral physics and geodynamics, to image Earth's deep interior, reconstruct subducted oceanic slabs, and map mantle convection patterns. Regional studies focus on the continental structure of Tibet, East Asia, and North America, and subduction under western Pacific arcs. Techniques include body-wave travel-time tomography, receiver-function imaging, and generalized Radon-transform reflection imaging.

robert-desimone-bcs.s: Robert Desimone's group studies neural mechanisms of visual attention and cognitive control in primates and humans, showing that attention selectively amplifies task-relevant neural activity and synchronizes it into gamma-band oscillations while suppressing distractor signals. Work implicates prefrontal cortex as the top-down controller orchestrating attentional selection across sensory cortices. Methods include multi-electrode extracellular recording in behaving macaques, local field potential/LFP coherence analysis, and human fMRI/MEG.

robert-g-griffin-chemistry.l: Griffin Group / Francis Bitter Magnet Laboratory
robert-g-griffin-chemistry.s: Robert Griffin's group develops and applies high-field dynamic nuclear polarization (DNP) combined with magic-angle-spinning (MAS) solid-state NMR to determine the structure and dynamics of membrane proteins, amyloid fibrils, and biological solids. The lab pioneered gyrotron microwave sources for high-frequency DNP and designed biradical polarizing agents such as TOTAPOL and AMUPol. Techniques include gyrotron-driven DNP, cryogenic MAS probes, multidimensional correlation NMR, and isotope-labeled protein biochemistry.

robert-j-gilliard-chemistry.s: Robert Gilliard's group does synthetic main-group inorganic and organometallic chemistry, using N-heterocyclic carbene ligands to stabilize low-valent boron, beryllium, magnesium, and group-13/14 species for small-molecule activation and stimuli-responsive materials. Projects include carbene-supported borylenes, magnesium-based reversible hydrogen storage, CO2 reduction by carbene/alkali-metal systems, and boron-doped polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons as luminescent materials. Methods include air-free Schlenk/glovebox synthesis, X-ray crystallography, and photophysical characterization.

robert-langer-bioe.s: Robert Langer's laboratory develops polymer- and lipid-based materials for controlled drug delivery, tissue engineering, and RNA/DNA therapeutics, and is the world's largest biomedical engineering lab. Foundational work established biodegradable polyanhydride and PLGA sustained-release systems, antiangiogenesis strategies, and lipid-nanoparticle formulations underlying modern mRNA vaccines. Techniques include polymer synthesis, microfabrication, in vivo pharmacokinetics, and cell-scaffold tissue constructs.
robert-langer-bioe.notes: Duplicate of r-langer-bioe; same enrichment applied.

robert-v-gibbons-economics.s: Robert Gibbons works on organizational economics, relational contracts, and the economics of incentives inside and between firms, with applications to firm boundaries, alliances, and persistent productivity differences across otherwise-similar organizations. He authored the canonical graduate textbook 'Game Theory for Applied Economists.' Methods combine game theory, contract theory, and applied microeconomics.
robert-v-gibbons-economics.notes: labs.json lists 'Robert V. Gibbons' but the MIT Sloan faculty name is Robert S. Gibbons; name fix applied.

robert-w-field-chemistry.s: Robert Field's group performs high-resolution laser and microwave molecular spectroscopy of gas-phase polyatomic molecules to probe highly excited vibrational and electronic states, Rydberg states, and intramolecular dynamics including isomerization and energy flow. Techniques include stimulated emission pumping (SEP) pump-dump spectroscopy (invented in his lab), buffer-gas-cooled molecular beams, and millimeter-wave detection of Rydberg-Rydberg transitions. The group develops effective Hamiltonians and pattern-recognition methods to extract dynamical information from dense spectra.

roger-d-kamm-bioe.s: Roger Kamm's group pioneered mechanobiology and 3D organ-on-chip microfluidic models of human tissues, focusing on vascular function, the blood-brain barrier, neurological disease, and steps of the cancer metastatic cascade including EMT, migration, intravasation, and extravasation. The lab builds perfusable microvasculature-on-chip with hierarchical vessel networks for drug screening and disease modeling. Techniques include soft lithography, 3D co-culture of endothelial and parenchymal cells, time-lapse fluorescence imaging, and fluid-structure mechanical modeling.

roger-e-summons-eaps.s: Roger Summons's group studies molecular biosignatures in ancient rocks and modern environments, using lipid biomarkers and stable-isotope chemistry to reconstruct the co-evolution of Earth's early life and environment, mass extinction events, and the origins of fossil fuels. Summons is a co-investigator on the Sample Analysis at Mars (SAM) instrument on NASA's Curiosity rover, searching for organics in Martian sediments. Methods include GC-MS, compound-specific isotope analysis, and Precambrian field geology.
roger-e-summons-eaps.notes: Duplicate of r-e-summons-eaps; same enrichment applied.

rogier-braakman-eaps.s: Rogier Braakman's group reconstructs the evolution of cellular metabolism over geological timescales and studies marine microbial cross-feeding networks, focusing on how Prochlorococcus and SAR11 bacteria exchange metabolites in the ocean carbon cycle. Work integrates comparative genomics, metagenomics of global seawater samples, stoichiometric flux modeling, and biogeochemistry. Methods include genome-scale metabolic reconstruction, phylogenomics, and isotope-labeled metabolite tracing.
