roman-bezrukavnikov-math.s: Roman Bezrukavnikov works on geometric representation theory and algebraic geometry, with central contributions to the geometric Langlands program, the representation theory of semisimple Lie algebras and affine Hecke algebras in positive characteristic, and noncommutative counterparts of the Springer resolution. Methods are algebraic: derived categories of coherent sheaves, D-modules, perverse sheaves, and quantum groups at roots of unity.

ron-weiss-bioe.l: Weiss Lab / MIT Synthetic Biology Center
ron-weiss-bioe.s: Ron Weiss's lab designs and builds synthetic gene networks in bacteria, yeast, and mammalian cells, applying computer-engineering principles of abstraction and composition to program cell behavior with analog and digital logic circuits. Projects include mammalian transcriptional classifiers for cancer cell-type detection, RNA-only circuits with self-amplifying replicons, organoid patterning via engineered signaling, and programmable mRNA therapeutics (STX-001 in clinical trials). Methods include standardized DNA assembly, high-throughput flow cytometry characterization, and dynamical modeling of gene networks.

ronald-g-prinn-eaps.l: Center for Global Change Science / AGAGE
ronald-g-prinn-eaps.s: Ronald Prinn leads the Advanced Global Atmospheric Gases Experiment (AGAGE), a global network continuously monitoring concentrations of greenhouse gases, ozone-depleting substances, and other trace atmospheric species, and directs MIT's Center for Global Change Science. His group uses inverse modeling with 3D atmospheric transport models to estimate regional emissions, and co-developed the MIT Integrated Global System Model coupling economics, atmospheric chemistry, climate physics, and ecosystems. Methods include GC-MS of atmospheric samples, Bayesian inverse modeling, and integrated assessment modeling.

ronald-t-raines-chemistry.s: Ronald Raines's group studies protein chemistry and chemical biology, with programs on ribonucleases as anticancer agents, the structure and stability of collagen (including synthetic collagens stronger than natural ones), and unappreciated stabilizing forces in proteins (n→π* interaction, C5 hydrogen bond). Projects include human RNase-based cancer therapeutics in clinical trials, protein-folding catalysts, and lignocellulosic biomass conversion to chemicals. Techniques include peptide/protein synthesis, X-ray crystallography, enzymology, and fluorescence spectroscopy.

ruben-juanes-cee.s: Ruben Juanes's group studies multiphase flow through porous media combining theory, pore-scale microfluidic experiments, and large-scale computational simulation, with applications to geologic carbon sequestration, methane hydrates, groundwater hydrology, and induced seismicity. The lab uses 3D-printed micromodels for visualizing immiscible displacement and develops continuum and network models of capillarity-driven flow instabilities. Methods include finite-element multiphase simulation, stability analysis, and optical flow imaging.

rubén-juanes-cee.s: Ruben Juanes's group studies multiphase flow through porous media combining theory, pore-scale microfluidic experiments, and large-scale computational simulation, with applications to geologic carbon sequestration, methane hydrates, groundwater hydrology, and induced seismicity. The lab uses 3D-printed micromodels for visualizing immiscible displacement and develops continuum and network models of capillarity-driven flow instabilities. Methods include finite-element multiphase simulation, stability analysis, and optical flow imaging.
rubén-juanes-cee.notes: Duplicate of ruben-juanes-cee (one uses accent, one doesn't).

rudolf-jaenisch-biology.s: Rudolf Jaenisch's group uses mouse models and human pluripotent stem cells (ES and iPS) to study epigenetic regulation of development and model neurological and metabolic diseases including Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, and autism. The lab produced the first transgenic animals, made foundational contributions to DNA-methylation biology, and was among the first to reprogram somatic cells to iPSCs and demonstrate therapeutic use in sickle-cell and Parkinson's disease models. Methods include somatic cell nuclear transfer, CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing, bisulfite sequencing, and neural differentiation protocols.

russ-tedrake-meche.s: Russ Tedrake's group develops rigorous planning and control algorithms for dynamic legged locomotion, aerial vehicles, and dexterous manipulation, combining nonlinear optimization, Lyapunov-based stability analysis, sum-of-squares programming, and deep learning for robotic manipulation. Projects include the Atlas humanoid competing in the DARPA Robotics Challenge and diffusion-policy manipulation learning. Methods span trajectory optimization, contact-implicit planning, and formal verification of neural-network controllers.

ruth-lehmann-biology.s: Ruth Lehmann's group uses Drosophila to study germline specification, migration, and maintenance, identifying the genetic networks that set aside germ cells in the embryo and enable them to transmit totipotency across generations. Work illuminates how germ granules (polar granules) assemble, how primordial germ cells migrate to the gonad, and how lipid and RNA metabolism protect germline identity. Methods include forward and reverse Drosophila genetics, germline-specific RNAi, transcriptomics, and live fluorescence imaging of migrating germ cells.

róbert-langer-bioe.s: Robert Langer's laboratory develops polymer- and lipid-based materials for controlled drug delivery, tissue engineering, and RNA/DNA therapeutics, and is the world's largest biomedical engineering lab. Foundational work established biodegradable polyanhydride and PLGA sustained-release systems, antiangiogenesis strategies, and lipid-nanoparticle formulations underlying modern mRNA vaccines. Techniques include polymer synthesis, microfabrication, in vivo pharmacokinetics, and cell-scaffold tissue constructs.
róbert-langer-bioe.notes: Accented 'Róbert Langer' is the same as Robert Langer — third dup (with r-langer-bioe and robert-langer-bioe).

s-r-tannenbaum-chemistry.s: Steven Tannenbaum's laboratory studies the biological chemistry of nitric oxide and its oxidation products, discovering endogenous mammalian nitric-oxide synthesis and its links to inflammation and cancer. The lab develops high-sensitivity mass-spectrometric and laser-induced fluorescence assays for DNA adducts and biomarkers of oxidative/nitrosative stress. Techniques include LC-MS/MS at sub-femtomole sensitivity, stable-isotope dilution, and toxicology of N-nitroso compounds.

s-t-ceyer-chemistry.s: Sylvia Ceyer's group studies the dynamics and mechanisms of gas-surface chemical reactions on metal and semiconductor surfaces using molecular-beam scattering under ultra-high vacuum, with applications to heterogeneous catalysis and thin-film growth. The lab discovered 'chemistry with a hammer' (collision-induced reactions), atom abstraction mechanisms, and demonstrated that hydrogen buried beneath a Ni surface—not surface-adsorbed H—is the active species in ethylene hydrogenation. Techniques include supersonic molecular beams, HREELS, TPD, and Auger spectroscopy.

s-vitale-physics.s: Salvatore Vitale works on gravitational-wave astrophysics with the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration, developing data-analysis algorithms to detect and characterize compact-object mergers and using populations of gravitational-wave events to probe black-hole and neutron-star formation channels and to test general relativity in the strong-field regime. He co-developed a low-latency unmodeled-signal search that helped discover GW150914, the first direct detection. Methods include Bayesian inference, parameter estimation via MCMC/nested sampling, and hierarchical population modeling.

sallie-w-chisholm-biology.s: The Chisholm Lab studies Prochlorococcus, the most abundant photosynthetic organism on Earth and a dominant primary producer in open-ocean gyres. Research combines comparative and population genomics, single-cell sorting, flow cytometry, metatranscriptomics, and chemostat culturing to dissect the ecology, evolution, and diversification of marine cyanobacteria and their viruses. The group also characterizes extracellular vesicles released by Prochlorococcus and integrates ocean field sampling with laboratory physiology to link cell-scale traits to global biogeochemical cycles.

saman-amarasinghe-eecs.s: The Commit group develops compilers, programming languages, and runtime systems that make high-performance code easier to write and optimize. Projects include Halide (for image and array processing), TACO (for sparse tensor algebra), StreamIt, Simit, Ithemal, and OpenTuner, combining domain-specific languages, autoscheduling, autotuning, and machine-learning-based performance prediction. The group targets CPUs, GPUs, and specialized accelerators, and studies parallelization, vectorization, locality optimization, and program synthesis for scientific and graphics workloads.

sangeeta-n-bhatia-eecs.s: The Bhatia lab engineers miniaturized human tissues and nanomaterials to study and treat liver disease, cancer, and infectious disease. Methods include micropatterned co-cultures of primary hepatocytes, bioprinted and microfabricated liver models, humanized mouse models for hepatitis and malaria, and synthetic biomarkers based on protease-activated peptide nanoparticles read out via urine mass spectrometry. The group also develops tumor-targeted nanoparticles, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and activity-based nanosensors for early cancer detection.

sara-beery-eecs.s: The Beery lab develops computer vision and machine learning methods for ecological and environmental monitoring at global scale. Research addresses domain generalization, long-tailed recognition, and weak supervision on camera-trap imagery, aerial and satellite remote sensing, acoustic data, and citizen-science photos, supporting species classification, individual re-identification, and population estimation. The group partners with conservation organizations on datasets like iWildCam and Caltech Camera Traps and integrates geospatial foundation models with ecological workflows.

satrajit-ghosh-mcgovern.s: The Sensein group develops computational tools, open data standards, and machine-learning models for studying human brain function, mental health, and speech. Work spans fMRI and structural MRI analysis pipelines (Nipype, Nipy), voice and speech biomarker extraction for depression and neurological conditions, and federated data ecosystems like OpenNeuro and DataLad for reproducible neuroimaging. Projects combine deep learning on audio and neuroimaging with ontologies, provenance tracking, and cloud workflows.

saurabh-amin-cee.s: The RESIL lab studies the security, reliability, and economics of networked infrastructure systems including transportation, water, power, and supply-chain networks. Methods combine game theory, stochastic control, mechanism design, network optimization, and reinforcement learning to analyze attacker–defender interactions, cascading failures, and incentive-aligned response strategies. Projects include cyber-physical security of SCADA-controlled water systems, traffic routing under strategic users, and data-driven resilience metrics for critical infrastructure.

scott-r-manalis-bioe.s: The Manalis lab builds suspended microchannel resonators (SMRs) that weigh single cells with femtogram precision to measure buoyant mass, density, and growth rate. The group uses serial SMR arrays to track how individual cancer cells grow and respond to drugs, and develops mass-based biomarkers predicting treatment response in patient-derived tumor samples and immune cells. Techniques span MEMS fabrication, microfluidics, single-cell biophysics, and integration with sequencing and proteomics readouts.

sebastian-lourido-biology.s: The Lourido lab studies the cell biology and genetics of apicomplexan parasites, especially Toxoplasma gondii and Plasmodium. The group pioneered genome-wide CRISPR/Cas9 screens in Toxoplasma to define essential genes and identify parasite-specific drug targets, and dissects calcium signaling, motility, and host-cell invasion using conditional knockouts, proximity labeling, and live imaging. Work integrates parasite genetics, biochemistry, and chemical biology to uncover vulnerabilities in eukaryotic pathogens.

semyon-dyatlov-math.s: Dyatlov is a mathematician working on microlocal analysis, scattering theory, and quantum chaos. Research topics include resonances for open quantum and classical systems, decay of correlations for Anosov flows, and the fractal uncertainty principle, which connects harmonic analysis to delocalization of eigenfunctions on negatively curved manifolds. Techniques draw on semiclassical analysis, dynamical systems, hyperbolic geometry, and partial differential equations.

sendhil-mullainathan-eecs.s: Mullainathan works at the intersection of machine learning, behavioral economics, and public policy. Research uses supervised learning, interpretable models, and causal inference on large administrative datasets to study algorithmic bias in healthcare and criminal-justice risk scores, judicial decision-making, and the psychology of scarcity. He co-founded J-PAL and applies field experiments, prediction-policy frameworks, and econometric methods to questions in poverty, labor, and discrimination.

sergey-ovchinnikov-biology.s: The Ovchinnikov lab develops machine-learning methods for protein structure prediction, design, and evolution. Work includes coevolution-based contact prediction (GREMLIN), protein hallucination and sequence design using AlphaFold and RoseTTAFold gradients, and metagenomic sequence analysis to discover novel protein families and enzymes. The group combines deep learning, statistical physics of sequence landscapes, and structural bioinformatics to connect sequence, structure, and function.

sertaç-karaman-eecs.s: The Karaman group studies planning, perception, and control for autonomous vehicles, drones, and other robotic systems. Research develops sampling-based motion planners (RRT*, PRM*), energy-efficient visual-inertial SLAM, event-camera and LIDAR perception pipelines, and high-speed agile flight for micro aerial vehicles. The group also designs custom low-power navigation chips ('Navion') and combines optimal control, differential privacy, and deep learning for autonomy at the edge.

seth-lloyd-meche.s: Lloyd is a pioneer of quantum computing, quantum information, and quantum machine learning. Research includes the design of feasible quantum computer architectures, quantum algorithms for linear algebra and data analysis (HHL, quantum PCA, quantum SVM), quantum annealing, quantum control, and the thermodynamics of computation. The group also studies quantum sensing, quantum communication, and the role of information in physical and biological systems.

seychelle-m-vos-biology.s: The Vos lab uses structural biology to understand how transcription by RNA polymerase II is coupled to chromatin organization, elongation control, and DNA damage responses. Methods include cryo-electron microscopy and cryo-ET of reconstituted transcription complexes, biochemical reconstitution of Pol II with elongation factors and nucleosomes, and single-particle analysis of bacterial condensin-like SMC complexes such as MukBEF. The group connects atomic-level mechanism to gene regulation and genome organization.

shafi-goldwasser-math.l: Cryptography and Information Security Group (MIT CSAIL)
shafi-goldwasser-math.s: Goldwasser is a foundational figure in modern cryptography and complexity theory. Her group works on probabilistic encryption, zero-knowledge proofs, secure multi-party computation, interactive and probabilistically checkable proof systems, and the theory of pseudorandomness, with current emphasis on privacy-preserving machine learning, verifiable computation, and delegated computation in the cloud. The group also studies number-theoretic algorithms and the complexity of approximation.

sherrie-wang-meche.s: The Earth Intelligence Lab develops machine learning methods that turn satellite imagery, aerial photos, LiDAR, and crowdsourced ground data into measurements of crops, water, and land use at global scale. Research targets weak supervision, domain adaptation, and self-supervised learning to handle limited labels and noisy multimodal sensor data, with applications to crop type and yield mapping, smallholder farm monitoring, irrigation detection, and climate-related land change. Projects emphasize low- and middle-income regions and connect to sustainable development and food security.

shu-heng-shao-physics.s: Shao is a theoretical physicist working on the structure of quantum field theories, lattice systems, and string theory. His research has developed the framework of generalized global symmetries and 't Hooft anomalies, including non-invertible symmetries that lack inverses, and applies these tools to constrain renormalization group flows, classify phases of quantum matter, and probe naturalness in the Standard Model. Examples include the Ising model, Yang–Mills theories, fracton models, and 4d gauge theories.

shuhei-ono-eaps.s: The Ono lab uses non-traditional stable isotope systems—including multiple sulfur isotopes and methane clumped isotopes—to study microbial metabolism, the deep biosphere, hydrothermal systems, and Earth's atmospheric history. The group combines high-precision isotope-ratio mass spectrometry, tunable infrared laser direct absorption spectroscopy (TILDAS) for portable methane isotopologue measurements, laboratory cultures of methanogens and sulfate reducers, and field sampling from seafloor and continental subsurface sites. Work bridges geochemistry, microbiology, and atmospheric science.

sili-deng-meche.s: The Deng group studies combustion, chemical kinetics, and flame-based synthesis of nanomaterials. Research combines laser diagnostics, high-speed imaging, and direct numerical simulation of reacting flows to study flame instabilities, pollutant formation, and metal fuel combustion as carbon-free energy carriers. The group also uses flame spray pyrolysis to produce battery cathode materials and catalysts at high throughput, linking combustion science to electrochemical energy storage and conversion.

siniša-hrvatin-biology.s: The Hrvatin lab studies the biology of mammalian torpor, hibernation, and other states of stasis as a window into how cells and tissues survive extreme metabolic and temperature stress. Work combines mouse and Syrian hamster models, AAV-based neural circuit manipulation, single-cell RNA sequencing, and chemogenetics to map hypothalamic neurons (such as avMLPA) that drive entry into torpor, and to define cell-intrinsic adaptations to cold. The group also uses tardigrade and cell-culture systems to dissect cryoprotective mechanisms.

soonwon-choi-physics.s: The Choi group is a theoretical research group studying out-of-equilibrium quantum many-body dynamics and quantum information science. Topics include measurement-induced entanglement phase transitions, scrambling and quantum chaos, classical shadow tomography, and learning algorithms for analog quantum simulators built from Rydberg-atom arrays, trapped ions, and superconducting qubits. The group connects condensed-matter many-body physics with quantum computation, sensing, and benchmarking.

srinivas-devadas-eecs.s: Devadas leads work on secure computer architecture, applied cryptography, and hardware security. The group invented Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) for device authentication and key generation, designed the Aegis and Sanctum secure processors with hardware enclaves, and developed encrypted-computation processors and oblivious RAM constructions. Research also covers cache modeling, hardware information-flow tracking, and provably secure architectures resistant to side-channel attacks.

stefani-spranger-biology.s: The Spranger lab studies how T cell immunity recognizes—and frequently fails to recognize—solid tumors, with a focus on lung, ovarian, pancreatic, and melanoma cancers. The group uses genetically engineered mouse models that mimic human tumor progression, dendritic cell biology, single-cell transcriptomics, and TCR repertoire analysis to map how tumors exclude or co-opt cytotoxic T cells, and to identify dendritic cell subsets and signaling pathways (e.g., Wnt/β-catenin, type I IFN) that determine response to checkpoint immunotherapy.

stephen-bates-eecs.s: Bates works on statistical methods for uncertainty quantification and reliable machine learning. Research focuses on conformal prediction, distribution-free inference, calibration of black-box predictors, prediction-powered inference, and false-discovery-rate control for large-scale hypothesis testing. Applications include safe deployment of ML in medicine and the sciences, AI evaluation, and decision-making under uncertainty.

stephen-j-lippard-chemistry.s: The Lippard lab is a foundational bioinorganic chemistry group studying the interactions of metal ions with biological molecules. Major research lines include the mechanism of cisplatin and the design of new platinum, osmium, and rhenium anti-cancer agents that target DNA and cancer stem cells; structural and mechanistic studies of metalloenzymes such as soluble methane monooxygenase and ribonucleotide reductase; and fluorescent metal-ion sensors for detecting mobile zinc and nitric oxide in neurons. Methods span synthesis, X-ray crystallography, spectroscopy, and cell biology.

stephen-l-buchwald-chemistry.s: The Buchwald group develops palladium- and copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions for forming carbon–nitrogen, carbon–oxygen, and carbon–carbon bonds, including the Buchwald–Hartwig amination. The group designs dialkylbiaryl phosphine ligands ('Buchwald ligands') and palladacycle precatalysts that enable efficient reactions of aryl chlorides, fluoroalkylation, and late-stage functionalization of pharmaceuticals. Mechanistic studies use kinetics, NMR, and isolated oxidative addition complexes to guide ligand design.

stephen-morris-economics.s: Morris is an economic theorist specializing in game theory with incomplete information and the role of higher-order beliefs. His research developed the theory of global games, where players observe noisy signals of an underlying state, and used it to study coordination failures, currency crises, and bank runs; he also works on information design, Bayes correlated equilibrium, and robust mechanism design. The work connects pure game theory to macroeconomics and finance.

stephen-p-bell-biology.s: The Bell lab studies the initiation of eukaryotic DNA replication using budding yeast as the principal model. Research dissects how the six-subunit Origin Recognition Complex (ORC) selects origins of replication, how the Mcm2-7 replicative helicase is loaded and activated to form the CMG complex, and how nucleosome positioning and chromatin context regulate origin licensing. The group pioneered single-molecule fluorescence assays of reconstituted replisome assembly and combines biochemistry, genetics, and genome-wide mapping of replication intermediates.

steven-g-johnson-physics.s: Johnson develops computational methods for nanophotonics, electromagnetism, and wave phenomena in complex geometries. He co-created the FFTW library (Fastest Fourier Transform in the West), the Meep FDTD Maxwell-equation solver, and MPB for photonic band-structure calculation, and contributes to the Julia scientific-computing ecosystem. Research spans photonic crystals, inverse design of optical devices, fast algorithms for PDEs, and the mathematics of Casimir forces and slow light.

steven-r-tannenbaum-chemistry.s: The Tannenbaum lab works at the interface of chemistry, toxicology, and biological engineering. Major research themes include the pathophysiology of nitric oxide, development of biomarkers for inflammation and carcinogen metabolism, tissue-engineered in-vitro models for drug-toxicity screening, and quantitative ultramicro mass spectrometry for metabolite profiling. The group contributed to the discovery of endogenous nitrogen-oxide synthesis and develops organ-on-chip platforms for drug evaluation.

steven-w-flavell-bcs.s: The Flavell lab uses C. elegans to map how neural circuits generate persistent behavioral states such as roaming, dwelling, and feeding. The group combines brain-wide calcium imaging of the entire 302-neuron nervous system, optogenetics, single-cell RNA sequencing, and quantitative behavioral tracking to chart the neuromodulatory (serotonergic, dopaminergic) circuits that control arousal, motivation, and locomotory state transitions. Work provides a complete wiring-diagram-level understanding linking genes, circuits, and behavior.

sungjae-kim-meche.notes: No MIT MechE faculty named Sungjae Kim found. MechE has Sangbae Kim (Biomimetic Robotics) and Sang-Gook Kim (MEMS), neither matching. Likely mislabeled student or postdoc. Recommend delete.

suraj-cheema-eecs.s: The Cheema lab engineers atomic-scale ferroelectric heterostructures in fluorite-structure oxides (HfO₂, ZrO₂) to create new paradigms for energy-efficient microelectronics. Research demonstrated negative capacitance, ultrathin ferroelectric tunnel junctions, and record-breaking polarization in sub-nanometer films grown directly on silicon, targeting ultra-low-power transistors, fast energy storage, and energy-harvesting devices. Methods include atomic-layer deposition, TEM, piezoresponse force microscopy, and integration into foundry CMOS processes.

susan-hockfield-koch-institute.s: Hockfield uses monoclonal antibody technology and molecular neuroscience to study brain development and brain-cancer invasion. Her group identified BEHAB/brevican, a membrane-associated chondroitin sulfate proteoglycan whose tumor-specific splice variant and ADAMTS-mediated cleavage promote invasive glioma spread, and characterized other extracellular-matrix glycoproteins that regulate neuronal migration, axon guidance, and cortical patterning during development. Work bridges neural stem-cell biology with glioblastoma pathology.

susan-solomon-chemistry.s: The Solomon group studies chemistry–climate coupling, focusing on how stratospheric ozone depletion and greenhouse-gas forcing interact to alter atmospheric circulation, surface climate, and precipitation patterns at both poles and in the tropics. Solomon led the 1986–87 National Ozone Expedition that confirmed the CFC mechanism for the Antarctic ozone hole, co-chaired the IPCC AR4 physical-science assessment, and her current work uses satellite, reanalysis, and chemistry-climate model data to quantify the roles of volcanic aerosols, short-lived climate forcers, and mitigation pathways.

susumu-tonegawa-biology.s: Tonegawa won the 1987 Nobel Prize for discovering the genetic mechanism of antibody diversity (V(D)J recombination) and subsequently redirected his research to the neural basis of memory. Using optogenetics, engram-labeling with c-Fos-driven channelrhodopsin in mouse hippocampus, and functional connectomics, his lab demonstrated that specific dentate-gyrus cell ensembles store individual memories and can be artificially reactivated or suppressed to create, modify, or erase fear and reward memories. Current work extends engram biology to social memory, depression, and Alzheimer's models.

t-a-herring-eaps.s: Herring develops and applies space-geodetic techniques—GPS, VLBI, and InSAR—for millimeter-precision measurements of Earth's deformation, plate motion, and rotation. He created the GAMIT/GLOBK GPS processing software suite widely used for tectonic and geodynamic studies, and his group uses these tools to measure postseismic relaxation, ice-sheet loading, sea-level change, and tropospheric water vapor. Work contributed early definitive VLBI evidence of plate tectonics and continuous GPS monitoring of fault-zone strain.

t-leighton-math.s: Leighton is an applied mathematician and theoretical computer scientist who co-founded Akamai Technologies based on content-delivery algorithms developed at MIT. His research spans graph algorithms, consistent hashing, network routing, combinatorial optimization, parallel architectures, and cryptography, with over 100 publications and numerous patents on distributed-computing infrastructure. He also teaches widely on discrete mathematics and algorithms (Introduction to Algorithms, Mathematics for Computer Science).

t-r-akylas-meche.s: Akylas studies nonlinear wave propagation in fluids, including gravity-capillary solitary waves ('lumps'), internal gravity wave beams in stratified flows, and resonant wave–wave interactions. His work uses asymptotic analysis, perturbation methods, and direct numerical simulation to investigate envelope solitons, wavepacket–long-wave resonance, mountain gravity-wave generation, and flow instabilities in rotating and stratified systems. He also teaches graduate-level nonlinear dynamics and waves at MIT MechE.

tal-cohen-meche.s: The Cohen group studies the nonlinear mechanics of soft materials, including instabilities, cavitation, shock-wave propagation, and growth-induced deformation. Research combines continuum mechanics theory, finite-element simulation, and experiments on elastomers, gels, and biological tissues to understand phenomena such as dynamic void growth, torsion-mediated buckling, and the mechanics of cell motility and muscle shock absorption. Applications range from understanding traumatic brain injury to engineering failure-resistant soft composites.

tami-d-lieberman-cee.s: The Lieberman lab studies within-person bacterial evolution and the assembly of the human skin and gut microbiome. The group sequences whole genomes of cultured bacterial isolates from longitudinal human samples to track adaptive mutations, horizontal gene transfer, and strain-replacement events over months to decades, using new computational phylogenetic and population-genetics methods. Work reveals how commensal bacteria evolve in situ to evade immunity, metabolize host-derived nutrients, and stably colonize niche-specific body sites.

tania-a-baker-biology.s: The Baker lab studies the mechanism and regulation of AAA+ protein machines, principally the ClpXP protease that unfolds and degrades targeted substrates in bacteria using ATP-powered mechanical work. Research uses single-molecule enzymology, cryo-EM, biochemical reconstitution, and bacterial genetics to dissect how ClpX recognizes degron tags (ssrA, SspB adaptor), translocates polypeptides through its central pore, and coordinates with the ClpP peptidase. The group also studies DNA transposition by Mu transposase and the regulation of protein quality control in stress responses.

tanja-bosak-eaps.s: The Bosak lab studies microbial life and its geological record through laboratory experiments, field sedimentology, and stable-isotope geochemistry. Research recreates stromatolite and microbialite formation in the lab using anoxygenic and cyanobacterial mats, measures sulfur- and carbon-isotope fractionation by living microbes, and examines the morphology and chemistry of Archean–Proterozoic biosignatures to constrain early Earth environments. The group also contributes to Mars sample-return astrobiology through analysis of Jezero crater samples.

themistoklis-p-sapsis-meche.l: Stochastic Analysis and Nonlinear Dynamics Lab (SAND)
themistoklis-p-sapsis-meche.s: The SAND lab develops analytical, computational, and data-driven methods for predicting extreme events and quantifying uncertainty in high-dimensional nonlinear systems. Applications include rogue ocean waves, turbulent fluid flows, ship capsize, and extreme weather under climate change, using reduced-order stochastic models, rare-event sampling, extreme-value theory, and physics-informed machine learning. The group also studies energy transfer in multi-degree-of-freedom mechanical oscillators and Lagrangian mixing of finite-size particles.

thomas-love-peacock-meche.s: The ENDLab studies environmental fluid dynamics through field campaigns, laboratory experiments, and numerical modeling, with emphasis on internal waves, tidal dynamics, and Lagrangian transport structures in the ocean. The group's pioneering experiments revealed the physics of large-amplitude internal solitary waves in the Luzon Strait and elsewhere, developed laboratory analogs of oceanic stratified flows, and identified coherent structures controlling deep-ocean mixing and pollutant dispersion. Current projects include the environmental fluid mechanics of deep-sea mining plumes.
thomas-love-peacock-meche.notes: Dup of thomas-peacock-meche. Same person — Thomas Peacock (MechE / ENDLab).

thomas-peacock-meche.s: The ENDLab studies environmental fluid dynamics through field campaigns, laboratory experiments, and numerical modeling, with emphasis on internal waves, tidal dynamics, and Lagrangian transport structures in the ocean. The group's pioneering experiments revealed the physics of large-amplitude internal solitary waves in the Luzon Strait and elsewhere, developed laboratory analogs of oceanic stratified flows, and identified coherent structures controlling deep-ocean mixing and pollutant dispersion. Current projects include the environmental fluid mechanics of deep-sea mining plumes.

thomas-schwartz-biology.s: The Schwartz lab studies the structure and function of the nuclear pore complex (NPC), the ~50 MDa gateway that governs all macromolecular transport between the cytoplasm and nucleus. Using X-ray crystallography, cryo-EM, and biochemical reconstitution of subcomplexes such as Nup107–Nup133, the group has determined atomic structures of many NPC building blocks and established the evolutionary link between the NPC scaffold and vesicle coat proteins. Current work addresses how specific cargo classes are transported, cell-type-specific NPC variation, and viral hijacking of nuclear import.

tim-kraska-eecs.s: The DSAIL group builds database systems that leverage machine learning to replace or augment traditional data-structure internals. Flagship contributions include learned index structures (replacing B-trees with neural nets for up to 70× memory savings), SageDB and Tsunami for adaptive storage layouts, and ML-driven query optimization. The group also co-directs MIT's Generative AI Impact Consortium and the Everest@CSAIL initiative, applying similar ML-for-systems ideas to data cleaning, approximate query processing, and cloud-scale data infrastructure.

timothy-f-jamison-chemistry.s: The Jamison group develops new reactions and technologies for organic synthesis, with a major emphasis on continuous-flow chemistry and nickel-catalyzed C–C and C–heteroatom bond formation. Continuous-flow platforms enable multistep telescoped syntheses of pharmaceuticals (e.g., rufinamide, atropine, lidocaine) without intermediate purification, and the group's work on NHC- and phosphine-ligated nickel(0)-catalyzed reductive couplings and epoxide-opening cascades provides enantioselective routes to complex natural products. Methods combine organocatalysis, transition-metal catalysis, and reactor engineering.

timothy-m-swager-chemistry.s: The Swager group designs functional conjugated polymers, carbon nanomaterials, and liquid crystals for chemical sensing, electronics, and energy applications. The group invented amplifying fluorescent polymers that detect trace explosive vapors (TNT, TATP) in handheld 'Fido' sensors deployed in conflict zones, and develops iptycene-based molecular scaffolds, carbon-nanotube chemistors, and RFID chemical sensors. Methods span organic and polymer synthesis, thin-film fabrication, spectroscopy, and device integration.

tobias-colding-math.s: Colding is a geometric analyst who, often with collaborator William Minicozzi, has proved foundational results on Ricci-curvature convergence of manifolds, the structure of minimal surfaces and their singularities, and the classification of singularities of mean curvature flow. The Colding–Minicozzi theory established that generic singularities of mean curvature flow in R³ are spheres or cylinders, settling a long-standing conjecture. Methods include comparison geometry, monotonicity formulas, entropy-based uniqueness arguments, and analysis on manifolds.

tomasz-mrowka-math.s: Mrowka is a low-dimensional topologist and gauge theorist who, primarily with Peter Kronheimer, developed the theory of Kronheimer–Mrowka basic classes for Donaldson invariants, proved the Thom conjecture using Seiberg–Witten invariants, and constructed monopole Floer homology to prove the Property P conjecture for knots. These tools relate the topology of 3- and 4-manifolds to solutions of non-linear partial differential equations (anti-self-dual Yang–Mills, Seiberg–Witten monopole equations). Methods span gauge theory, symplectic geometry, and low-dimensional topology.

tonio-buonassisi-meche.l: Buonassisi Lab / Accelerated Materials Lab for Sustainability (AMLS)
tonio-buonassisi-meche.s: The Buonassisi lab combines defect engineering, advanced characterization, and machine-learning-accelerated materials discovery to improve photovoltaic performance and lower manufacturing costs. Research addresses silicon defect physics (iron, transition-metal precipitates), perovskite stability for tandem solar cells, and physics-informed Bayesian optimization of crystal-growth and processing conditions. The group co-leads the ADDEPT Center on durable semi-transparent perovskites and uses technoeconomic analysis to guide research priorities toward terawatt-scale solar deployment.

tracy-r-slatyer-physics.s: Slatyer is a theoretical particle astrophysicist who uses cosmological and astrophysical data to constrain the particle nature of dark matter. Research develops precision predictions for indirect dark-matter signals—gamma rays, CMB spectral distortions, 21-cm absorption, and cosmic-ray positrons—from annihilation or decay, and models energy injection into the intergalactic medium from new-physics sources. She has placed leading bounds on dark-matter annihilation cross sections using Fermi-LAT, Planck, and HERA data, and studies self-interacting and secluded dark sectors.

troy-van-voorhis-chemistry.s: The Van Voorhis group develops and applies electronic-structure methods—chiefly constrained density functional theory (CDFT) and time-dependent DFT—to study charge and energy transfer in molecular and material systems. Applications include organic photovoltaics, singlet fission, photocatalysis, and light-harvesting complexes, where the group computes diabatic states, electronic couplings, reorganization energies, and barrier heights to bridge quantum chemistry and electron-transfer kinetics. The group also works on embedding methods, excited-state dynamics, and open-system quantum chemistry.

tyler-jacks-biology.s: The Jacks lab uses genetically engineered mouse models carrying oncogenic K-ras, p53, and other mutations to study how genetic events drive tumor initiation, progression, and metastasis in lung, pancreatic, and other cancers. The group combines Cre/lox conditional alleles, CRISPR screens, single-cell RNA sequencing, and immunoprofiling to dissect tumor–immune co-evolution, identify therapeutic vulnerabilities, and test combination immunotherapies. As founding director of the Koch Institute, Jacks has also advanced integrative engineering approaches to cancer biology.

victor-chernozhukov-economics.s: Chernozhukov develops econometric theory and machine-learning methods for causal inference with high-dimensional data. Key contributions include double/debiased machine learning (DML) for treatment effects, quantile regression for heterogeneous impacts, partial identification, and automatic debiased estimation of structural parameters. His work bridges statistics and economics, applying lasso, random forests, and neural nets to policy evaluation, with applications in labor, health, and criminal justice.

vladan-vuletić-physics.s: The Vuletić group performs precision measurements and quantum-information experiments with trapped atoms, ions, and photons. Research includes large-scale entanglement of thousands of atoms via cavity QED, spin squeezing for beyond-standard-quantum-limit atomic clocks using ytterbium-171, quantum networks linking trapped-ion and neutral-atom platforms, and single-photon nonlinear optics in optical resonators. Methods span laser cooling, optical cavities, ion traps, Rydberg atoms, and quantum state tomography.

vladimir-bulović-eecs.l: Organic and Nanostructured Electronics Laboratory (ONE Lab)
vladimir-bulović-eecs.s: ONE Lab develops next-generation optoelectronic devices—LEDs, solar cells, lasers, photodetectors, chemical sensors, and molecular memories—using organic semiconductors, colloidal quantum dots, and perovskite nanocrystals. The group combines vacuum thermal evaporation, solution processing, and nanopatterning to fabricate ultrathin films and nanostructured active layers, and studies carrier dynamics, exciton diffusion, and energy transfer at the molecular and nanoscale. Spin-off companies include QD Vision (quantum-dot displays), Ubiquitous Energy (transparent solar), and Kateeva (printed electronics).

w-craig-carter-dmse.s: Carter applies computational materials science to microstructural evolution, phase transformations, and fracture, with emphasis on interfaces and grain boundaries. Research combines continuum thermodynamics, phase-field modeling (the KWC equation for polycrystals), and finite-element simulations to predict how microstructure controls material properties. Recent work with Yet-Ming Chiang on electrochemistry of battery electrodes led to the semi-solid flow battery technology commercialized by 24M.

wanying-kang-eaps.s: Kang uses geophysical fluid dynamics theory and numerical climate models to study atmospheric and oceanic circulation on Earth, exoplanets, and icy moons. Research topics include the ocean circulation beneath Enceladus's ice shell, atmospheric dynamics of ultra-hot rocky exoplanets and high-obliquity aquaplanets, and how large-scale transport shapes habitability and biosignature detectability. Methods span general-circulation models (GCMs), scaling theories, and idealized dynamical-core experiments.

washington-taylor-physics.s: Taylor is a string theorist and director of MIT's Center for Theoretical Physics. His research addresses the geometry and physics of F-theory compactifications, the landscape of string vacua, and connections to observable particle physics and cosmology. Contributions include work on D-branes, M-theory matrix models, string field theory, and systematic classification of 4D and 6D supergravity theories arising from F-theory, using tools from algebraic geometry, toric geometry, and effective field theory.

whitney-k-newey-economics.s: Newey is an econometrician who developed foundational methods in semiparametric estimation, generalized method of moments (GMM), and nonparametric instrumental variables. His work on efficient semiparametric estimators showed that the asymptotic variance is invariant to whether series or kernel methods estimate the nonparametric component, and his GMM handbook chapter with McFadden is a standard PhD reference. Current research addresses automatic debiasing, machine-learning-based estimation of structural parameters, and regularized instrumental-variable regression.

whitney-s-henry-biology.s: The Henry lab investigates ferroptosis—an iron-dependent, lipid-peroxidation-driven form of regulated cell death—and its roles in cancer therapy resistance, metastatic dormancy, and tumor relapse. Research uses CRISPR screens, lipidomics, metabolomics, and mouse models to dissect how oxidative stress, lipid metabolism, and immune signaling intersect to determine whether cancer cells survive or die. The group also extends ferroptosis mechanisms to liver disease and endometrial physiology.

william-b-frank-eaps.s: The Frank group studies crustal deformation and fault mechanics, focusing on slow earthquakes, tectonic tremor, and low-frequency earthquakes at subduction zones. Research combines seismological analysis of microseismicity catalogs, GPS/InSAR geodesy, and statistical modeling to reconstruct the spatiotemporal evolution of slow slip and to constrain frictional properties of the plate interface. Field targets include the Cascadia, Mexican, and Chilean subduction zones.

william-detmold-physics.s: Detmold uses lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) on supercomputers to calculate the properties of hadrons, nuclei, and nuclear reactions from first principles. Key results include the first QCD calculations of light nuclear binding energies, nucleon–nucleon and hyperon–nucleon scattering, and the gluonic structure of the proton. His work in the NPLQCD collaboration connects lattice data to effective field theories describing nuclear forces, neutron-star equations of state, and double-beta-decay matrix elements.

william-p-minicozzi-math.s: Minicozzi, often in collaboration with Tobias Colding, works on geometric analysis—particularly the structure of minimal surfaces, singularities of mean curvature flow, and Ricci flow. Their structure theory for embedded minimal surfaces in 3-manifolds won the 2010 Veblen Prize, and their proof that generic singularities of mean curvature flow in R³ are spheres or cylinders resolved a long-standing conjecture. Methods include monotonicity formulas, compactness arguments, and PDE analysis on manifolds.

william-t-freeman-eecs.s: The Freeman group works on computer vision, computational photography, and machine learning for visual understanding. Research spans motion magnification (amplifying invisible motion in video), visual vibrometry, image and video generation, appearance-based models, Bayesian inference for low-level vision, and mid-level representations bridging pixels and semantics. The group also developed visual deprojection methods, texture synthesis algorithms, and contributed to foundational work on belief propagation for vision and example-based super-resolution.

wojciech-matusik-eecs.s: The CDFG develops algorithms at the intersection of computer graphics, applied machine learning, and digital fabrication. Projects include topology-optimized multimaterial 3D printing, data-driven appearance and material modeling, differentiable physics simulators for inverse design, and computational tools for textile manufacturing, soft robotics, and optics. The group combines deep learning, PDE-constrained optimization, and geometry processing to automate the design of objects with specified mechanical, optical, or acoustic properties.

xiao-wang-chemistry.s: The Wang lab develops chemistry-based spatial genomics tools to map RNA and DNA in intact tissues at single-molecule resolution. Core technologies include multiplexed in-situ sequencing and combinatorial FISH approaches that image hundreds to thousands of RNA species per cell, enabling high-resolution brain cell-type atlases and spatial maps of gene expression across development, disease, and neural circuits. Methods span probe chemistry, expansion microscopy, high-throughput automated imaging, and computational analysis of spatial transcriptomic data.

xiao-gang-wen-physics.s: Wen is a condensed-matter theorist who introduced the concept of topological order to classify quantum states of matter beyond Landau symmetry breaking. He showed that string-net condensation can give rise to emergent photons and fermions, proposed Z₂ topological order (realized in the toric code), and developed the theory of symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases classified by group cohomology. Methods include tensor-network representations, exactly solvable lattice models, and entanglement-based characterization of quantum phases.

xiaoyi-wang-chemistry.notes: Xiaoyi Wang is a postdoc in the Strano Group at MIT, not chemistry faculty. Likely confused with Xiao Wang (xiao-wang-chemistry, already enriched). Recommend delete.

xuanhe-zhao-meche.s: The Zhao lab develops soft materials and devices for medical robotics, wearable electronics, and bioengineering. Research spans tough adhesive hydrogels, hard-magnetic soft materials that crawl and jump, 3D-printed hydrogel robots, wearable ultrasound patches, and telerobotic systems for endovascular stroke treatment. Methods integrate continuum mechanics (large deformation, instability, fracture, fatigue), microfluidic fabrication, biocompatible adhesive chemistry, and magnetic composite design.

xun-zhao-meche.notes: No MIT MechE/CEE faculty named 'Xun Zhao'. Likely a misnaming of Xuanhe Zhao (xuanhe-zhao-meche). Recommend delete.

y-j-lee-physics.s: Yen-Jie Lee is an experimental nuclear and particle physicist studying the quark–gluon plasma (QGP) produced in ultrarelativistic heavy-ion collisions at the LHC's CMS experiment. Research measures jet quenching, heavy-flavor diffusion, and medium-response effects using high-pT jets, b-quarks, and dimuon probes in PbPb and pPb collisions, and compares collective phenomena in small systems (pp, pPb) with electron–positron baselines. Methods include precision calorimetry, particle-flow reconstruction, and unfolding of jet substructure observables.
y-j-lee-physics.notes: Dup of yen-jie-lee-physics. Same person — Yen-Jie Lee (Physics / LNS / CMS / QGP). Same enrichment applied.

y-chen-biology.s: The Chen lab studies how commensal skin bacteria communicate with and shape the host immune system. Using microbial genetics, gnotobiotic mouse models, single-cell immunology, and engineered skin bacteria, the group investigates how specific commensals educate T cells, induce protective or pathogenic immune responses, and contribute to skin barrier function. A translational arm develops microbial vaccines—skin bacteria engineered to express tumor antigens—to generate anti-tumor immunity.
y-chen-biology.notes: Name in labs.json was 'Y. Chen' — corrected to 'Y. Erin Chen' (Yiyin Erin Chen, Biology/Broad/MGH). Existing k.g (jxwlCUUAAAAJ) was not verified as hers; cleared.

y-kim-eecs.s: Kim works on natural language processing, deep learning for language structure, and efficient AI systems. Research includes neural language models, structured prediction, knowledge distillation, and reasoning with large language models, with contributions to understanding how LLMs represent linguistic structure and to methods for compressing and accelerating inference. He co-directs work at the MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab and is a 2026 Sloan Research Fellow.

yadira-m-soto-feliciano-biology.s: The Soto-Feliciano lab investigates how chromatin regulators and epigenetic factors are targeted to specific genomic loci and how cancer-associated mutations—particularly in MEN1/Menin and Polycomb-group complexes—disrupt chromatin landscapes and gene expression programs. The group uses CRISPR screens, CUT&Tag and CUT&Run profiling, mouse models of neuroendocrine tumors, and biochemical reconstitution to connect chromatin structural changes to oncogenic transcriptional rewiring. Goals include leveraging chromatin vulnerabilities for targeted therapies.

yael-tauman-kalai-eecs.s: Kalai is a cryptographer and complexity theorist who developed breakthrough constructions for verifiable delegation of computation, allowing a weak device to verify that a powerful (potentially malicious) server performed a computation correctly. She co-invented ring signatures with Rivest and Shamir, contributed to succinct non-interactive arguments of knowledge, and works on interactive proofs, program obfuscation, and lattice-based cryptography. She received the 2022 ACM Prize in Computing.

yen-jie-lee-physics.s: Yen-Jie Lee is an experimental nuclear and particle physicist studying the quark–gluon plasma (QGP) produced in ultrarelativistic heavy-ion collisions at the LHC's CMS experiment. Research measures jet quenching, heavy-flavor diffusion, and medium-response effects using high-pT jets, b-quarks, and dimuon probes in PbPb and pPb collisions, and compares collective phenomena in small systems (pp, pPb) with electron–positron baselines. Methods include precision calorimetry, particle-flow reconstruction, and unfolding of jet substructure observables.

yetming-chiang-dmse.s: The Chiang group designs, synthesizes, and characterizes electrochemical materials for energy storage and sustainable manufacturing. Key contributions include the development of lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO₄) battery cathodes, semi-solid flow batteries (commercialized via 24M), iron-air batteries for long-duration grid storage (Form Energy), and electrochemical cement production that avoids CO₂ emissions. Research combines ceramic processing, electrochemistry, phase-field modeling, and in-situ characterization (synchrotron, TEM) to understand dendrite formation and degradation mechanisms in solid-state and liquid-electrolyte cells.

yoel-fink-dmse.s: The fibers@mit group pioneered multimaterial multifunctional fibers that integrate semiconductors, metals, insulators, and polymers into single filaments with nanoscale features drawn from macroscopic preforms. These fibers embed LEDs, photodetectors, piezoelectrics, transistors, and energy-storage elements, enabling fabrics that sense, compute, communicate, and harvest energy. Research spans materials synthesis, thermal drawing, device physics, and machine-learning inference on fiber-based fabrics for wearable health monitoring and soft robotics.

yogesh-surendranath-chemistry.s: The Surendranath lab develops molecular-level understanding and design of electrocatalytic interfaces for energy conversion and environmental chemistry. A central innovation is graphite conjugation—covalently linking molecular catalysts to carbon electrodes through conductive pyrazine linkages—to create tunable, metallic-like surface active sites. The group uses kinetic isotope effects, open-circuit-potential transients, and electrochemical thermodynamics to build mechanistic frameworks for interfacial proton-coupled electron transfer, guiding catalyst design for CO₂ reduction, O₂ reduction, and water splitting.

yufei-zhao-math.s: Zhao is a combinatorialist whose research bridges graph theory, additive combinatorics, and discrete geometry. Topics include extremal graph theory (Turán-type problems, Ramsey theory), Szemerédi's regularity method, sum-product phenomena, sphere packing, and connections to number theory via Fourier analysis and the Green–Tao theorem. He authored the textbook 'Graph Theory and Additive Combinatorics' (Cambridge, 2023) and is a recipient of the SIAM Dénes Kőnig Prize.

yukiko-yamashita-biology.s: The Yamashita lab studies asymmetric stem cell division and germline immortality, using Drosophila male germline stem cells as a model. Research uncovered that ribosomal DNA (rDNA) copy number is actively maintained across generations through asymmetric segregation of sister chromatids during GSC division, revealing functional roles for repetitive 'junk' DNA. The group combines live imaging, CRISPR, single-cell genomics, and cytogenetics to dissect how centromere identity, spindle orientation, and chromatin asymmetry diversify cell fate.

yunha-hwang-biology.s: The Hwang lab combines machine learning and microbiology to study the biochemistry, ecology, and evolution of microorganisms—especially uncultured environmental bacteria with novel biosynthetic potential. Research uses genomic language models, metagenomic mining, and experimental biochemistry to discover and characterize new enzymes, secondary metabolites, and microbial community interactions relevant to sustainable biomanufacturing and environmental health. Hwang co-founded Tatta Bio, a nonprofit advancing genomic AI for biological discovery.

yury-polyanskiy-eecs.s: Polyanskiy works on the non-asymptotic foundations of information theory, channel coding, and statistical learning. His finite-blocklength analysis of channel capacity established tight converse and achievability bounds that supersede classical asymptotic results, with applications to ultra-reliable low-latency communication. Current research extends information-theoretic tools to machine learning, including optimal transport, distribution testing, and high-dimensional statistics. He co-authored the textbook 'Information Theory: From Coding to Learning'.

zach-hartwig-nse.s: The Hartwig group develops high-temperature superconductor (HTS) magnet technology, fusion device engineering, and radiation diagnostics for the path to commercial fusion energy. The group designed, built, and tested the SPARC Toroidal Field Model Coil—the first fusion-scale 20+ tesla REBCO magnet—and continues to advance large-bore, high-field magnets for tokamaks. Research also covers plasma-material interactions, Monte Carlo neutronics, radiation-hard detector development, and nuclear security applications.

zhiwei-yun-math.s: Yun works at the crossroads of algebraic geometry, number theory, and representation theory, with a focus on the geometric Langlands program. Key contributions include the theory of rigid automorphic forms (answering a question of Serre on motives and advancing the inverse Galois problem), and joint work with Wei Zhang giving geometric interpretations of higher derivatives of automorphic L-functions via intersection numbers on moduli spaces of Shtukas. Methods span algebraic stacks, perverse sheaves, and derived algebraic geometry.
